And finally, I find the firm’s long-term thinking interesting — especially the way Jamie Dimon has consistently invested in the wealth franchise as a strategic core, not a side business.”

Variant 3 — Merrill specifically

“Merrill is interesting to me for two reasons. The first is the Bank of America integration — the way the bank deposits, mortgages, and Preferred Rewards tiers feed into the wealth platform creates a different client conversation than what I’ve seen at standalone wirehouses. The second is the training reputation. The ADP program has been turning out advisors for decades and the apprenticeship culture in branches is something I’ve heard about from three different people independently. As a freshman, that combination matters more to me than brand alone.”

Variant 4 — UBS specifically (leveraging your background)

“Honestly, UBS is a structural fit for me in a way few other firms are. The international DNA. The cross-border franchise. The fact that a large share of UBS’s US UHNW client base is first-generation immigrant entrepreneurs — the kind of clientele I’d eventually like to serve.

As an international student from Nepal, multilingual, with a software background, I think I’d learn things at UBS that simply wouldn’t be available at a US-centric wirehouse. And the post-Credit Suisse moment makes it an especially interesting time to be inside the firm — I’d be learning while a real integration is playing out.”

Variant 5 — the boutique RIA version

“What pulls me to a boutique RIA like this one, specifically, is the depth I’d get to see. At a wirehouse I’d be one of many freshmen on a CRM cleanup project. Here, given the size of the team, I’d sit in on real client meetings, see real plans, see how a fee-only fiduciary actually delivers value — not just talks about it.

I’ve been particularly drawn to the planning-first ethos. Reading your team’s page, the emphasis on the integrated plan rather than the product pitch is something that resonates with how I want to learn the business. And being able to learn that closely under a senior advisor, rather than as one of 50 in an analyst class, is a real trade-off I’m making in your favor.”

Traps

Don’t name three generic things any firm could claim (“prestige, training, exposure”). Anchor in something specific that only this firm or this team has. Mentioning a name (an advisor, an executive), an acquisition, a product, or a recent firm initiative is the move that signals real preparation.

What would you bring to our team as a freshman?
Are you self-aware enough to know what you can and can’t offer at this stage? Can you sell yourself without being arrogant?
Variant 1 — the “leverage, not expertise” version (most disarming)

“Honestly, I won’t pretend I’m bringing technical expertise as a freshman. I’m here to learn that.

What I can bring is leverage. I write quickly, I’m comfortable with data and CRMs, I can pick up the platforms fast given my software background. I speak three languages and I’m learning a fourth, which might be useful for some of the cross-border conversations your team has.

And honestly — I’m willing to do the boring work. If something is dragging on the team — a backlog of outreach, a half-built newsletter, an outdated prospect list, a CRM that needs cleaning — I’d like to be the person you can hand it to and not worry about. That kind of intern is honestly more valuable than someone who shows up with a finance certificate they bought online.”

Variant 2 — the “here’s what I’d actually do day one” version

“Honestly, the most useful thing I can tell you is what I’d try to do in my first month.

Week one, I’d be a sponge. Sit with the CSA, sit with the IA, learn the systems, watch how the team operates. Take a lot of notes.

Week two and three, I’d pick the most obviously broken operational thing and just quietly fix it. Could be cleaning up the CRM, building a better prospect list, refreshing a deck template. Something small but visible.

By week four, I’d propose one slightly bigger project I could own — something that would actually save the team time over the rest of the summer.

What I’m bringing isn’t expertise yet. It’s the willingness to be useful in small ways and learn fast.”

Variant 3 — the “edge” version (leans into background)

“A few things, honestly. The first is the software piece. I’ve had pull requests merged into real open-source projects, so I’m comfortable around code, data, and the kind of automation that often saves advisor teams hours per week.

The second is the multilingual side. I speak Hindi, Nepali, and English fluently, and I’m studying Spanish. If your team has — or wants — clients in the South Asian or Latino communities, I can be useful in ways most freshmen can’t.

The third is the work ethic. I’m from a place where opportunities like this don’t come around easily, so I show up early, deliver on time, and don’t complain. That sounds simple but it’s honestly rarer than people think.

And on top of all that, I’d be genuinely curious about the business. I’d ask thoughtful questions and try to get better every week.”

Variant 4 — the “honest about gaps” version

“Let me be honest about what I don’t bring first — I don’t have a finance degree yet, I’ve never sat in a real client meeting, and I haven’t passed Series 7 or any other license. So I’m not coming in as a producer.

What I do bring is — a willingness to be useful in small, reliable ways. Comfort with technology and data. A multilingual background. And the kind of work ethic that comes from being an international student trying to break into this industry on the unusual path.

I’d rather be the intern your team can count on to finish things, rather than the one who’s trying to impress you with technical answers I haven’t actually earned yet.”

Variant 5 — the “quiet competence” version

“Honestly, three things.

One, reliability. If you say you need something by Wednesday, it’s done by Tuesday. Every time. No drama.

Two, the ability to write and communicate. A lot of the work your team does — outreach, follow-ups, internal notes — needs to be clean and professional. I’m comfortable doing that quickly.

Three, discretion. I’ll hear sensitive things about clients. I won’t talk about them. Not to friends, not to family, not online. That’s table stakes for the job, but it’s also a real differentiator at the freshman level — some people don’t take it seriously enough.”

Why each variant works

All five lead with humility (“I’m not pretending to have technical expertise”) and then pivot to concrete value (“here’s what I can actually do”). The trap to avoid is overselling: saying you’ll “contribute to portfolio strategy” or “help win clients” as a freshman intern is laughable and triggers immediate skepticism. Be honest about what you bring, and be specific.

How do you build a financial plan for a 35-year-old earning $200K?
Can you think holistically about a person’s financial life, or do you immediately jump to portfolio percentages?
Variant 1 — the “understand the human first” version

“Honestly, the first thing I’d do is just ask questions. Before I’d build a plan, I’d want to understand the human.

Things I’d ask: is he married, kids, any planned? What does his career look like — stable, growing, scary? Does he have any debts? What scares him about money? What does his life look like in ten years? Does his employer offer a 401(k) match, an HSA, a deferred comp plan? Where does he live, and is buying a house in the plan?

Once I had that context, I’d build the plan in layers.

Foundation first: emergency fund covering 3 to 6 months of expenses, adequate term life insurance if he has dependents, disability insurance because he’s in his peak earning years.

Tax-advantaged accumulation: max the 401(k) at least to the employer match. Then a Roth IRA if eligible, or a backdoor Roth if he’s over the income limit (which he probably is at $200K). Then fund an HSA if his employer offers a high-deductible health plan.

Taxable brokerage for anything beyond. Given his 30-year horizon, I’d lean equity-heavy — maybe 85% equities, 15% bonds — with global diversification.

On top of all of that, I’d schedule a real check-in in twelve months. Life will change — raises, kids, a house. The plan has to live, not just sit in a binder.”

Variant 2 — the structured layer-cake version

“I’d build it in four layers.

Layer one, foundation. Emergency fund of three to six months expenses. Term life insurance if he has dependents. Disability insurance.

Layer two, tax-advantaged growth. Max the 401(k) to capture the full employer match at minimum, then up to the annual contribution limit if cash flow allows. Fund a Roth IRA via backdoor if his income is above the direct contribution limit, which at $200K it likely is. Fund an HSA if eligible, and treat it as a stealth retirement account — don’t spend it on current medical bills.

Layer three, taxable accumulation. After tax-advantaged accounts are maxed, invest excess savings in a taxable brokerage account. Use ETFs or direct indexing for tax efficiency. Set up automatic tax-loss harvesting.

Layer four, asset allocation. With a 30-year horizon and stable income, I’d default to something like 80 to 90% equities, with global diversification — US large-cap, US small-cap, developed international, emerging markets. The rest in bonds and a little cash buffer.

Then revisit annually. Life changes, the plan changes.”

Variant 3 — the “what would I tell my friend” version (most human)

“Okay, so this is honestly the kind of conversation I’d have with a friend. So let me answer it that way.

If a 35-year-old friend making $200K asked me what to do, I’d say: first, make sure you have a few months of expenses in a high-yield savings account in case something goes wrong. Next, get term life insurance if you have anyone depending on you, and disability insurance because your earning power is your biggest asset.

Then I’d say: 401(k) at least to the match, no exceptions, that’s free money. After that, do a backdoor Roth IRA every year — the tax-free growth over thirty years is incredible. And if your employer has an HSA, fund it and don’t spend it — it’s the most tax-advantaged account in the code.

For investing, given how long you have, lean heavily into stocks — like 85% — spread across the world, with a small bond piece for stability. Don’t buy individual stocks; buy broad index ETFs.

And finally, talk to me again in a year. Life is gonna change — kids, a house, a raise. The plan has to evolve.”

Variant 4 — the “dollar math” version (good for analytical interviewers)

“Let’s walk through the numbers. $200K of income at his age, after federal and state taxes plus FICA, is probably around $135K take-home.

Of that take-home, target maybe 30% to savings — so around $40K a year. Here’s the savings cascade:

Step one, capture the 401(k) match. If the company matches 5% and he’s contributing 5%, that’s $10K of his money plus $10K of employer money going into the 401(k) annually. Up to the IRS limit of $23K, his contribution can go higher with no match on the marginal dollar.

Step two, backdoor Roth IRA. $7K a year. Tax-free for the rest of his life.

Step three, HSA if eligible. $4K a year if individual, $8K if family.

Step four, anything left over goes to a taxable brokerage account. With direct indexing or low-cost ETFs.

For asset allocation, his 30-year horizon supports 85% equities globally diversified. Bonds for psychological cushioning, not for return.

Net result: he’s saving $40K+ a year tax-efficiently, with most of it in long-term growth assets. If he keeps that up and earns reasonable returns, he’s in great shape by his 50s.”

Variant 5 — the “what most advisors get wrong” version

“The standard answer is foundation plus tax-advantaged accumulation plus asset allocation, which is correct. But what I’d emphasize is what most advisors actually get wrong with a 35-year-old.

Number one, they default to too conservative an allocation. A 35-year-old has thirty years before retirement. They can afford to be 85 to 90% equities. Many advisors put them at 65 or 70% because it’s the safe default. That conservative drift quietly costs hundreds of thousands of dollars of long-term wealth.

Number two, they neglect tax efficiency. A taxable account run through index ETFs or direct indexing can be vastly more efficient than the same dollars in an actively managed mutual fund. The tax drag on a high-turnover mutual fund is real.

Number three, they ignore behavioral coaching. A 35-year-old hasn’t lived through a real bear market yet. The first one is going to be hard. The advisor’s job is to prepare him for it now, not after he’s already panic-selling.

So my plan would address all three: aggressive but appropriate allocation, tax-efficient implementation, and a real conversation about what to expect when markets drop.”

What is risk tolerance, and how do you measure it?
Do you understand that risk tolerance isn’t a single number on a questionnaire?
Variant 1 — the “three pieces” version

“The way I’d describe it is — risk tolerance is really three different things combined.

One, ability to take risk — the financial capacity to absorb losses without breaking the plan. A retiree with $10M and $300K of annual spending has high ability. A 35-year-old with $50K saved and high fixed expenses has low ability.

Two, willingness to take risk — the emotional comfort with seeing the portfolio drop. This is what most questionnaires actually measure, even though it’s only one piece.

Three, need to take risk — whether the client’s goals require taking risk to reach them. A retiree who’s already very wealthy and just needs to fund their lifestyle has low need. A young saver trying to build retirement wealth has high need.

The trick as an advisor is that all three have to align. High ability and willingness but low need? Don’t over-risk just because you can. High need but low willingness? You have to either coach them up or moderate the goal. The questionnaire alone catches almost none of this.”

Variant 2 — the “what would you actually do” version

“Honestly, I think the standard risk-tolerance questionnaire is mostly bad. People say they’re comfortable with a 30% drawdown when their portfolio is up 20%. Then 2008 happens and they sell at the bottom.

What I think actually works is the lived-experience question — ‘in 2008, or in March 2020, what did you actually do? Did you stay invested? Did you sell? Did you add?’ The behavior is much more honest than the survey.

And the bigger frame is that risk tolerance isn’t a single number. It’s a combination of three things — financial capacity to lose, emotional comfort with volatility, and how much risk the plan actually needs. All three have to be considered together. A good advisor asks all three and listens to what’s under each answer.”

Variant 3 — the “capacity vs willingness” version

“The textbook answer is that risk tolerance is the client’s ability to absorb losses without it disrupting their plan, combined with their emotional comfort with volatility, combined with whether the goals actually require it.

The interesting part is that those three things are often in conflict. A young saver might have huge capacity for risk — long horizon, no immediate spending needs — but very low willingness because they’ve never lived through a real drawdown. A retiree might have low capacity but very high willingness because they lived through 2008 and learned to hold.

An advisor’s job is to navigate the gap. Sometimes you coach the client’s willingness up so they can take the risk their capacity supports. Sometimes you talk them down so they don’t take more risk than their capacity can handle. Either way, ‘risk tolerance’ isn’t one number — it’s a conversation.”

Variant 4 — the “short and confident” version

“Three pieces. Ability — can the client financially absorb losses. Willingness — can they emotionally handle volatility. Need — do their goals actually require taking risk. The questionnaire mostly measures willingness, which is why a real advisor tests all three through actual conversation, not a five-question survey.”

Variant 5 — the “the question I’d actually ask” version

“The framework is ability plus willingness plus need. But the practical question I think actually works is — ‘tell me about the worst financial moment of your life. What did you do?’

That single question gets at willingness more honestly than any survey. If they say ‘I sold and moved to cash and felt terrible for years,’ you’ve learned something. If they say ‘I kept investing my paycheck right through it,’ you’ve learned something different. And the answer reshapes the portfolio in ways the survey would never catch.

Of course you still have to do the ability piece — the math — and the need piece — the goals. But that one conversation usually tells you more than the questionnaire ever will.”

What is an ETF, and why has it taken over?
Do you understand the modern asset-management landscape, or are you stuck in 1995?
Variant 1 — the “three reasons” version

“An ETF is an exchange-traded fund. It’s a pooled investment vehicle — basically a basket of securities — that trades on an exchange like a stock, all day long.

Three reasons they’ve taken over from mutual funds. One, they’re cheap — expense ratios on big index ETFs are under 0.05%, compared to 0.5 to 1.5% for many mutual funds. Two, they’re tax-efficient because of the in-kind creation/redemption mechanism, which lets the fund manage its tax lots without distributing capital gains to all shareholders. Three, they trade intraday, which gives clients flexibility that mutual funds — priced once a day at NAV — just can’t match.

The big shift is that for most asset classes — US large-cap, total market, total bond — ETFs are basically the default vehicle now. The next frontier is active ETFs and direct indexing, which are reshaping the wrapper conversation again.”

Variant 2 — the “mutual fund vs ETF” comparison

“An ETF is basically a mutual fund that trades on an exchange. Same idea — pooled investment vehicle, professionally managed or index-tracking — but with three structural advantages over a traditional mutual fund.

First, they’re tax-efficient. When a mutual fund manager sells appreciated positions, the gains get distributed pro rata to all shareholders, who owe tax. ETFs avoid this through in-kind redemptions — the fund hands out actual securities to redeeming investors instead of cash, which doesn’t trigger a taxable event for the remaining shareholders.

Second, they’re cheap. Big index ETFs run 3 to 10 basis points. Comparable mutual funds often run 50 to 100 basis points or more.

Third, they’re flexible. You can buy or sell an ETF at any point during trading hours, see real-time prices, use limit orders, set stop losses. Mutual funds only price once a day at the close.

For most clients, especially in taxable accounts, ETFs win on all three. That’s why they’ve grown so much.”

Variant 3 — the “in-kind redemption mechanism” (for technical interviewers)

“ETFs are pooled investment vehicles that trade on an exchange like stocks. The thing that makes them genuinely different from mutual funds isn’t the trading flexibility — it’s the in-kind creation/redemption mechanism.

Here’s how it works. Large institutions called Authorized Participants — usually market makers — can swap baskets of underlying securities for ETF shares, or vice versa, with the fund directly. This keeps the ETF’s market price close to its NAV.

The tax magic happens during redemption. When an AP redeems ETF shares, the fund hands them a basket of the underlying securities — not cash. The fund can choose which specific lots to hand out, usually the lowest-basis ones, which moves the embedded gain off the fund’s books and onto the AP’s. The remaining shareholders never see a capital-gain distribution.

This structural difference is the main reason ETFs have outgrown mutual funds in the US over the past decade, and it’s why you’ll hear the phrase ‘ETF tax efficiency’ constantly in WM conversations.”

Variant 4 — the simple version

“An ETF is a fund that trades on an exchange like a stock. Most track an index, like the S&P 500. The advantages over a mutual fund are that they’re cheaper, more tax-efficient because of how they manage their underlying holdings, and tradeable throughout the day.

They’ve grown to dominate because once you have the same diversification at lower cost and lower tax drag, there’s not much reason to keep using mutual funds for most asset classes. Especially in taxable accounts.”

Variant 5 — the “but ETFs aren’t always better” nuance

“The basic story is — ETFs are pooled vehicles trading on an exchange, with low fees and tax efficiency from in-kind redemptions. They’ve taken over for most index-tracking use cases.

The nuance I’d add is that ETFs aren’t always strictly better. In a 401(k), you don’t need tax efficiency, so a cheap mutual fund works just as well. For an asset class where the manager genuinely adds value — some segments of small-cap, international, or distressed debt — active mutual funds can outperform passive ETFs after fees.

And the rise of active ETFs is starting to blur the line completely. So I’d say the right framing is — ETFs have taken over for passive, cost-conscious, tax-aware investing in efficient asset classes. Active mutual funds are still useful in specific niches. And the wrapper conversation is actively evolving with active ETFs and direct indexing.”

If you had $100K to invest for a 65-year-old, how would you allocate it?
Hidden test — the right first move is to ask questions, not to spit a portfolio. Strong candidates make that explicit.
Variant 1 — the ‘ask first’ version (most correct)

“Honestly, before I’d allocate anything, I’d need to ask a lot of questions. Does she still work? Does she need income from the portfolio? What are her annual expenses? Does she have a pension or Social Security? Is $100K her only investable money or is it a small piece? What’s her tax bracket? What’s her risk tolerance? What’s her health and longevity expectation?

Each of those changes the answer. A $100K piece for a wealthy client with $5M in other accounts is a completely different allocation than $100K as a retiree’s only investable wealth.

If I really had to give a starting-point allocation in isolation, for someone newly retired with normal longevity expectations and $100K as a meaningful piece of her wealth, I’d sketch something like 50% diversified equities, 40% fixed income, 10% cash. Equities for inflation protection over a 25-year retirement. Bonds for income and stability. Cash as a buffer so she never has to sell equities into a down market. But that’s a sketch, not a recommendation — the real allocation depends on the questions I’d ask first.”

Variant 2 — the bucketing-by-time-horizon version

“The way I’d think about this is by time horizon, since she’s 65 and her retirement could last 25 to 30 years. So the $100K isn’t one allocation — it’s really three buckets.

Bucket one, the next 2 to 3 years: cash and short-term Treasuries. Maybe $10K to $15K. This is the ‘won’t have to sell into a down market’ buffer.

Bucket two, the next 3 to 10 years: high-quality intermediate-term bonds, maybe a small allocation to dividend equities. Maybe $30K to $40K. Generating income, less volatile.

Bucket three, the rest: diversified global equities, maybe a small alternatives sleeve if she’s qualified. Maybe $45K to $55K. This is the long-horizon money that has to grow to last.

Same portfolio, but the bucketing makes the conversation about risk much more concrete for her — she can see why we’re holding cash, why we’re holding equity, why neither piece is wrong.”

Variant 3 — the income-first version

“If she needs income from the $100K, I&rsquo>d orient the allocation around income first, growth second. Specifically:

About 40 to 50% in a fixed-income sleeve generating reasonable yield — investment-grade corporates, municipal bonds if she’s in a high tax bracket, possibly a small TIPS allocation for inflation protection.

About 35 to 45% in equities tilted toward quality dividend payers — large-cap companies with stable cash flows and rising dividends. Not pure income-stocks, but more dividend-aware than a growth-tilted portfolio.

About 10 to 15% in cash. Especially important for retirees because of the ‘sequence-of-returns risk’ — if she has to draw from a down portfolio in her first few years of retirement, she can’t recover. The cash sleeve protects against that.

We’d revisit annually as her circumstances and the rate environment change.”

Variant 4 — the simple-but-honest version

“Honestly, without knowing more, the textbook answer is something like 50% diversified equities, 40% fixed income, 10% cash. The reasoning is: she has a 20- to 30-year retirement ahead, equities for inflation protection, bonds for income and stability, cash for short-term needs.

But the real answer is — I’d want to ask about her other assets, her income needs, her health, her risk tolerance. Without those, anything I say is just a starting point.”

Variant 5 — the ‘what most advisors get wrong’ version

“The standard answer is 50/40/10, and that’s a fine starting point. But the thing most advisors get wrong with newly-retired clients is they go too conservative too fast.

A 65-year-old has a 20 to 30 year retirement ahead. The single biggest risk isn’t a stock-market crash — it’s running out of money before she runs out of years. To beat that, the portfolio has to keep growing for the next 30 years, which means meaningful equity exposure.

So while I’d start at something like 50/40/10, I’d probably push back if she or another advisor suggested going to 30% equities or below. Inflation alone destroys purchasing power over a long retirement. The safer-feeling portfolio is often the riskier one in real terms.”

Tell me about a current macro theme you’re watching.
Can you hold a substantive macro view with a portfolio implication and an honest uncertainty?
Variant 1 — structural real rates

“The theme I keep coming back to is the structural rise in real interest rates. After more than a decade where the risk-free rate was effectively zero, real yields are back in positive territory. That changes the cost of capital for everything — mortgages, growth-stock multiples, private-credit returns, even how much leverage HNW clients can productively carry.

For a wealth practice, the conversation is just different than it was in the 2010s. Cash is a real asset class again. Fixed income is genuinely attractive for the first time in fifteen years. Equity valuations have to be reasoned about with more discipline.

What I’m honestly less sure about is whether this is a permanent regime shift or a cyclical move that’ll reverse. The 2010s narrative was ‘rates are zero forever,’ and we know how wrong that turned out. So I try to hold the new regime lightly — act on it, but stay open to it being temporary.”

Variant 2 — the AI capex story

“The macro story I find most interesting right now is the AI capex cycle. The hyperscalers are collectively spending hundreds of billions on data centers, chips, and infrastructure. The scale of it is genuinely historical — comparable to the telecom buildout of the late 1990s.

The second-order effects are what I’m really watching. The capex is lifting power demand, which is reshaping utility valuations. It’s pulling forward semis demand. It’s creating massive concentration risk in the S&P, where AI names drive a huge share of index moves. It’s also creating commercial real estate demand for data centers.

The honest uncertainty: whether the returns eventually justify the capex. The 90s telecom buildout enabled the internet but vaporized a lot of capital along the way. Whether this cycle is different is one of the most contested questions in markets.”

Variant 3 — the demographic / labor-supply theme

“Something I’ve been thinking about lately is the labor-supply piece of US inflation. We’re in an unusual situation where boomers are retiring, immigration policy is uncertain, and the prime-age labor force has tighter participation than usual.

That dynamic puts structural pressure on wages, especially in services. Even as goods inflation has cooled, services and wages have stayed sticky. And the Fed’s 2% target gets harder when there’s a structural floor under labor costs.

What this means for portfolios is that the deflationary regime of 2010–2020 may not return on demand — we may live with structurally higher inflation for a while. I’d be cautious about pricing in a return to zero real rates.”

Variant 4 — the geopolitical-fragmentation theme

“The macro theme I find most important but hardest to trade is geopolitical fragmentation. The world is moving from the integrated globalization of 1990–2015 toward something more fragmented — US-China decoupling, supply-chain reshoring, sanctions, capital controls in some emerging markets.

For portfolios, this matters because the long-run advantages of global diversification depend on the world staying integrated. If we’re moving toward a more multipolar world, currency hedging matters more, EM exposure has to be more selective, and even global equity correlations might behave differently than the historical pattern.

I don’t have a strong forecast on how this resolves. But it’s the theme I think most underestimated by the average advisor portfolio.”

Variant 5 — the ‘everything I’m watching’ honest version

“Honestly, there are three things I’m watching and I’d be lying if I said I had high confidence about any of them. The Fed’s policy path versus what markets are pricing — the dot plot and the futures market are still diverging. Services inflation stickiness, especially in shelter and labor-intensive categories. And the impact of the AI capex cycle on the rest of the economy.

What I think matters less is having a confident forecast on any one of those. What matters more is being honest with clients about the uncertainty and tilting portfolios in a way that’s robust to several scenarios rather than betting hard on one.”

If a client’s daughter is getting married, what should the advisor be doing?
Are you thinking like an advisor about life events — not just ‘congratulations’ but the cascading planning implications?
Variant 1 — the ‘three things’ structured version

“Three things, beyond saying congratulations.

One: review the parents’ estate plan. A major family event like a wedding is a natural prompt to revisit beneficiaries on retirement accounts and life insurance, gifting strategy, and any trusts. Often things need updating that nobody’s thought about in years.

Two: think about a soft introduction to the daughter and her spouse. They’re forming a new financial unit. The firm has a real opportunity to grow the relationship into the next generation. Not a pitch — just a relationship-building conversation.

Three: if there’s a meaningful wedding gift, the advisor should be ready to discuss titling, gift-tax implications, the annual exclusion, and how to deploy the gift productively. A 529 contribution for future kids, a Roth IRA contribution if the daughter has earned income, a taxable brokerage account — the right answer depends on the family.

The best advisors treat every life event as a relationship and planning moment, never just a social one.”

Variant 2 — the ‘next-generation’ framing

“Honestly, the way I’d frame this is — a wedding is one of the natural moments to start a next-generation relationship.

The statistic that gets thrown around is that 80%+ of inheritors change advisors when wealth passes to them. That’s an enormous retention risk. The way to manage it is to start building relationships with the next generation while the parents are still clients, not after.

So the moves I’d expect a thoughtful advisor to make: send a warm personal note from the team. Offer to meet the daughter and her new husband for a no-pressure introduction. Maybe contribute a thoughtful book or resource on early-marriage financial planning — something genuinely useful, not branded.

And on the planning side, revisit the parents’ estate plan and gifting strategy. A wedding is exactly the kind of trigger that should prompt that review.”

Variant 3 — the human-first version

“Before I’d think about the planning, I’d think about the human moment. A wedding is one of the most emotionally loaded events in a family. Some clients are thrilled. Some have mixed feelings — the spouse isn’t who they’d have picked, money tensions are running through the family, the parents are worried about the daughter financially.

So the first move from the advisor is a personal note. Not corporate. Not from the firm. From the advisor as a human. Asking how the parents are doing — not just ‘congrats.’ That call usually surfaces what’s actually going on emotionally, which then shapes the planning conversation that follows.

Once you have the emotional read, then yes — estate plan review, next-gen introduction, gift-tax discussion. But the human moment comes first, or the planning lands wrong.”

Variant 4 — the short version

“Personal warm note from the advisor. Estate plan and beneficiary review for the parents. Soft introduction to the daughter and new spouse for next-gen relationship building. Discussion of gift-tax exclusion and the most productive use of any meaningful wedding gift. The advisor treats it as both a human moment and a planning moment.”

What questions do you have for me?
This is the closer. Most candidates throw it away. The candidates who use it well are the ones the interviewer remembers an hour later.
The principle

The best questions make the interviewer talk about themselves, their pain points, their vision, or their hard-won opinions. People remember interns who made them feel interesting.

Category 1 — questions about their practice
  • “What does the team’s ideal client look like, and how has that profile changed over the last five years?”
  • “Where in the practice do you feel under-leveraged — the place where an extra pair of hands would actually move the needle?”
  • “What’s the part of your day right now that you most wish you had help with?”
  • “Is there a specific kind of client conversation that’s been showing up more often in the last year? What’s driving it?”
  • “What separates the clients who stay with this team for 20 years from the ones who eventually leave?”
Category 2 — questions about the interviewer’s own story
  • “What changed for you the year you went from ‘junior advisor’ to ‘real advisor’? Was there a specific moment?”
  • “What surprised you most about this business when you got into it?”
  • “If you were 18 again and starting in WM, what would you do that I’m probably not doing yet?”
  • “Of the practices you watched fail or stagnate over the years, what was the most common reason?”
  • “What’s the question a prospect asks you that you wish you could answer better?”
Category 3 — questions about the firm
  • “If you could change one thing about the firm’s platform tomorrow, what would it be?”
  • “The integration of E*TRADE and Eaton Vance has been a major theme — how is that actually showing up in your work day to day?”
  • “The GIC’s recent commentary has been pretty cautious on rates. Is that view reflected in client conversations, or are clients more upbeat than the house view?”
  • “Where do you think the firm is most under-appreciated by competitors? Most over-rated?”
Category 4 — questions about the role and trajectory
  • “What does the strongest version of an intern on your team look like at the end of summer?”
  • “What’s the highest-leverage thing an intern could do in their first month here?”
  • “If I had a free week to spend just learning, what would you want me to read or shadow first?”
  • “How do you think about the development arc from intern to junior advisor — is there a specific path you watch interns succeed on?”
Category 5 — the ‘hard, specific’ questions that signal seriousness
  • “The blended advisory fee has been compressing across the industry for years. How are you and the firm thinking about that pressure long-term?”
  • “The next generation of clients seems to want a different kind of relationship than the boomer clients did. Are you seeing that on your team, and how are you adapting?”
  • “The fiduciary vs. suitability debate has shifted a lot in the last decade. Where do you think we are now, and where is the industry heading?”
  • “Private credit has exploded as a category. How are you thinking about it for HNW clients given the lockup terms?”
  • “Direct indexing is growing fast. Is that something your team is implementing yet, or are you waiting for it to mature further?”
How to actually pick

Have 3 to 5 of these ready before any conversation. Don’t use them all — just have them on hand. Pick the 2 or 3 that feel right based on (a) what you genuinely want to know, (b) what fits the interviewer’s energy, and (c) what hasn’t already been covered. The hardest one of the bunch — usually a Category 5 question — should be your last one because it leaves the interviewer with a strong final impression of how serious you are.

When AUM, fee-based, fiduciary, behavioral coaching, and the structure of an advisor team all feel obvious, you’re ready.
Chapter 03 — The Playbook

How freshmen actually get Morgan Stanley and J.P. Morgan wealth-management internships.

Almost everything you’ve read online about “breaking in” assumes you’re a junior, at a target school, applying through Workday. None of that is your situation. This chapter is the real playbook for freshmen, written for the human path: local branches, personal outreach, walking in, and earning a seat one human being at a time.

The first thing to understand: there are two hiring systems

At every major wealth-management firm, there are essentially two parallel hiring systems:

  1. The centralized program: HQ-run internships, applications through the firm’s portal, mostly aimed at juniors, recruiting starts a year in advance, heavily target-school filtered. Examples: Morgan Stanley’s Wealth Management Summer Analyst Program, J.P. Morgan’s Wealth Management Summer Analyst Program. This system is real, it is competitive, and you should apply, but it is not your most reliable path as a freshman.
  2. The local branch / advisor team hiring: individual Financial Advisor teams at local branches hiring interns directly, with their own money, on their own timeline. This is informal, relationship-driven, year-round, and largely off the radar. This is your path.
Why local hiring is your path

A Morgan Stanley Wealth Management branch in a metro area might have 30 advisor teams. Many of them have at any moment an unmet need for a sharp, motivated, available undergrad — sometimes paid, sometimes for-credit, sometimes as a junior “business development associate.” They hire when the right person walks through their door, or sends the right email. They almost never advertise. The vast majority of freshmen never realize this market exists.

The realistic timeline for a freshman

You are entering Soka in the fall. Here is the realistic, month-by-month rhythm you should be running.

WindowWhat you should be doing
Summer before collegeRead this site front to back. Build a clean resume and LinkedIn. Start identifying advisors near your hometown and near Soka. Begin sending introductory emails.
Fall freshman (Aug–Dec)15–20 informational coffee chats. Apply to centralized freshman/sophomore diversity programs (MS Early Insights, JPM Winning Women, Goldman Possibilities Summit, etc., when applicable). Build relationships with 3–5 advisors who feel real.
Winter freshman (Dec–Feb)Convert one of those relationships into an unpaid or paid winter-break shadow / part-time gig. Tell people about it. Use it as leverage. Keep networking.
Spring freshman (Feb–May)Apply formally to local branch internships for the summer. Re-engage every advisor you’ve spoken to. Schedule final coffee chats with branch managers. Decide your top 2–3 targets.
Summer freshman (Jun–Aug)Do the internship. Be excellent. Generate testimonials. Quietly start planning your sophomore-year recruiting now.

Step 1 — Build your “outreach asset stack”

Before you talk to anyone, you need a tight stack:

  • Resume — one page, clean, no creativity. Education, experience, leadership, skills. Quantify everything. If a CSA glances at it for 8 seconds, can they place you?
  • LinkedIn — professional headshot, location set to your campus, headline that’s a sentence rather than a job title, About section that reads like a human. Connections gradually built up with classmates, alumni, professors, advisors.
  • Email signature — name, school, phone, LinkedIn URL. Simple. No graphics.
  • Email account — use your school email, not a Gmail handle that looks 14 years old.
  • 30-second story — who you are, why finance, why WM, why now. Memorized but not robotic.

Step 2 — Build the “target list”

Open a spreadsheet. Columns: First Name | Last Name | Firm | Branch | City | Title | LinkedIn URL | Email guess | Phone | Source | Status | Last contact | Notes.

Populate it with advisors from:

  • Morgan Stanley WM branches within a 30-mile radius of (a) your home city and (b) Aliso Viejo / Orange County (Soka).
  • J.P. Morgan Wealth Management offices in the same areas.
  • Merrill Lynch (Bank of America) offices.
  • UBS, RBC Wealth, Raymond James.
  • 2–3 independent fee-only RIAs in each area (Google “[city] fee-only fiduciary”).
  • Soka alumni anywhere in financial services.

Target a list of at least 80 advisors. You will only end up speaking to 15–25, but the funnel matters.

How to find them

  • Each big firm’s “Find an Advisor” tool by zip code (morganstanley.com’s Find an FA, ml.com’s advisor locator, etc.).
  • LinkedIn Sales Navigator if you can finagle a trial, otherwise basic LinkedIn search for “Financial Advisor at Morgan Stanley” near a city.
  • The firm’s “Barron’s Top 1200” or “Forbes Best-in-State” advisor lists (publicly available; high-quality target list).
  • Local business journals’ “40 under 40” lists — you’ll find younger, more approachable advisors there.

How to guess emails

Most firms use predictable patterns. firstname.lastname@morganstanley.com, firstname.lastname@jpmorgan.com, first.last@ml.com (Merrill), first.last@ubs.com, first.last@rbc.com, etc. Tools like Hunter.io can confirm, but you usually don’t need them — the patterns are stable.

Step 3 — The cold email that actually works

Most cold emails to advisors are bad in the same five ways:

  • They’re too long.
  • They make the email about the student, not the advisor.
  • They ask for an internship in the first message.
  • They sound copy-pasted.
  • They have no clear, low-friction ask.

Here’s the template I’d build for you. Don’t copy this verbatim — rewrite it in your own voice. The structure is the point.

Cold email template — first contact

Subject: Soka freshman — quick question about your work at Morgan Stanley

Dear Ms. Lee,

I’m a freshman at Soka University of America, originally from Nepal, with a background in math and software. I came across your team while reading the Barron’s Top 1200 list and was struck by the emphasis your practice places on multi-generational planning for first-generation business-owner families — it’s a model I’ve been trying to understand more deeply.

I’m not looking for a job. I’m trying to learn the wealth-management business from people actually doing it, and I’d be grateful for fifteen minutes on the phone whenever the next month allows. I’m happy to send a few specific questions in advance so the time is useful, and I’m flexible around your calendar.

Thank you for considering it.

Warmly,
Apex Poudel
Soka University of America · Class of 2029
[phone] · [LinkedIn URL]

Why this works:

  • Specific opener: you reference something real about her practice. She knows you didn’t mail-merge.
  • Low-friction ask: fifteen minutes, on the phone, no commitment.
  • Offer of preparation: questions in advance — this is unusual and reads as serious.
  • Quiet credibility: Soka, Nepal, math/software — in three phrases you signal “curious and not from the usual mold.” That’s a feature.
  • No internship ask: you’re not begging. You’re building.

Variants by firm

Variant for J.P. Morgan Wealth Management / Chase Private Client

Reference Chase Private Client’s integration with the bank, or J.P. Morgan’s research and lending platform. If the advisor came from a private-bank background, ask about it.

Sample opener: “I came across your team while reading J.P. Morgan’s wealth-management commentary on the integrated banking model — the way your team appears to combine deposit relationships with portfolio advice is something I’ve been trying to understand more deeply…”

Variant for a Merrill Lynch advisor

Reference the Bank of America integration, the Goldman-comparable training program in some segments, or Merrill’s long history serving multi-generational families.

Sample opener: “Your practice was profiled in a Barron’s feature on multi-generational planning, and the way you talked about preparing the next generation before they inherit was the most thoughtful framing I’ve read on it…”

Variant for a UBS advisor

Reference UBS’s global private-bank franchise, the cross-border capabilities for international families, the alternatives platform, or the post-Credit Suisse integration.

Sample opener: “As an international student, I’ve been particularly interested in how UBS serves cross-border families — the platform’s global reach is unusual in this country, and I’d love to understand how that shapes the conversations you have with clients who hold assets in multiple jurisdictions…”

Variant for a Raymond James / RBC office

Reference the partnership culture and the larger autonomy advisors at those firms have. Both firms famously attract advisors who want a less corporate environment.

Sample opener: “I’ve been struck by the cultural reputation Raymond James has among advisors who’ve moved from the wirehouses — the autonomy and the partnership model seem to attract a particular kind of practice, and I’d love to understand how that’s shaped yours…”

Variant for a local fee-only RIA

Reference their fiduciary-only model, their specialization (e.g. women in transition, tech executives), and that you’re curious how their advice process differs from the wirehouse model.

Sample opener: “Your practice page describes a planning-first approach that’s very different from the wirehouse model I’d been reading about — the choice to be fee-only and fiduciary, and to serve [niche], strikes me as a serious one. I’d love to understand the trade-offs from the inside…”

Variant for an alumnus

Always lead with the alumni connection. Soka is small enough that any alumnus will recognize the name and the unusual nature of the school. Be warmer than you would with a stranger.

Sample opener: “I’m a current Soka freshman — I think we share an unusual lineage. I came across your profile while looking for alumni who’d ended up in financial services, and I’d be grateful for fifteen minutes whenever the next month allows. I’m not looking for a favor — just to learn from someone who walked a path I’m trying to understand…”

Variant for a branch manager (more senior, more cautious)

Branch managers are gatekeepers. Approach later in your sequence, after you’ve already had warm chats with advisors in their office. Be more deferential, shorter, and more direct about your intent.

Sample opener: “I’ve had the chance to speak with two of the advisors in your branch over the last several months, both of whom suggested you might be the right person for a brief introductory conversation. I’m a freshman at Soka exploring wealth management seriously and would be grateful for fifteen minutes whenever the next month allows…”

Variant for an advisor who hasn’t replied in months

Wait for an event-trigger and use it as a soft re-entry. Don’t apologize for re-emerging.

Sample opener: “Saw the firm’s recent commentary on [topic] and thought of our exchange earlier this year. I won’t bury you in catch-up — just wanted to reopen the door if there’s ever a time to grab fifteen minutes. Happy to revisit whenever it’s convenient…”

Email templates for every stage of the cycle

These are templates, not scripts. Rewrite each in your voice before you ever press send. What follows is the structure to imitate, not the words to copy.

Template — the post-coffee-chat thank you (within 24 hours)

Subject: Thank you — and the [article / paper / question] you mentioned

Dear [Name],

Thank you again for the time this morning. The point you made about [specific thing they said, paraphrased] has stayed with me — particularly your framing of [specific phrase or insight], which I hadn’t thought about that way.

As promised, here’s the [article / resource] I mentioned: [link or summary]. If the introduction to [colleague’s name] is still possible, I would be genuinely grateful — and either way, I’ll plan to send a short update in a few weeks on what I’ve been reading and thinking.

With thanks,
Apex

Template — the “keeping warm” touch (60–90 days after a chat)

Subject: A short update — and a quick question

Dear [Name],

Following up briefly on our conversation in [month]. Two short updates and one question.

Since we last spoke, I’ve been working through [book or area you went deep on, ideally one they mentioned], and the chapter on [specific concept] connected back to your point about [thing they said]. The other piece I’ve been spending time on is [project or learning].

One question I’ve been turning over: [a substantive, well-formed question on a topic you actually care about, not generic]. No need for a long reply — even a sentence would help.

Hope the [season / quarter / specific recent event] is going well.

Warmly,
Apex

Template — asking for a referral inside their office

Subject: A small ask

Dear [Name],

Thinking about my second conversation with someone in [their firm/branch]. Our chat in [month] was easily the most useful one I’ve had this year, and you mentioned [colleague’s name] in passing — the way you described their work made me think they’d be someone I’d genuinely benefit from speaking with.

If you’re comfortable making a quick introduction, I’d be very grateful. If not, no pressure at all — I’ll reach out separately. Either way, thank you for everything you’ve already shared.

Warmly,
Apex

Template — explicitly asking about an internship (when the time is right)

Subject: Quick follow-up — thinking about this summer

Dear [Name],

Stepping back from our last conversation, I wanted to be honest about where my thinking has landed. I’d love to spend this summer inside a practice like yours — learning operationally, helping in a junior capacity, and getting a real feel for the rhythm of the business.

I’m comfortable starting in a small role, paid or unpaid for credit, whichever fits how your team has done these in the past. If there’s any chance this is something your team could explore — or that you’d be open to introducing me to a colleague in the branch who might — I would be very grateful for the conversation.

Either way, thank you for the generosity you’ve already shown me.

Warmly,
Apex

Template — the gracious post-rejection thank-you

Subject: Thank you — and staying in touch

Dear [Name],

Thank you for the update and, more importantly, for the time and care your team has shown me through the process. I’m disappointed not to be joining you this summer, but I’m genuinely grateful for the conversations — they’ve sharpened how I think about this work.

If it’s alright, I’d like to stay in touch — light, every couple of months — and reapproach next year as a sophomore. Any feedback you’d be willing to share would also help me show up better next time.

Wishing your team a great summer.

With thanks,
Apex

Template — the “saw something that made me think of you” touch

Subject: This made me think of our conversation

Dear [Name],

Came across [specific article / paper / podcast] this week and it tied back to the point you made about [thing they said] in our last chat. Two short pieces of it that I think you’d find interesting:

1. [Brief specific takeaway]
2. [Brief specific takeaway]

Sharing in case it’s useful. No reply needed.

Warmly,
Apex

Coffee-chat dialogue scenarios — the moments most freshmen blow

The cold email is hard. The coffee chat is harder. Below are the awkward moments that actually decide whether the conversation works, with the strong move in each one.

Scenario 1: The advisor opens with “So, tell me about yourself.”

This is your 60–90 second opener. Three beats: where you’re from and what shaped you, what you’re working on now, why you’re in this conversation. Don’t recite your resume. Don’t list clubs. Speak slowly, end clearly, then stop. Stopping cleanly is the move — most freshmen trail off into “and yeah, that’s kind of me, I guess.”

Advisor “So, tell me a little about yourself.”
You “Sure. I’m a freshman at Soka, originally from Nepal. My background is in math and software — I’ve had a couple of open-source pull requests merged into projects I cared about, which is how I learned that I really like building things in domains I have to understand deeply. Over the last six months I’ve become genuinely curious about wealth management — the way an advisor sits at the intersection of investing, taxes, and family dynamics — and that’s why I reached out. I’m mostly trying to understand the business from people doing it well.”
Advisor “Got it. Why us?”

Notice how clean the stop is. No filler. No “and so yeah.” She knows you’re finished, you’re comfortable in the silence, and now she’s on her next question.

Scenario 2: The chat is supposed to be 20 minutes, you’re 5 minutes in, and the advisor is being terse.

This happens. The advisor is busy, distracted, possibly having a bad day. The wrong move is to over-compensate by talking more. The right move is to ask a question that gets her talking, and to make it specific enough that she has to think.

You “I imagine your last few weeks have been busy — I appreciate the time. One thing I’ve been genuinely curious about, that I haven’t been able to get from any article: when you watched practices fail or stall in your career, what was the most common reason?”

Why this works: it’s specific, it’s about her experience (which she has a lot of), and it requires a real answer. She’ll engage. Terse advisors usually become talkative once the question respects their time.

Scenario 3: The advisor asks a technical question you don’t know.

You will get one technical question you don’t know. The trap is to bluff. The move is to be honest cleanly, demonstrate the way you’d think about it, and promise the follow-up.

Advisor “What do you think about how Roth conversions are affected by IRMAA brackets?”
You “Honestly, I don’t have a clean answer to that off the top of my head — I know what each piece is in isolation, but I haven’t worked through the interaction in the way you’re asking. Would you mind if I think about it after this conversation and email you a one-paragraph answer? I’d genuinely like to be able to walk through that one cleanly.”
Advisor “Sure.”

Then you actually do it. That follow-up email, with a careful one-paragraph answer she can use, is one of the best deposits you can possibly make on an advisor’s ledger.

Scenario 4: The advisor turns the chat into a soft interview.

Sometimes a coffee chat suddenly shifts — the advisor is impressed enough that she starts asking interview-style questions about your fit. This is your moment. Don’t slip into casual mode. Treat the rest of the conversation as a real interview while keeping the warmth.

Advisor “If we were considering you for a small role in this office, what would you want to do first?”
You “Sit with the CSA for a couple of days — understand the operational shape of the practice, what’s on every plate, what’s annoying everyone right now. Then I’d look for one operational thing that’s clearly behind and just quietly catch it up so the team feels relief. Once I’ve done that, I’d propose a slightly bigger project I could own — a CRM cleanup, a newsletter framework, a prospect list build — that you’d sign off on. By the end of month one I want the team to feel they’d miss me if I left.”
Advisor “That’s a thoughtful answer.”
Scenario 5: The advisor brings up a recent market headline expecting you to have an opinion.

You need a current view. Have one ready every time. The structure: one specific driver, one piece of evidence, one portfolio implication, one uncertainty you respect.

Advisor “What did you think about the Fed yesterday?”
You “The most interesting thing for me wasn’t the rate decision itself, which was priced in. It was the shift in the dot plot — the median FOMC view moved [direction]. The market is now pricing [implication], but the longer end of the curve hasn’t fully caught up, which to me suggests duration is still slightly under-priced for the new view. The uncertainty I’d respect is that core services inflation has been stickier than everyone hoped, so any single CPI print could re-anchor expectations.”

If you don’t know the specifics of yesterday, say so cleanly: “I haven’t read the statement yet — what was your take?” The honesty is fine. The bluff is fatal.

Scenario 6: The advisor asks about your visa / work authorization.

As an international student you will get this. The right move is to be calm, specific, and brief. Don’t volunteer concerns; answer factually.

Advisor “You’re an international student — can you work in the US over the summer?”
You “Yes — F-1 students at Soka can use CPT or pre-completion OPT for paid internships if the role aligns with my course of study, which a wealth-management internship would. My international office walks through the paperwork, and I’ve already had the preliminary conversation. Long-term work authorization is a longer answer, but for this summer there’s no real obstacle.”

That answer is short, factual, and doesn’t panic the advisor. Most advisors only need to hear that you’ve thought about it and the summer paperwork is solvable.

Scenario 7: The advisor wraps the call abruptly.

Sometimes you only get ten minutes. Don’t panic about the asks you didn’t make. Close warmly, send the follow-up email within four hours instead of twenty-four, and treat the truncated conversation as the start of a slow build.

Advisor “Apex, I’m so sorry, I’ve got a meeting starting in two minutes — can we wrap?”
You “Of course — thank you for what you’ve already shared. Would you be open to me staying in touch lightly, and if there’s a colleague in your office I’d benefit from speaking with as well, I’d be grateful for an intro. I’ll send a short follow-up note this afternoon either way.”
Scenario 8: The advisor says no internship is available, but is otherwise warm.

This is a frequent outcome and not actually a failure. Treat it as the start of a multi-year relationship.

Advisor “We don’t hire freshman interns — we typically take juniors during the firm’s summer-analyst window.”
You “Understood — that’s helpful to hear directly. Two things, if I may. First, would you mind if I stayed in touch lightly over the next year, with the hope of being a stronger candidate when the sophomore-summer process opens? Second, even before then, is there anyone in the branch — whether a different team or a more junior advisor — who has occasionally taken freshmen on for smaller engagements? I’m flexible on shape and would be open to anything substantive, paid or unpaid for credit.”

You may not get a yes. You will get respect. And a year from now, when she’s deciding who to call back, you’ll be top of mind.

Step 4 — The follow-up sequence

Most replies arrive on the second contact. Many on the third. Almost none after the fourth without an event-trigger. Here is the rhythm:

  • Day 0: send the email.
  • Day 5: gentle follow-up. “Wanted to gently bump this up — I’m sure your inbox is busy. Still glad to make this work whenever it’s convenient.”
  • Day 14: one more, with a softer angle. “Last note from me — no pressure at all. If now’s not a good time, I’d love to revisit in a couple of months.”
  • Day 60+: an event-trigger. The firm announces something, they win an award, the market does something dramatic, you finish a relevant project. Use the event as a natural reason to reach out without sounding like you’re still chasing.

If after three contacts you get no reply, move on cheerfully. A non-reply is not personal. Most advisors are buried.

Step 5 — The phone call / walk-in

Cold-calling a branch is unusual but powerful. The script is short and respectful.

Phone script — calling a branch

Receptionist picks up.
You: “Hi, my name is Apex Poudel, I’m a freshman at Soka University. I was hoping to leave a message for Ms. Lee — I sent her an email last week introducing myself and wanted to gently follow up. I’m happy to call back at a better time if she’d prefer.”

Tone: warm, polite, low-key. You are not selling. You are introducing yourself like a young professional. The receptionist often becomes your quiet ally.

Walk-ins (only if you happen to be near a branch) are even less common but occasionally work for an extraordinarily polished candidate. Dress like an intern who already has the job, walk in, ask politely if it would be possible to leave a short note or a resume for the branch manager. Be brief, leave, follow up by email. The point is the story you can tell other advisors next week: “I dropped by the office, met the front desk, and left a note for the branch manager.”

Step 6 — The coffee chat

You got the reply. You’re on the calendar for 20 minutes. Don’t blow it.

Before the meeting

  • Re-read the advisor’s bio, their team’s page, and any recent articles or interviews.
  • Skim their firm’s latest weekly market commentary so you have current context.
  • Prepare 5–7 thoughtful questions. Always have more than you need.
  • Decide on your 30-second story.
  • Dress one level above what you’d wear to class. Even on Zoom.

The flow of a 20-minute chat

  1. Minute 0–2: warm greeting, thanks, light context. (“Thanks so much for the time. I know you’re in the middle of a busy week.”)
  2. Minute 2–5: your story, briefly. Where you’re from, what you’re studying, why you’re curious about WM.
  3. Minute 5–15: ask them about their path, their practice, their thinking on the business. Listen more than you talk.
  4. Minute 15–18: ask one substantive question about the work — something you genuinely want to know.
  5. Minute 18–20: the close. Thank them. Ask for a referral. Ask if it’s okay to stay in touch.

The all-important close

Most freshmen flub the close. They mumble “thanks so much” and disappear. Here is the close that quietly opens doors:

You “This has been incredibly helpful — truly. Before I let you go, two quick things. First, would you be open to me staying in touch as I learn? I’ll keep it light — one email every couple of months. Second — and please tell me if this is too much — if there’s a colleague or another advisor you think I’d benefit from speaking with, even briefly, I’d be grateful for an introduction.”

You will be amazed how often the answer to both is yes. Every advisor knows other advisors. A warm intro from inside the firm is worth ten cold emails.

Step 7 — The follow-up note

Within 24 hours, send a short, specific thank-you email. Reference one thing they said. Reaffirm anything you offered or they offered. Keep it brief.

Follow-up email template

Subject: Thank you — and the article you mentioned

Dear Ms. Lee,

Thank you again for the time yesterday. The point you made about how the integrated planning conversation has shifted since rates moved higher landed for me — I’ll be thinking about it.

As promised, here’s the article on cross-border estate planning I mentioned. And if the introduction to David in your office is still possible, I would be very grateful. Either way, I’ll send a short update in a few weeks on what I’ve been reading.

Warmly,
Apex

Step 8 — The slow build (the part nobody teaches)

Most students think the coffee chat is the goal. It isn’t. The goal is the second conversation. People hire interns they’ve spoken to two or three times, not once. Your job after a successful first chat is to quietly stay on the radar until they think of you when an opportunity opens.

Methods:

  • Email an article you read that connects to something they said. Two sentences max.
  • Share something you built or wrote — a short summary of a book on behavioral finance, a tool you put together, a finance-blog post.
  • Congratulate them on something public — an award, a Barron’s mention, a hire.
  • At 90 days, ask a substantive question by email and reference the previous conversation.
  • At 120 days, request another short call. By this point you’re a known quantity.

Step 9 — Asking for the role

You don’t want to be the freshman who shows up and says “please give me an internship.” You want to be the freshman they offer one to. The way that conversion happens is something like this:

Advisor “So what are you thinking about doing this summer?”
You “Honestly, I’m hoping to get in the door of a practice like yours and learn the business from the inside. I’d be useful in a junior capacity — outreach, organization, anything analytical or technical — and I’m willing to start unpaid for credit if that’s how it works at your office. Is that the kind of thing your team or any team in the branch ever takes on?”

That phrasing is light, gracious, and very hard to refuse outright. If the answer is “not us, but maybe Bob in our branch” — perfect. If the answer is “let me think about it” — perfect, the door is open. If the answer is “no” — thank them sincerely and move on.

Special tactics for your specific situation

Lean into being international and multilingual

Most freshmen sound the same. You don’t. You speak Hindi and Nepali (and Spanish-in-progress), you have a software background, and you’re at a small intellectual liberal arts college most advisors have never recruited from. This is rare, and rare is valuable. A growing share of HNW wealth in the US flows through immigrant families — Indian, Chinese, Latin American, South Asian first-generation entrepreneurs who built businesses and now have to plan for taxes, succession, and the next generation. Many of them want to be served by advisors who get them. You can be that future advisor, and a sharp branch manager will see it immediately.

Lean into your technology background

You merged PRs. You can build. Most advisor offices have terrible internal tooling and would pay real money for someone who can clean up a CRM, build a Power Query report, automate a dashboard, or set up a simple internal AI assistant. Make this part of your pitch in early outreach. It separates you from English majors who want to “learn about markets.”

Soka-specific angle

Soka is small and unusual. Don’t hide it — package it. A good frame: “I picked Soka because I wanted a small, deeply intellectual environment for the core years of my education. The trade-off is that I have to be more proactive about real-world finance exposure, which is why I’m reaching out earlier and more directly than most students would.” That sentence flips a perceived weakness into proof of seriousness.

What “making it” actually looks like

If you do the work in this chapter for ten weeks, here is what your funnel will plausibly look like:

  • 80 advisors identified.
  • 60 emails sent.
  • 18 replies.
  • 14 coffee chats.
  • 4–6 follow-up conversations.
  • 2–3 real opportunities (winter shadow, summer intern, branch-wide role).
  • 1 freshman internship offer at a place that matters.

This is not magic. It’s a numbers game played with care.

The mentor’s final word on this chapter

The single most underrated trait among successful freshmen is composure. Be the person who emails politely, follows up gracefully, never panics, and never resents an unanswered email. Branch managers and advisors are quietly testing this from the first message. They are wondering: if I put this kid in front of a client, will I be embarrassed? Your composure is half the answer.

The complete catalog of freshman mistakes — avoid every one of these

This is the unfiltered list. If you make even half of these, you’ll be invisible. If you avoid most of them, you’ll be remembered. Read through every item and check yourself honestly.

Outreach mistakes

Sending the same generic email to 50 advisors.

Advisors recognize mail-merge templates within three seconds. The advisor receives a dozen of these a month, and they all blur into a single mental category labeled ‘ignore.’ Always include one specific detail about that advisor’s practice in the first paragraph.

Asking for an internship in the first email.

You haven’t built any standing yet. Asking for the role up front is the lowest-conversion move possible. Ask for fifteen minutes of conversation; the internship conversation can come on the third or fourth contact.

Writing a 600-word cold email.

The advisor will not finish it. Aim for under 180 words. Lead with one specific reference to her practice, one sentence about who you are, one low-friction ask, one polite sign-off.

Using a personal Gmail address that looks unprofessional.

Use your school email. If it’s genuinely something embarrassing, create a clean Gmail using your real name (firstname.lastname@gmail.com) and use that consistently.

Sending email at 11 p.m. or on Sunday.

Schedule sends for Tuesday through Thursday morning, 8–10 a.m. local time. Your email arrives at the top of her inbox rather than buried in a weekend backlog.

Following up too many times.

Three is the limit. After that, you’re in stalker territory and an advisor will tell colleagues about it. Wait for an event-trigger and re-enter with grace months later.

Sending the ‘just bumping this up’ follow-up the day after the original.

Five business days is the minimum. Anything faster signals impatience and reads as needy.

Coffee chat mistakes

Showing up without having read the advisor’s bio.

This is unforgivable and instantly noticed. Spend ten minutes before any conversation. Know her team, her credentials, her clientele, anything she’s been quoted in.

Talking more than the advisor.

You are not the show. Aim for a 30/70 split — you talking 30% of the time, the advisor 70%. Most freshmen invert this ratio and lose the room.

Asking generic questions you could have Googled.

‘What does Morgan Stanley do?’ is a 5-second internet search. Your questions should be substantive enough that the advisor has to think.

Trying to sound smart by using jargon you don’t fully understand.

The advisor catches this instantly. Stick to vocabulary you can defend, and admit it cleanly when something is new to you. Curiosity beats false sophistication every time.

Letting the conversation end without three asks.

Close every chat with: (1) permission to stay in touch, (2) a request for a colleague introduction, (3) an offer to send the article or resource you mentioned. Three asks. Every time. Most freshmen ask zero.

Failing to send the same-day follow-up.

Within 24 hours, a specific thank-you referencing one substantive thing discussed. This single habit separates the top 10% of student networkers from everyone else.

Being late or early by more than five minutes.

Five minutes early is fine. Ten minutes early creates awkwardness. Late, even by two minutes, is genuinely costly. Build buffer time into your travel.

Dressing one notch too casual.

For an advisor coffee chat: business casual at minimum, business formal preferred. Even for a Zoom. Even if she explicitly said it’s casual. The cost of overdressing is zero; the cost of underdressing is real.

Talking poorly about other advisors or firms.

Never. The financial-services world is small, and word travels. Praise generously; criticize never.

Spending the chat in ‘impress mode’ instead of ‘learn mode.’

The freshmen who get remembered are the ones who made the advisor talk about herself, not the ones who recited their resume.

Resume and LinkedIn mistakes

A two-page resume.

One page. Always. Even if it feels sparse. A sparse resume is fine for a freshman; a two-page resume signals padding.

Listing ‘Microsoft Office’ as a skill.

This is 2024. Excel is assumed. List things that actually differentiate you.

Generic action verbs (‘helped,’ ‘assisted,’ ‘worked on’).

Stronger: ‘built,’ ‘launched,’ ‘led,’ ‘analyzed,’ ‘designed,’ ‘wrote,’ ‘quantified.’ And follow each with a number.

A LinkedIn headline that just says ‘Student at Soka University.’

Make it a sentence: ‘Freshman at Soka studying math and economics; curious about wealth management and how families think about money.’

A LinkedIn photo that’s a cropped party picture.

Take a clean headshot. Plain background. Professional-but-warm expression. Use the same photo on resume, LinkedIn, and any application.

Not having a LinkedIn at all.

You need one. Every advisor will Google you. The absence signals lack of seriousness.

Interview mistakes

Answering ‘tell me about yourself’ with a 3-minute autobiography.

60–90 seconds. Three beats: where you’re from, what you’re doing now, why you’re in this conversation.

Saying ‘I’m passionate about finance.’

Every freshman says this. It means nothing. Replace with a specific moment that ignited the curiosity.

Saying your biggest weakness is that you ‘work too hard’ or are ‘a perfectionist.’

Dead. Pick a real weakness, name it precisely, show the work you’ve done to manage it.

Pretending to know something you don’t.

Every interviewer catches this. Say cleanly: ‘Honestly, I don’t have a clean answer to that off the top — I’d want to think about it. May I follow up afterward?’ That answer wins.

Not having questions to ask the interviewer.

Have three to five prepared. Generic questions are worse than no questions. The best ones make the interviewer talk about themselves and their work.

Asking about salary, hours, or benefits in a freshman interview.

Save those for the offer stage. Asking too early signals you’re here for the wrong reasons.

Forgetting to send a same-day thank-you.

One short paragraph, within 24 hours, referencing one substantive thing from the conversation. Most candidates skip this. The cost of skipping is far higher than the candidate realizes.

Long-arc mistakes

Burning relationships when an advisor says no.

A polite, gracious no-thank-you reply keeps the door open for two, three years. A bitter or pushy reply closes it permanently. The compound interest on graciousness over time is enormous.

Networking in bursts and then disappearing.

Consistency beats intensity. Five conversations a week for thirty weeks is far more effective than thirty conversations in a single week.

Treating networking as transactional.

Advisors can smell it instantly. Treat people as people, not as steps on a ladder. Five years from now, you’ll be grateful you did.

Not keeping a CRM.

You will lose track of who said what, when, in which conversation. A simple spreadsheet with name, firm, date of last contact, notes, and next-step date will save you a hundred small embarrassments.

Pretending to be someone you’re not.

The freshmen who succeed long-term are the ones whose interview persona matches the person who shows up on day one. Faking a polish you don’t have is exhausting and short-lived. Be the more careful, more prepared, more articulate version of who you actually are.

When you have a target list of 60+ advisors, a polished email template, and 5 coffee chats already on your calendar, this chapter has done its job.
Roadmaps — The Real Plans

Roadmaps. For each path you might actually want.

Okay, so. Now you have all this knowledge sitting in your head. The Playbook chapter showed you the general moves. But what you actually need is a specific plan for the path you care about. So this chapter does exactly that — month-by-month plans for Morgan Stanley Wealth Management (the main one, since that’s your target), J.P. Morgan, Merrill, UBS, RBC, Raymond James, and then briefer roadmaps for the other paths like IB, PE, S&T, AM, and HF. Pick the one you care about most. Read it slowly. Then come back to the others.

How to use these roadmaps

A few honest things up front, so you don’t treat these like commandments:

  • These are guides, not laws. Your real path will adjust based on who answers your emails, what your grades look like, what your family situation needs, and what opportunities show up randomly. Don’t panic if your timeline slips by a month.
  • The dates are anchored to a freshman starting college in late August. Adjust if your school year starts differently.
  • The roadmaps assume you’ll read the rest of this site in parallel. The roadmap tells you what to do each month; the other chapters tell you how.
  • The Morgan Stanley one is the most detailed. Because that’s probably what you actually want, and because the other firms’ roadmaps share most of the same structure with different specifics layered on top.

1. Morgan Stanley Wealth Management — your freshman-year roadmap, month by month

This is the centerpiece. Take it seriously. If you actually do what’s in this section over the next twelve months, you will be in the top 1% of freshman candidates targeting Morgan Stanley WM. Almost no one does what’s in here. That is exactly why you should.

Before we start — what Morgan Stanley Wealth Management actually is

Okay, so first, let’s get the picture clear in your head. When people say “Morgan Stanley,” they could mean one of several things:

  • Institutional Securities (IS): this is the famous side — investment banking, sales & trading, equity and fixed-income research. It serves corporations, governments, hedge funds, asset managers. This is where the “Wall Street” image lives.
  • Wealth Management (WM): this is what you actually want. It serves individuals and families — from $250K mass-affluent clients up through $20M+ households. It’s the largest revenue contributor to the firm now, after the Smith Barney acquisition (2009) and the E*TRADE acquisition (2020).
  • Investment Management (IM): this is the asset-management arm, building investment products. It serves institutions and is part of what shows up in client portfolios.
  • Morgan Stanley Private Wealth Management (MSPWM): the elite tier of WM, serving UHNW clients ($20M+). Sometimes lumped with WM, sometimes treated as separate.

For your purposes, MS WM is what you’re targeting. Within MS WM, there are tens of thousands of Financial Advisors (FAs) spread across hundreds of branch offices in the US. Each branch has a Complex Manager (the senior leader for several offices), a Branch Manager, and the FA teams themselves. The FA teams are where you, the freshman intern, actually live.

How MS WM hiring actually works (this is the key insight)

There are two completely parallel hiring systems at MS WM. Most freshmen only know about one. The one nobody talks about is the one you’ll actually use.

Track A: The centralized firm programs

These are the programs MS publishes online. They’re competitive, polished, and heavily target-school filtered:

  • Wealth Management Summer Analyst Program (juniors): 10-week paid internship, structured rotations, formal recruiting through Workday, applications open in summer for the following year.
