For a bakery, inventory is mostly raw materials — flour, butter, packaging — that’ll be used to make bread soon. Some inventory might also be finished goods waiting to be sold: bread that didn’t sell today, packaged goods that go out tomorrow.

Inventory is the trickiest current asset to manage. Too little and you run out of stock and miss sales. Too much and you tie up cash in goods that might go stale (literally, in a bakery’s case) or become unsellable.

Inventory turnover — COGS / Inventory — tells you how many times per year the inventory cycles through. For Crumb: $200K COGS / $10K inventory = 20x. The bakery sells through its inventory about every 18 days, which makes sense given the perishability of bread.

Compare that to a luxury watchmaker that might have inventory turnover of 1–2x (watches sit in display cases for months) or Costco at 12x (rapid throughput).

Equipment (net of depreciation): $108,000

Crumb bought $120,000 of equipment on Day 1. After one year of depreciation at $12,000 per year, the “book value” of that equipment is $108,000.

This is the “Property, Plant, and Equipment” (PP&E) line you’ll see on most balance sheets. For a tech company it might be servers and offices. For an airline it’s planes. For a retailer it’s store buildouts. For Crumb it’s ovens, mixers, fridges.

The thing to understand: book value isn’t the same as market value. The equipment might be worth more or less than $108K if Crumb tried to sell it. Book value is just the accounting record. Real-world value is what you’d get in the market.

Accounts Payable (A/P): $8,000

The mirror image of accounts receivable. Money Crumb owes to suppliers but hasn’t paid yet.

The flour supplier delivers $4,000 of flour and gives Crumb 30 days to pay. Until that 30 days is up, the $4,000 sits as A/P on the liabilities side. The packaging supplier might be $3,500. The utility company that bills monthly $500. Total $8,000.

A/P is “good debt” in the sense that it’s interest-free financing from your suppliers. The longer you can stretch your A/P (within reasonable limits), the more cash you free up. Big retailers like Walmart and Amazon have famously long A/P cycles — they sell the goods before paying the supplier, which generates “negative working capital,” meaning growth actually generates cash instead of consuming it.

Accrued expenses: $4,000

Costs Crumb has incurred but hasn’t paid yet. Examples: unpaid wages owed to bakers (you pay them every two weeks but the next payday hasn’t come yet), interest accrued on the bank loan but not yet due, utilities used this month but not yet billed.

Conceptually similar to A/P, but for non-supplier costs.

Short-term portion of debt: $5,000

The piece of the bank loan that’s due in the next 12 months. Crumb’s loan was a 10-year amortizing loan, so each year about $10K of principal is being paid down. The next 12 months’ worth ($5K, roughly) sits as a current liability.

Long-term debt: $87,500

The rest of the bank loan. Originally $100K, Crumb paid down some principal during the year, and is now down to $92,500 in total debt — of which $5K is short-term and $87,500 is long-term.

Debt is one of the most important lines on the balance sheet. Too much debt makes the company fragile — a bad year and you can’t pay interest, and bankruptcy follows. Too little debt and you might be missing the “tax shield” advantage and operating leverage that debt provides. There’s no single right answer; it depends on the business.

Owners’ equity: $161,500

The owners’ claim on the business after all liabilities are paid off. It started at $100,000 (the founders’ original contribution) and grew by the $61,500 of net income that was retained inside the business (rather than distributed as dividends). New balance: $161,500.

Equity grows when:

  • The business is profitable and retains earnings.
  • New equity is issued (e.g., a stock offering).

Equity shrinks when:

  • The business loses money.
  • Dividends are paid out.
  • The company buys back stock.

For public companies, the equity section is usually broken into “common stock at par value,” “additional paid-in capital,” “retained earnings,” and “treasury stock” (negative, if any). You don’t need to memorize the breakdown for most freshman interviews.

The big idea about the balance sheet

Here’s the way I want you to think about a balance sheet from now on: it’s a photograph of the business’s capital at one moment.

The asset side shows you where the capital is sitting right now. Cash, inventory, equipment, receivables — that’s where the money has been put to work.

The liabilities-and-equity side shows you where the capital came from. Borrowed from a bank? Equity from the owners? Sitting on a supplier’s tab? Earned by the business itself?

Every dollar on the left has a story on the right about how it got there. That’s why the two sides always balance — they’re just two views of the same set of dollars.

What balance sheets tell you in 60 seconds

When an interviewer asks you to scan a balance sheet quickly, the things to look at:

  1. Is the cash growing or shrinking? Healthy growing companies usually build cash unless they’re aggressively reinvesting or paying dividends.
  2. How big is debt relative to equity? A debt/equity ratio above 2 starts to feel risky for non-financial companies.
  3. How big is goodwill relative to total assets? Big goodwill = lots of past acquisitions. Watch for impairments.
  4. Are A/R or inventory growing way faster than revenue? Both are yellow flags — either credit terms slipping or demand softening.
  5. Is working capital expanding? If yes, the business is absorbing cash even as it grows.

Those five questions, run through any balance sheet, will tell you 80% of what you need to know about the company’s financial shape.

Key balance-sheet concepts you need to know cold

Accounts receivable (A/R)

Money customers owe you that you haven’t collected yet. Asset, because it’s a future inflow. Builds up when you sell on credit. If A/R grows faster than revenue, you’re effectively financing your customers, which is a yellow flag.

Accounts payable (A/P)

Money you owe suppliers for goods/services you’ve already received but haven’t paid for. Liability. Increases in A/P actually help your cash position — you have the goods, you haven’t paid yet.

Inventory

Stuff sitting in your warehouse waiting to be sold. Asset. When you sell it, it leaves inventory and becomes COGS. Excess inventory ties up cash and can become obsolete (think fashion retailers).

Prepaid expenses

Expenses paid in advance, like a year’s rent paid up front. Asset, because you’ve already paid and you’ll get the benefit over time. Reduces gradually as time passes.

Deferred revenue (unearned revenue)

Cash a customer paid you for something you haven’t delivered yet. Liability, because you owe them the product/service. Common in subscription businesses — Netflix collects your annual subscription cash on day one but earns the revenue monthly.

PP&E (Property, Plant, and Equipment)

Long-lived tangible assets the business uses to operate — buildings, machinery, vehicles. Capitalized when bought, then depreciated over their useful life. Net PP&E = gross PP&E − accumulated depreciation.

Goodwill

The premium one company paid to acquire another beyond the fair value of the target’s identifiable net assets. Sits on the balance sheet as an intangible asset, not depreciated, but tested for “impairment” periodically.

Retained earnings

The cumulative net income of the business since inception, minus any dividends paid. Lives inside equity. The bridge between the income statement and the balance sheet over time: each year’s net income adds to retained earnings.

The Cash Flow Statement — the truth about money

Okay. This is the part where it really starts to matter. Net income and cash flow are not the same thing. Profitable companies go bankrupt all the time because they run out of cash. This is the single most important non-obvious idea in accounting, and most freshmen learn it as a fact rather than really understanding why.

Let me give you a vivid scenario so you really feel it.

A made-up company that goes broke while “profitable”

Imagine a small construction company. They land a $5M contract to build a warehouse for a developer. The contract says: $2M upon completion of foundations, $2M upon completion of framing, $1M upon completion of finishes. Standard milestone billing.

The construction company has to pay for materials, subcontractors, and labor as the work happens — lumber suppliers, electricians, plumbers, the steel team. They pay roughly $3M of those costs in the first three months while they’re building the foundations and framing.

Under accrual accounting, they can “recognize” revenue based on percentage of completion. If they’re 60% done at the three-month mark, they record $3M of revenue. Costs of $3M. Reported profit: zero, but no loss yet. Things look fine on the income statement.

But what does the cash flow statement look like? They’ve only collected $2M (the foundations payment). They’ve spent $3M. Net cash impact: minus $1M. They had $500K of cash on hand at the start of the project. Now they have negative $500K. The bank won’t extend them more credit. Subcontractors aren’t getting paid. Lawsuits start. The project stalls. Within weeks they file for bankruptcy.

And here’s the punchline: on their last income statement, they reported a small profit. Their accountant could have said with a straight face, “You had a profitable quarter.” But they died from a lack of cash.

This is real. It happens to construction companies, manufacturers, importers, even fast-growing software companies. The income statement says one thing. The cash flow statement says the actual truth.

Why the disconnect happens — accrual accounting

Net income is calculated under accrual accounting: you record revenue when you earn it (regardless of whether the cash has arrived), and you record expenses when you incur them (regardless of whether you’ve paid). This produces a more honest picture of operating profitability over time, but it can diverge sharply from cash in any given period.

The cash flow statement reconciles the two — it shows you the actual cash that moved in and out, organized by source.

The three sources of cash — intuitively

There are only three ways a business gets or uses cash:

  • From running the business — collecting from customers, paying suppliers, paying employees, paying taxes. This is cash flow from operations (CFO). The lifeblood of the business.
  • From investing in long-term things — buying equipment, building a factory, acquiring another company. Or the opposite — selling assets to raise cash. This is cash flow from investing (CFI). Usually a use of cash for healthy growing companies.
  • From financing the business — borrowing money, paying back loans, issuing stock, buying back stock, paying dividends. This is cash flow from financing (CFF).

The three add up to the net change in cash for the period. That number has to match the change in the cash line on the balance sheet between the start and end of the period. If it doesn’t, you’ve made an arithmetic error.

Three sections of the cash flow statement

  1. Cash flow from operations (CFO): cash generated by running the business. Starts from net income and adjusts for non-cash items and working-capital changes.
  2. Cash flow from investing (CFI): cash spent on or received from long-term assets — mostly capex (buying equipment, etc.) and acquisitions, or sales of assets.
  3. Cash flow from financing (CFF): cash from raising money or returning it — issuing or repaying debt, issuing or buying back stock, paying dividends.

The three sections add up to the net change in cash, which has to match the change in the cash line on the balance sheet between two periods. If it doesn’t, you’ve made an error somewhere.

Crumb’s year-one cash flow statement

Cash Flow from Operations
Net income61,500
+ Depreciation12,000
− Increase in A/R(5,000)
− Increase in inventory(10,000)
+ Increase in A/P8,000
+ Increase in accrued expenses4,000
CFO70,500
Cash Flow from Investing
Equipment purchases (capex)0
CFI0
Cash Flow from Financing
Repayment of debt(7,500)
Dividends0
CFF(7,500)
Net change in cash
CFO + CFI + CFF63,000
+ Beginning cash(80,000 − ...)
Ending cash52,500

Notice how the cash flow statement starts with net income (the bottom of the income statement), then adjusts it for non-cash items (like depreciation) and working-capital changes to arrive at actual cash from operations. This bridge — net income to CFO — is the core of every “walk me through the three statements” interview question.

Depreciation, the most-asked concept in finance

Let’s slow down and make depreciation airtight, because every interview will test it.

What it is

Depreciation is the accounting practice of spreading the cost of a long-lived asset over its useful life, rather than expensing the entire cost in the year you bought it.

When Crumb bought $120,000 of equipment, that $120,000 didn’t leave the income statement in year one. It went onto the balance sheet as an asset and is now being “used up” gradually. If the useful life is 10 years and we use straight-line depreciation, that’s $12,000 of depreciation expense per year for ten years.

Why it exists

Because expensing the full $120,000 in year one would make year one look catastrophic and years two through ten look artificially great. The matching principle says: match expenses to the period in which they generate revenue. The oven generates revenue for ten years, so its cost should be spread across those ten years.

Why interviewers love it

Because depreciation is the cleanest example of how an item flows through all three statements at once, and how the three statements link. If you can walk through it confidently, you signal that you actually understand accounting, not just memorize it.

The greatest hits interview question: “Walk me through how $10 of depreciation flows through the three statements.”

Okay, this is the interview question. If you can’t answer this cleanly, you fail the technical screen at almost any wealth-management, IB, or analytical interview. If you can answer it cleanly, you immediately differentiate from 80% of freshman candidates. So let’s really nail it.

I’m going to give you the answer three different ways — first the slow walk-through, then a few mock-answer variants in your voice, then the variant that handles follow-up questions.

The slow walk-through — understand it deeply first

Assume a 25% tax rate. Take a deep breath. Here we go.

Step 1: Income statement. Depreciation is an expense on the income statement. So if depreciation increases by $10, operating income (EBIT) decreases by $10. That makes pre-tax income decrease by $10.

Now, taxes. At a 25% tax rate, $10 less pre-tax income means $2.50 less in taxes. So instead of paying X in taxes, we pay X − $2.50.

That means net income decreases by $10 minus $2.50 = $7.50.

Pause and absorb that. Even though depreciation was $10, the actual hit to net income is only $7.50, because the $10 expense also saves $2.50 in taxes.

Step 2: Cash flow statement. We always start the cash flow statement with net income. So we start with −$7.50 from this transaction (because net income dropped by $7.50).

But here’s the trick: depreciation is a non-cash expense. We didn’t actually write a $10 check to anyone. The $10 was just an accounting allocation of past spending. So in the cash flow statement, we “add back” the $10 of depreciation to undo it.

Net effect on cash from operations: −$7.50 + $10 = +$2.50.

That extra $2.50 of cash is real. It comes from the tax savings — we paid $2.50 less in taxes because of the depreciation deduction. This is the famous “depreciation tax shield” and it’s why high-depreciation businesses can have surprisingly strong cash flow.

Step 3: Balance sheet. Let’s tally the asset side first.

  • PP&E (Property, Plant, & Equipment) decreases by $10. Because that’s what depreciation is — the gradual reduction of the book value of long-term assets.
  • Cash increases by $2.50. From the cash flow effect we just calculated.
  • Net change on the asset side: −$10 + $2.50 = −$7.50.

Now the liabilities + equity side.

  • Liabilities don’t change. (Depreciation doesn’t affect what the company owes anyone.)
  • Equity decreases by $7.50. Because net income flowed into retained earnings, and net income dropped by $7.50.
  • Net change on the L+E side: −$7.50.

Both sides changed by exactly −$7.50. The balance sheet balances. That’s the magic of double-entry accounting.

Variant 1 — the conversational mock answer (your voice)

“Okay, so — let’s assume a 25% tax rate.

On the income statement, $10 of depreciation hits as an expense. It reduces pre-tax income by $10, and taxes drop by $2.50, so net income falls by $7.50.

On the cash flow statement, we start with net income, which is now $7.50 lower. But depreciation was non-cash — we didn’t actually write a check. So we add the $10 back as a non-cash adjustment. Net effect on cash from operations is plus $2.50. That extra cash is basically the tax shield.

On the balance sheet, PP&E drops by $10, cash rises by $2.50. So the asset side falls by $7.50. On the other side, retained earnings drops by $7.50 because of the lower net income. Both sides balance.

And that’s the cool thing about this question — it’s a tiny accounting move, but it touches all three statements at once, and the balance sheet still balances. That’s why interviewers love asking it.”

Variant 2 — the slightly more detailed version (good for technical interviewers)

“Sure. Let’s do it with a 25% tax rate so the math is clean.

Income statement first. Depreciation is an operating expense, so it reduces EBIT by $10. That means pre-tax income drops by $10. Taxes drop by $2.50 because we’re paying tax on a smaller number. So net income falls by $7.50.

Cash flow statement next. We start at net income, which is down $7.50. But depreciation is a non-cash expense — no money actually left the company. So we add the $10 back in the operating-activities section. Net result: operating cash flow goes up by $2.50, which is exactly the tax shield generated by the deduction.

Balance sheet last. On the asset side, gross PP&E hasn’t changed, but accumulated depreciation has gone up by $10, so net PP&E falls by $10. Cash is up $2.50. Net asset change: minus $7.50. On the other side, no change to liabilities; retained earnings drops by $7.50. So both sides drop by $7.50, and we’re still balanced.

One nuance worth mentioning — depreciation is also what gives growing capital-intensive businesses a structural cash-flow advantage. The book-vs-cash gap can be huge for things like utilities, telecom, and pipelines.”

Variant 3 — the version when you forget the tax rate

If the interviewer doesn’t specify a tax rate, just say:

“Can I assume a 25% tax rate for the math?”

They will almost always say yes. If they say “assume zero for simplicity,” then the math is even cleaner: net income drops by $10, cash flow rises by $0 (the $10 expense and the $10 add-back cancel out), and on the balance sheet PP&E drops by $10 and retained earnings drops by $10. Still balanced.

The follow-up questions interviewers love

Why does the balance sheet still balance?

Because every accounting entry has two sides — double-entry accounting. When we expensed $10 of depreciation, we did two things at once: we reduced an asset (PP&E) by $10, and we recognized an expense that flowed through to a $7.50 reduction in net income, which flows to retained earnings. The $2.50 cash savings is the tax effect. The asset side and equity side both move down by $7.50 net. That symmetry is what makes the balance sheet always balance.

What if I told you the company has a net operating loss and isn’t paying taxes?

Good question. If the company has no current tax liability — because it’s in a loss position — then the $10 of depreciation doesn’t generate a current cash tax shield. The tax savings might still build a deferred tax asset, but in the current period cash from operations would be roughly flat instead of up $2.50. Net income would drop by the full $10. Balance sheet still balances, just with a different combination of effects.

Walk me through how $10 of capex flows through the three statements.

Slightly different. Capex hits the cash flow statement in “investing activities” — cash flow drops by $10. On the balance sheet, cash decreases by $10, but PP&E increases by $10. Net asset change: zero. Equity unchanged. The income statement is unaffected at the moment of purchase — that’s the whole point of capitalizing it instead of expensing it. The income-statement impact comes later, in the form of annual depreciation. Capex is the “turn cash into a long-lived asset” entry. Depreciation is the “turn the long-lived asset back into an expense over time” entry. Together they form the full cycle.

What about $10 of stock-based compensation?

Similar structure but with a twist. Stock-based comp is recorded as an operating expense on the income statement, reducing EBIT by $10 and net income by $7.50 (at 25% tax). On the cash flow statement, it’s added back as non-cash, so operating cash flow rises by $2.50 from the tax shield. On the balance sheet, cash rises by $2.50, retained earnings drops by $7.50, and — this is the twist — additional paid-in capital (a piece of equity) rises by $10. So the equity side has two pieces that net out: retained earnings down $7.50, APIC up $10, net equity change is up $2.50, exactly matching the cash increase. The mature interviewer point here is that SBC looks like depreciation accounting-wise but has real economic dilution because new shares are being created.

If you can deliver Variant 1 or Variant 2 cleanly, and handle one or two of the follow-ups, you are in the top 5% of freshmen on this question, full stop. The competition is genuinely that thin. Memorize the structure, then practice until it doesn’t feel memorized.

Walking through other interview-favorite scenarios

Walk me through how the three statements connect.
This is the rock-bottom test of whether you actually understand accounting. If you can’t answer this cleanly, every other technical question gets harder.
Variant 1 — the “follow the dollar” version (most intuitive)

“Okay, so the way I’d describe it is — you follow the dollar.

Start with the income statement. Net income comes out the bottom. That number flows into two places. One, it goes to the top of the cash flow statement — that’s where the cash flow statement starts. Two, it flows into retained earnings on the balance sheet, in the equity section.

On the cash flow statement, we adjust net income for non-cash items like depreciation, and for working-capital changes, to get to cash from operations. Then we layer in investing activities — mainly capex — and financing activities, like debt issuance, debt paydown, dividends. Sum it up and you get net change in cash.

That net change in cash flows into the cash line on the balance sheet, which lets the balance sheet reconcile period over period.

Other connections worth mentioning — capex on the cash flow statement adds to PP&E on the balance sheet, and depreciation on the income statement reduces PP&E over time. Debt activity on the cash flow statement changes the debt line on the balance sheet. Stock activity changes the equity section. Every line on the cash flow statement traces back to a balance sheet account that’s either increasing or decreasing.”

Variant 2 — the “structured connections” version

“Sure. The three statements connect through three main flows.

First, net income from the income statement flows into the cash flow statement as the starting point, and also into retained earnings on the balance sheet.

Second, the cash flow statement reconciles net income to actual cash. Non-cash items like depreciation get added back. Working-capital changes — A/R, inventory, A/P — get adjusted because they affect cash but not net income on a one-to-one basis. Investing activities like capex are added. Financing activities like debt and dividends are added. The result is net change in cash.

Third, the net change in cash from the CFS plugs into the cash line on the balance sheet, which keeps the balance sheet balanced from one period to the next.

So in essence, the income statement tells you profitability over a period, the balance sheet snapshots position at one moment, and the cash flow statement is the bridge that makes the two reconcile.”

Variant 3 — the simple version (when you’re nervous)

“Net income flows from the income statement to two places. One, to the top of the cash flow statement, where we adjust for non-cash items and working capital to get to cash from operations. Then we add investing and financing activities to get net change in cash. That net change updates the cash line on the balance sheet.

Two, net income flows into retained earnings on the balance sheet, in the equity section. Capex on the CFS updates PP&E on the balance sheet. Depreciation on the IS reduces PP&E. That’s the loop.”

Variant 4 — the “analogy” version (most memorable)

“The way I’d describe it — the three statements are three different views of the same company.

The income statement is the ‘did we make money this period’ view. It shows revenue minus all the costs.

The balance sheet is the ‘what do we own and owe right now’ view. A snapshot at a single moment.

The cash flow statement is the ‘where did the cash actually move’ view. It tracks dollars in and out.

The three connect because they’re views of the same set of transactions. Net income from the IS flows into retained earnings on the BS and to the top of the CFS. The bottom of the CFS — net change in cash — updates the cash line on the BS. Capex on the CFS updates PP&E on the BS. Depreciation on the IS reduces PP&E on the BS. Everything reconciles.

The reason this question gets asked so often is — if you can do it cleanly, it proves you understand accounting from first principles, not just memorized definitions.”

Variant 5 — the “why this matters” version (good for senior interviewers)

“The mechanical answer is — net income flows from the income statement into the cash flow statement and retained earnings, the cash flow statement reconciles net income to actual cash, and the net change in cash plus other CFS line items update the balance sheet.

But the bigger point I’d want to make is why the three statements all exist. Net income is opinion — it depends on accrual decisions, depreciation methods, accounting choices. Cash flow is fact — it’s what actually moved. The balance sheet is the snapshot — it’s what you have at one moment.

A complete picture of any business needs all three. Profitable companies have gone broke because they couldn’t generate cash. Cash-rich companies can be unprofitable. Asset-heavy companies can have terrible returns. You need to read all three to see what’s actually happening.”

The most common follow-up

After you walk through the connections, interviewers often follow up with “okay, walk me through how $10 of depreciation flows through all three.” That’s the most-asked follow-up. Practice both questions back-to-back — if you can do them in sequence cleanly, you’re fluent.

If you could only pick one statement to evaluate a business, which would you pick and why?
Elite answer

“The cash flow statement. Net income is an accounting construct — it includes accruals, deferrals, and non-cash items that can be manipulated. The cash flow statement tells you whether the business is actually generating cash, which is what ultimately funds growth, pays creditors, and rewards owners. Income is opinion; cash is fact. That said, you really want to read all three together — the income statement gives you margins, the balance sheet gives you leverage and asset quality, and the cash flow statement gives you the truth about how the business actually runs.”

Why is the cash flow statement different from net income?
Elite answer

“Net income is calculated on an accrual basis — you record revenue when earned and expenses when incurred, not when cash moves. The cash flow statement strips out the non-cash and timing differences so you can see what actually happened to cash. The biggest reconciling items are usually depreciation and amortization (non-cash expenses you add back), and changes in working capital — a growth in receivables uses cash even though it doesn’t hit net income, because you’ve recognized the sale but haven’t collected the cash yet.”

What is working capital, and why does it matter?
Elite answer

“Working capital is current assets minus current liabilities — the short-term capital tied up in the operating cycle. For most businesses, the major components are inventory plus receivables on the asset side and payables on the liability side. As the business grows, working capital usually grows with it — more inventory, more receivables — which absorbs cash even when the business is profitable. That’s why high-growth businesses can be cash-negative despite strong income. The flip side: businesses with negative working capital, like Amazon historically, get to grow on suppliers’ money.”

A company spends $100M on a new factory. Walk me through the impact on the three statements at the moment of purchase and over time.
Elite answer

“At the moment of purchase, the income statement is unaffected because capex is capitalized, not expensed. On the cash flow statement, $100M leaves under cash flow from investing. On the balance sheet, PP&E increases by $100M, cash decreases by $100M — net asset change is zero, so the balance sheet still balances. Over time, the $100M is depreciated. Assume a 10-year useful life and straight-line depreciation: $10M of depreciation per year. Each year, the income statement takes a $10M depreciation expense, reducing pre-tax income by $10M and net income by $10M times (1 − tax rate). On the cash flow statement, that $10M is added back to net income as non-cash. On the balance sheet, PP&E goes down $10M each year, retained earnings adjust by the net-income hit, and cash adjusts by the tax savings. The factory’s book value goes to zero after ten years even though it may still be useful, and the difference between book value and economic value is one of the limits of GAAP.”

What is goodwill, and how is it created?
Elite answer

“Goodwill is the premium an acquirer pays for a company above the fair value of its identifiable net assets. If you pay $1B for a company whose identifiable net assets are worth $700M, you book $300M of goodwill on the acquirer’s balance sheet. It sits there as an intangible asset, is not depreciated, but is tested annually for impairment. If the acquired business deteriorates, the acquirer writes down goodwill — a non-cash charge that hits net income but not cash flow. Big goodwill writedowns are often signals that an acquisition didn’t go as planned.”

If A/R goes up by $50, what’s the impact on the three statements?
Elite answer

“On the income statement, nothing immediate — the sale was already recorded as revenue. On the cash flow statement, an increase in A/R is a use of cash — CFO drops by $50. On the balance sheet, A/R goes up $50 (asset increase), and cash falls $50 (asset decrease). Net asset change is zero. Equity is unchanged. So everything balances. The big-picture interpretation: the company recognized $50 of revenue without yet collecting the cash, so its earnings overstate its cash generation that period by $50.”

Why does inventory matter for a business’s cash position?
Elite answer

“Inventory is cash sitting on a shelf in a different form. When inventory rises, cash falls (you paid for the goods); when it falls (because you sold it), cash rises. The danger is when inventory rises faster than sales, signaling either overproduction or weakening demand. Retailers and manufacturers manage inventory turnover obsessively because every dollar of stuck inventory is a dollar that isn’t earning a return.”

What’s the difference between depreciation and amortization?
Elite answer

“Same idea, different asset class. Depreciation spreads the cost of tangible long-lived assets like buildings and equipment. Amortization spreads the cost of intangible assets like patents, software, or capitalized R&D. Both are non-cash expenses that hit the income statement and get added back on the cash flow statement.”

What’s the difference between EBITDA and net income, and when does each matter?
Elite answer

“EBITDA is earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization — a proxy for the cash-generating ability of the operating business, stripped of capital structure and non-cash accounting. It’s useful for comparing companies with different leverage or different capex patterns, and it’s widely used in M&A and credit analysis. Net income is the after-everything bottom line that flows into retained earnings and EPS. EBITDA is great for valuation comparability; net income is the legal profit number. Importantly, EBITDA is not cash flow — it ignores working capital changes and ignores capex, both of which can be huge for capital-intensive businesses.”

How is the income statement linked to the balance sheet?
Elite answer

“Through net income and retained earnings. Each period, net income from the income statement flows into the equity section of the balance sheet via retained earnings, reduced by any dividends paid. That single flow is what links the two statements over time.”

What are accruals?
Elite answer

“Accruals are the accounting entries that recognize revenue or expense in a period even when the cash hasn’t moved in that period. Recognizing a sale before collecting cash creates accounts receivable. Recognizing an expense before paying creates accrued expenses or accounts payable. Receiving cash before delivering creates deferred revenue. Accruals are the difference between cash accounting and accrual accounting; the cash flow statement is where you reconcile the two.”

The cash conversion cycle — how cash actually moves through a business

Imagine Crumb again. A loaf of bread goes through a journey from raw flour to cash in the register. The cash conversion cycle measures how long, in days, a dollar of cash spent on flour takes to come back as a dollar of cash from a customer.

The formula:

CCC = DIO + DSO − DPO

  • DIO (Days Inventory Outstanding): how many days inventory sits before being sold. = (Inventory ÷ COGS) × 365.
  • DSO (Days Sales Outstanding): how many days it takes to collect cash from a credit sale. = (A/R ÷ Revenue) × 365.
  • DPO (Days Payable Outstanding): how many days the business takes to pay its suppliers. = (A/P ÷ COGS) × 365.

A short CCC is generally great — the business gets its money back fast and has less capital trapped in operations. A negative CCC, like Amazon’s, means the business collects cash from customers before paying suppliers, effectively financing growth on the suppliers’ balance sheets. A long CCC, like a luxury watchmaker holding inventory for years, requires more working capital and is more sensitive to financing costs.

Interview-grade insight

“Two companies with the same operating margin can have wildly different ROICs because one ties up working capital and the other doesn’t. The cash conversion cycle is the single best one-glance measure of how operationally efficient a business actually is — you can quote it before you ever look at the income statement.”

Financial ratios you should know on instinct

Ratios let you compare businesses of different sizes and quickly read a company’s shape. The good news: you don’t need to memorize all of them. You need to understand what each one is asking, so you can compute it from first principles whenever it’s useful.

Liquidity ratios — can the company pay its bills?

  • Current ratio = current assets ÷ current liabilities. Above 1 means short-term assets exceed short-term obligations. 1.5–2 is generally healthy for non-financial businesses; far above 3 can signal capital sitting idle.
  • Quick ratio (acid test) = (cash + marketable securities + A/R) ÷ current liabilities. Same idea, stricter — excludes inventory, which can be hard to sell quickly. Above 1 is healthy.
  • Cash ratio = (cash + marketable securities) ÷ current liabilities. The most conservative test.

Leverage ratios — how indebted is the business?

  • Debt-to-equity = total debt ÷ total equity. Quick read on capital structure mix. Highly variable by industry — utilities run high D/E; tech companies often run very low.
  • Debt-to-EBITDA = total debt ÷ trailing EBITDA. The single most-watched leverage metric in credit analysis. Sub-2x is conservative; 2–4x is moderate; above 6x is highly levered (often LBO territory). Bond covenants are usually written against this metric.
  • Interest coverage = EBIT ÷ interest expense. How many times over the operating profit covers the interest bill. Below 2x is stress; above 5x is comfortable.
  • Net debt / EBITDA: same as above but using net debt (debt minus cash). Often more economically accurate for cash-rich companies.

Profitability ratios — how good is the business?

  • Gross margin = gross profit ÷ revenue. Reveals how much room the business has between price and direct cost. Software companies run 70–90%; commodity manufacturers can run 10–20%.
  • Operating margin (EBIT margin) = EBIT ÷ revenue. After SG&A and R&D and depreciation, what’s left.
  • EBITDA margin: EBITDA ÷ revenue. A capital-structure-neutral, capex-agnostic profitability proxy.
  • Net margin = net income ÷ revenue. The end-to-end profitability after everything.
  • ROE (Return on Equity) = net income ÷ shareholder equity. How efficiently shareholders’ capital is being deployed. Be careful: ROE can be inflated by leverage.
  • ROA (Return on Assets) = net income ÷ total assets. Operating efficiency before financing.
  • ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) = NOPAT ÷ invested capital. The cleanest measure of how good a business actually is. Compare it to WACC: if ROIC > WACC, the business is creating value; if not, it’s destroying it.

The DuPont decomposition

An elegant way to break ROE into its component drivers:

ROE = (Net Income / Revenue) × (Revenue / Assets) × (Assets / Equity)

= Net Margin × Asset Turnover × Leverage

What it says: a company can be profitable (high margin), efficient (high asset turnover), or levered (high assets/equity). Two firms with the same ROE may have arrived there by very different routes. A high-margin software company gets there with margin; a low-margin retailer like Costco gets there with asset turnover; a bank gets there with leverage. Knowing which engine is driving the return is essential before you trust it.

Revenue recognition — the rules that decide when income is income

Under modern accounting (ASC 606 / IFRS 15), revenue is recognized when control of a good or service transfers to the customer. That sounds simple; in practice it’s the source of half of all accounting scandals.

The five-step model:

  1. Identify the contract with the customer.
  2. Identify the performance obligations in the contract.
  3. Determine the transaction price.
  4. Allocate the transaction price to performance obligations.
  5. Recognize revenue as each obligation is satisfied.

Examples of why this matters:

  • A SaaS company selling a 3-year subscription for $36,000 cash up front recognizes $1,000 of revenue per month, even though it has all the cash. The remaining $35,000 sits as deferred revenue on the balance sheet.
  • A construction company building a stadium recognizes revenue over time as the project progresses, based on the cost-completion method.
  • A retailer selling a phone with a bundled 2-year warranty has to allocate the price between the phone (recognized at sale) and the warranty (recognized over 2 years).

Aggressive revenue recognition — recognizing income too early, or treating non-revenue items as revenue — has been the engine of many famous frauds (Enron, WorldCom, Wirecard). When a public company beats earnings while its receivables explode, that’s a flag worth investigating.

Stock-based compensation — the non-cash that’s not really non-cash

Many companies, especially in tech, pay employees in equity rather than cash. The accounting treatment: the value of the equity grant is recognized as an expense on the income statement over the vesting period, just like cash compensation. The offsetting credit goes to additional paid-in capital in equity.

On the cash flow statement, the expense is added back as non-cash — because the company didn’t actually pay cash. This is why “adjusted EBITDA” and similar metrics often add back SBC.

The catch: while SBC is non-cash in the moment, it’s very real in two ways. First, it dilutes existing shareholders — new shares are being created. Second, the company typically buys back shares to offset the dilution, which is a real cash outflow. So the “non-cash” expense is, in steady state, paid for by the buyback. The reason this distinction matters: a tech company that adds back $2B of SBC and then spends $2B on buybacks is, in cash terms, paying $2B of compensation. Pretending otherwise — as some “adjusted” metrics implicitly do — flatters the true economics.

The mature view: include SBC as a real cost when comparing tech companies’ profitability and cash generation. The market has gotten more skeptical of “ex-SBC” metrics over time, partly because of this.

Lease accounting — an underrated topic that shows up everywhere

Under modern accounting (ASC 842 / IFRS 16), companies must capitalize most leases onto the balance sheet as a right-of-use asset and a corresponding lease liability. This was a major change — previously, operating leases sat off-balance-sheet, making heavily-leased companies (retailers, restaurants, airlines) look much less levered than they actually were.

Consequences:

  • Balance sheets of retailers ballooned overnight when the rules changed.
  • Comparisons across periods require care.
  • Adjusted leverage metrics now often include lease obligations.

Deferred taxes — the line that confuses everyone

Deferred tax assets and liabilities arise because GAAP accounting and tax accounting use different rules. A company might depreciate an asset over 10 years for GAAP but accelerate the depreciation for tax purposes — producing different taxable income in different years. The mismatch creates a deferred tax line on the balance sheet that reconciles over time. Net operating losses (NOLs) from past loss years also sit as deferred tax assets, which can be valuable to acquirers in M&A.

Goodwill impairment — the silent confessional of a bad deal

When an acquirer pays a premium for a target, the premium sits as goodwill on the acquirer’s balance sheet. It’s not amortized, but it’s tested for impairment at least annually. If the acquired business deteriorates — whether due to bad luck, bad execution, or a deal that should never have been done — the acquirer writes goodwill down. The write-down is a non-cash hit to the income statement but a very public admission of failure. AOL/Time Warner is the textbook case — tens of billions of goodwill impaired in the years following the merger.

Inventory accounting — LIFO vs FIFO, in plain English

This comes up in interviews. Let me walk you through it slowly.

Imagine a small electronics store. In January, they buy 100 televisions at $200 each. In June, they buy 100 more at $250 each (prices went up). In December, they sell 150 televisions.

Question: what was the cost of goods sold for those 150 televisions? It depends on which TVs you assume they sold first.

FIFO (First In, First Out)

FIFO assumes the oldest inventory gets sold first. So the 150 TVs sold are: all 100 of the $200 TVs (sold first) + 50 of the $250 TVs.

COGS = (100 × $200) + (50 × $250) = $20,000 + $12,500 = $32,500.

Inventory remaining = 50 TVs × $250 = $12,500.

LIFO (Last In, First Out)

LIFO assumes the newest inventory gets sold first. So the 150 TVs sold are: all 100 of the $250 TVs (sold first) + 50 of the $200 TVs.

COGS = (100 × $250) + (50 × $200) = $25,000 + $10,000 = $35,000.

Inventory remaining = 50 TVs × $200 = $10,000.

Why the choice matters

Notice: same physical transactions, but LIFO produces higher COGS, lower gross profit, lower taxes. FIFO produces lower COGS, higher gross profit, higher taxes.

During inflation (prices rising), LIFO gives you a tax break because COGS is higher and reported profits are lower. That’s why some industries use LIFO — petroleum, steel, anything with rising input costs.

During deflation, the reverse: FIFO gives lower taxes.

One catch: IFRS (international accounting standards) doesn’t allow LIFO. Only US GAAP does. So global comparisons get tricky.

The interview-grade insight

When you see “LIFO reserve” on a balance sheet, it’s the difference between what inventory would be worth under FIFO and what it’s currently worth under LIFO. To compare a LIFO company to a FIFO company, you adjust the LIFO company’s inventory and COGS using the LIFO reserve disclosure. Most analysts do this implicitly when running comps.

Capitalize vs expense — the most important judgment call in accounting

Here’s a deceptively simple decision that companies make every day: when you spend money on something, do you put the cost on the income statement right away (“expense it”) or do you put it on the balance sheet as an asset and depreciate it over time (“capitalize it”)?

This sounds technical but it has massive implications.

The rule

If the spending creates a long-lived benefit that will help generate future revenue, you capitalize it. If the benefit is consumed in the current period, you expense it.

Concrete examples:

  • Office rent for the month: expensed. Benefit consumed this month.
  • An oven that’ll last 10 years: capitalized. Benefit spread over 10 years.
  • R&D salaries: under US GAAP, mostly expensed (special treatment). Under IFRS, some can be capitalized.
  • Software development costs: in the development phase, sometimes capitalized; in the maintenance phase, expensed.
  • Marketing spend: expensed. (You can’t capitalize advertising under GAAP even though it arguably has long-term brand effects.)
  • Acquisition price for a competitor: capitalized. The premium becomes goodwill on the balance sheet.

Why management cares so much

If a company capitalizes spending, current-period expenses are lower, and current-period profit is higher. The depreciation hits future periods instead. So capitalizing aggressive amounts of cost can make a company look more profitable in the near term, at the cost of bigger depreciation later.

This has been the source of many accounting scandals. WorldCom famously capitalized operating expenses to inflate earnings; the fraud unraveled in 2002. AIG and Enron used aggressive interpretations in various ways. So “quality of earnings” analysts watch capitalization decisions carefully.

How it shows up in interviews

If asked “what changes for the 3 statements when a company capitalizes $10 of spending vs expenses it?” The answer:

  • Expensing it: $10 hits the income statement as expense, net income drops by $7.50 (at 25% tax), cash flow drops $7.50 (from net income) but the $10 was already spent so total cash impact is $10 leaving the business as operating cash. Balance sheet: cash −$10, retained earnings −$7.50, but we need to reconcile — the $2.50 tax shield offsets this.
  • Capitalizing it: nothing hits income statement this period. $10 of cash leaves under investing activities (capex line). Balance sheet: cash −$10, PP&E +$10. Future periods: $1/year of depreciation if 10-year useful life. So profit looks better now, worse later.

Ten “what changes in the 3 statements” scenarios — the question bank

Interviewers love these. Practice each one until you can rattle it off. Assume 25% tax rate unless noted.

1. Depreciation increases by $10

IS: D&A expense up $10, pre-tax income down $10, taxes down $2.50, net income down $7.50.

CFS: net income down $7.50, but add back $10 of non-cash depreciation, so cash from operations up $2.50.

BS: PP&E net down $10, cash up $2.50 (tax shield), retained earnings down $7.50. Both sides down $7.50.

2. Capex of $10

IS: nothing immediately. (Will depreciate over useful life going forward.)

CFS: $10 cash outflow under investing activities. CFO unchanged.

BS: cash down $10, PP&E up $10. Asset side flat. Equity unchanged.

3. Accounts receivable goes up by $50 (a sale made on credit)

IS: revenue up $50. Assuming COGS is unaffected for this question (just looking at A/R change in isolation), pre-tax income up $50, net income up $37.50.

CFS: net income up $37.50. But A/R is a use of cash — the $50 of revenue didn’t come in cash — so subtract $50 in working capital adjustment. Net effect on operating cash flow: $37.50 − $50 = −$12.50. Plus the cash tax outflow.

BS: A/R up $50, cash down $12.50, total assets up $37.50. Retained earnings up $37.50 on the equity side. Balanced.

The intuition: the company recognized profit but didn’t collect cash, so it’s actually worse off in cash terms.

4. Inventory of $100 is purchased on credit (using accounts payable)

IS: nothing. Inventory hasn’t been sold yet.

CFS: nothing. No cash moved.

BS: inventory up $100, accounts payable up $100. Asset side and liability side both up $100. Balanced.

The interesting wrinkle: when you eventually sell the inventory and pay the supplier, the IS gets revenue and COGS, and the BS will see the inventory and A/P both decline.

5. $20 of inventory previously held is sold for $50 cash

IS: revenue $50, COGS $20, gross profit $30, net income $30 × (1 − 0.25) = $22.50.

CFS: net income up $22.50. Working-capital change: inventory down $20 (a source of cash). So CFO up $22.50 + $20 = $42.50. (Less cash taxes paid of $7.50, so true cash change is +$35 from the sale assuming taxes paid in cash. Different conventions exist.)

BS: cash up by the after-tax cash, inventory down $20. Retained earnings up $22.50. Balanced.

6. Company issues $100 of equity

IS: nothing.

CFS: $100 cash inflow under financing activities.

BS: cash up $100, equity up $100 (specifically, common stock + additional paid-in capital). Asset side and equity side both up $100.

Note: existing shareholders are diluted because there are now more shares outstanding. The total equity is bigger but each share represents a smaller piece.

7. Company pays a $50 dividend

IS: nothing (dividends don’t hit the income statement — they’re a distribution to owners, not an expense).

CFS: $50 outflow under financing activities.

BS: cash down $50, retained earnings down $50.

8. Company issues $100 of debt

IS: nothing immediately. (Future interest expense will hit the IS.)

CFS: $100 inflow under financing activities.

BS: cash up $100, debt up $100.

Subsequent periods: interest expense of, say, $5/year (at 5%) hits the IS, reducing pre-tax income by $5 and net income by $3.75. The tax shield is $1.25. Cash interest of $5 leaves under operating cash flow.

9. Company repays $100 of debt principal

IS: nothing. (Interest expense is the only IS impact; principal repayment isn’t an expense.)

CFS: $100 outflow under financing activities.

BS: cash down $100, debt down $100.

10. Company writes down inventory by $30 (turned out to be obsolete)

IS: $30 expense (often shown as “inventory write-down” or buried in COGS). Pre-tax income down $30, net income down $22.50.

CFS: net income down $22.50, but add back $30 of non-cash write-down. Net CFO impact: +$7.50 (the cash tax shield).

BS: inventory down $30, cash up $7.50 (tax shield), retained earnings down $22.50. Total asset change −$22.50, total equity change −$22.50. Balanced.

Goodwill impairment, asset write-downs, and inventory write-downs all follow this same pattern: non-cash IS hit, retained earnings reduced, asset reduced, tax shield generates a small cash inflow.

Revenue recognition — the timing question that breaks companies

Under modern accounting (ASC 606 / IFRS 15), revenue is recognized when control of a good or service transfers to the customer. The five-step model:

  1. Identify the contract with the customer.
  2. Identify the performance obligations in the contract.
  3. Determine the transaction price.
  4. Allocate the transaction price to performance obligations.
  5. Recognize revenue as each obligation is satisfied.

Concrete examples

SaaS subscription: a company sells a 3-year contract for $36K paid up front. Even though all the cash hits day one, revenue is recognized $1K per month for 36 months. The remaining $35K sits as deferred revenue on the balance sheet — it’s a liability because the company still owes the customer 35 months of service.

Construction contract: a builder agrees to construct a $10M warehouse with 18 months of work. They can use the “percentage of completion” method: at month 9, if 50% of cost has been incurred, recognize 50% of revenue ($5M) and 50% of costs.

Bundled phone + warranty: a retailer sells an iPhone with a 2-year warranty for $1,100 total. They have to split the price between the phone (recognized at sale) and the warranty (recognized over 24 months). Say the phone’s fair value is $1,000 and the warranty is $100. Then $1,000 hits revenue immediately and $100 sits as deferred revenue, recognized at $4.17/month for 24 months.

Why aggressive revenue recognition has been the engine of frauds

Recognizing revenue too early is the most common form of accounting fraud. Famous examples:

  • Enron (2001): recognized future cash flows on energy contracts as current revenue, sometimes for contracts that didn’t actually exist.
  • WorldCom (2002): capitalized operating expenses as assets and aggressive revenue practices.
  • Lucent (2000): “channel stuffing” — recognizing revenue on shipments to distributors who hadn’t actually sold the product to customers.
  • Wirecard (2020): outright invention of revenue, including fictitious customers.

When a company beats earnings while its receivables explode disproportionately, that’s a flag. It’s revenue without cash — classic late-stage aggressive recognition.

Quality of earnings — the analyst’s deep read

“Net income” is one number. “Quality of earnings” is a judgment about how durable, sustainable, and real that number is. Sophisticated investors care more about quality than about the headline number itself.

Signs of high-quality earnings

  • Cash flow from operations roughly matches or exceeds net income.
  • Revenue growth tracks with industry demand and customer count.
  • Receivables grow in line with revenue, not faster.
  • Inventory turnover stays stable.
  • Tax rate is consistent and close to statutory.
  • No unusual one-time items inflating reported income.
  • Management discusses earnings using GAAP measures more than adjusted measures.

Signs of low-quality earnings

  • Persistent gap between net income and CFO — net income high but cash flow low.
  • Receivables growing significantly faster than revenue.
  • Inventory growing faster than sales (suggests stuffing or weakening demand).
  • Heavy reliance on “non-GAAP” or “adjusted” metrics that strip out a lot.
  • Big swings in tax rate from period to period.
  • Recurring “one-time” charges that show up every year.
  • Capitalizing things peers expense.
  • Aggressive estimates — bad-debt reserves dropped while receivables grew, useful lives extended to lower depreciation, pension assumption changes.

The CFO-to-net-income ratio

One quick screen: divide operating cash flow by net income. For a healthy mature business, the ratio should be roughly 1.0 to 1.3 (above 1.0 because depreciation is a non-cash add-back). If the ratio is well below 1.0, the company is recognizing income that isn’t turning into cash. That’s a yellow flag.

Financial ratios — the deep version with worked examples

You learned the ratios earlier in this chapter. Here let’s actually use them on a real-ish example.

Imagine a fictional consumer-products company:

  • Revenue: $1,000
  • COGS: $400 → Gross profit $600 → Gross margin 60%
  • OPEX: $300 → Operating income (EBIT) $300 → Operating margin 30%
  • D&A: $50 → EBITDA = EBIT + D&A = $350 → EBITDA margin 35%
  • Interest expense: $30
  • Pre-tax: $270
  • Tax (25%): $67.50 → Net income $202.50
  • Shares: 100, EPS = $2.025
  • Stock price: $40 → P/E = 19.75x
  • Total debt: $500, cash: $100 → Net debt $400
  • Market cap: 100 × $40 = $4,000
  • Enterprise value: $4,000 + $400 = $4,400
  • Total assets: $2,000, total equity: $1,200

Profitability ratios

  • ROE = Net income / Equity = $202.50 / $1,200 = 16.9%. A healthy ROE.
  • ROA = Net income / Total assets = $202.50 / $2,000 = 10.1%.
  • Asset turnover = Revenue / Total assets = $1,000 / $2,000 = 0.5x. Half a turn per year — typical for consumer goods.
  • DuPont ROE = Net margin × Asset turnover × Leverage = 20.25% × 0.5 × ($2,000/$1,200) = 20.25% × 0.5 × 1.67 = 16.9%. Checks out.

Valuation ratios

  • P/E = $40 / $2.025 = 19.75x. Slightly above market average; could indicate quality or overvaluation.
  • EV/EBITDA = $4,400 / $350 = 12.6x. Reasonable for a consumer products company.
  • EV/Sales = $4,400 / $1,000 = 4.4x. Premium — reflects high margins.

Leverage ratios

  • Debt-to-equity = $500 / $1,200 = 0.42. Moderate leverage.
  • Net debt / EBITDA = $400 / $350 = 1.14x. Conservative; lots of room for more debt before it gets risky.
  • Interest coverage = EBIT / Interest = $300 / $30 = 10x. Plenty of cushion.

What this tells us

This company is profitable, lightly levered, fairly valued. Nothing screaming. If we were comparing it to peers and they were all trading at 14x EV/EBITDA with comparable margins and growth, this would be a slight discount worth investigating. If peers were at 9x, this would look expensive. Always compare in context.

Working capital cycle — building the intuition

You met the cash conversion cycle earlier. Let me make it really visceral with two examples.

Costco — negative working capital

Costco has roughly:

  • Days inventory outstanding (DIO): ~32 days
  • Days sales outstanding (DSO): ~5 days (most sales are cash or credit card, near-immediate)
  • Days payable outstanding (DPO): ~32 days
  • Cash conversion cycle: 32 + 5 − 32 = 5 days

So Costco gets paid by customers about 5 days after it pays suppliers. Almost a wash. As Costco grows, it doesn’t absorb cash into working capital — growth is essentially self-funding.

A luxury watchmaker — positive working capital

A high-end watchmaker might have:

  • DIO: 365 days (watches sit in inventory for a year or more)
  • DSO: 60 days (wholesale customers on net-60 terms)
  • DPO: 45 days
  • CCC: 365 + 60 − 45 = 380 days

So this business has more than a year of cash trapped between buying raw materials and collecting from the customer. Growth requires huge working-capital investment. The faster the business grows, the more cash it consumes.

Amazon — the famous negative-CCC story

Amazon historically had:

  • DIO: ~30 days
  • DSO: ~20 days
  • DPO: ~80 days (terrifyingly long for suppliers, but Amazon’s scale lets it)
  • CCC: 30 + 20 − 80 = −30 days

Negative CCC means Amazon collects from customers before it has to pay suppliers. As Amazon grew, growth actually generated cash on the working-capital side. This is one of the deep economic advantages that made Amazon’s scaling possible despite thin profit margins.

Stock-based compensation — the accounting puzzle of modern tech

Tech companies pay employees heavily in stock. The accounting treatment is genuinely confusing for most investors. Let me explain it.

When a company grants an employee, say, $100K of stock that vests over 4 years, the company:

  • Recognizes a $25K expense on the income statement each year for 4 years (matching the vesting).
  • The offsetting credit goes to additional paid-in capital in equity (creating new shares essentially).

On the cash flow statement, the expense is added back as a non-cash item. So if you only look at “EBITDA ex SBC” or “adjusted EBITDA,” the SBC has disappeared.

The catch

While SBC is non-cash in the moment, it’s very real in two ways:

  • Dilution: new shares are being created. Existing shareholders own a smaller piece of the company.
  • Buybacks to offset dilution: many tech companies buy back shares to offset SBC dilution. Those buybacks are very real cash outflows.

So if a company adds back $2B of SBC to compute adjusted EBITDA, then spends $2B on buybacks to offset the new shares, the SBC was actually a real $2B of compensation paid in cash terms. The “adjusted” metric is misleading.

How to evaluate it

For tech companies, always include SBC as a real expense when comparing profitability. Many serious investors treat “EBITDA ex SBC” as the wrong number and look at “EBITDA including SBC” instead. The market has gotten more skeptical of ex-SBC metrics over the last decade, partly because of this dilution dynamic.

Goodwill and intangible assets — the M&A accounting deep dive

When one company buys another, the accounting treatment is more complex than just “cash leaves, asset comes in.” Let me walk through it.

Imagine Company A buys Company B for $1,000 in cash. Company B has:

  • Identifiable net assets: $700 (mostly PP&E, working capital, identifiable intangibles like patents)
  • Total purchase price: $1,000
  • Goodwill created: $1,000 − $700 = $300

On Company A’s post-acquisition balance sheet:

  • Cash down $1,000.
  • Company B’s identifiable assets and liabilities are absorbed at fair value, netting to +$700 in assets.
  • Goodwill of $300 is recorded as a new line on the balance sheet.

Goodwill is not amortized but is tested for impairment

Unlike PP&E or finite-life intangibles, goodwill doesn’t get amortized. It sits on the balance sheet at $300 indefinitely. But each year, the company has to test whether the acquired business is still worth at least the carrying value plus the goodwill.

If the acquired business deteriorates and the test fails, the company writes down goodwill. This is a non-cash hit to the income statement but a very public admission that the acquisition isn’t working out.

Famous goodwill impairments

  • AOL / Time Warner: $99 billion of goodwill written off in 2002, the largest accounting write-down in history.
  • HP / Autonomy (2012): $8.8 billion impairment after HP discovered accounting irregularities at the British software company it had acquired.
  • Microsoft / Nokia (2015): $7.6 billion impairment shortly after the deal.

When you see a big goodwill impairment in a company’s 10-K, it almost always tells a story of a bad acquisition.

Deferred taxes — the line that confuses everyone

Deferred tax assets and liabilities exist because GAAP accounting and tax accounting use different rules. Let me make this concrete.

Imagine a company buys equipment for $100. For GAAP purposes, it depreciates the asset over 10 years (so $10/year of depreciation expense). For tax purposes, it depreciates the asset over 5 years (more accelerated). The total depreciation is the same $100 in both cases, just spread differently.

What this means in any given year

In year 1:

  • GAAP depreciation: $10. GAAP pre-tax income lower by $10.
  • Tax depreciation: $20. Taxable income lower by $20.
  • The company actually pays tax on the lower taxable income (so saves $20 × 25% = $5).
  • But on the GAAP income statement, it shows a tax expense of $10 × 25% = $2.50.

The difference between GAAP tax expense ($2.50) and actual cash tax paid ($5 saved relative to no depreciation, or $2.50 saved relative to GAAP) creates a deferred tax liability. The company will pay more tax in future years to balance the early tax savings.

Deferred tax assets exist when the opposite happens — the company has paid more tax than GAAP would suggest, and will pay less in future years.

Why this matters

You don’t need to do the math live in an interview. You need to recognize the concept: GAAP and tax don’t use the same rules, and the difference creates balance-sheet line items called deferred taxes. Most companies have meaningful deferred tax items.

One practical application: net operating losses (NOLs). Companies that have lost money historically can carry those losses forward as deferred tax assets to shelter future profits. M&A acquirers sometimes target companies partly for their NOLs.

The 10-K — how to read it in 30 minutes

Every US public company files a 10-K each year. It’s the most important document for understanding any company. Here’s how to read one efficiently.

Section 1: Business overview (5 minutes). Read this if you don’t already know the company. Skim if you do.

Section 1A: Risk factors (5 minutes). Quickly scan the headings. Most risks are boilerplate. The unique ones — specific lawsuits, specific customer concentration, specific regulatory exposure — are worth pausing on.

Section 7: Management’s discussion and analysis (MD&A) (10 minutes). This is the gold. Management explains the year’s results, segment performance, and forward-looking thinking in plain English. Pay close attention to changes in language vs. last year — tone shifts often signal more than the numbers do.

Section 8: Financial statements (10 minutes). Read in this order: the cash flow statement first (truth), then the income statement (story), then the balance sheet (snapshot). Then footnotes — especially revenue recognition policies, segment data, and any unusual items.

Other useful sections: the proxy statement (Form DEF 14A) tells you executive compensation, board composition, and shareholder proposals. The 10-Q quarterly filings tell you the trajectory between annual reports.

Accounting red flags — what makes analysts nervous

A short checklist of things that should make you pause:

  • Net income growing faster than operating cash flow over multiple years.
  • Accounts receivable growing materially faster than revenue.
  • Inventory growing materially faster than sales.
  • Big “non-recurring” charges every year.
  • Heavy use of “adjusted” or “non-GAAP” metrics that strip out a lot.
  • Goodwill that’s a big share of assets — sign of acquisition-heavy past.
  • Recent change in auditor.
  • CFO turnover, especially without a clear successor.
  • Big stock-based compensation as a share of revenue.
  • Capitalized expenses growing faster than revenue (potentially aggressive cap policy).
  • Off-balance-sheet entities or unusual special-purpose vehicles.
  • Disclosures that grow long and complex year over year (often hides issues).

None of these is fatal alone. But seeing several together is when serious analysts get cautious.

Reading a real company — what to look for in 60 seconds

Once you have the basics, you can scan a 10-K or earnings press release in roughly a minute and pick up signals. Train this habit. Pick one S&P 500 company a week and do the following:

  1. Open the income statement. How fast is revenue growing year-over-year? Is gross margin stable, expanding, or compressing? Are operating expenses growing in line with revenue?
  2. Open the balance sheet. Is the cash balance rising or falling? Is debt rising? Is goodwill huge relative to total assets (signal of an acquisition-heavy past)?
  3. Open the cash flow statement. Is CFO meaningfully above or below net income? Why? What’s the capex level relative to depreciation?

Three minutes a week of this will, over a few months, turn you into someone who can talk fluently about any public company. That is interview gold.

When you can walk through depreciation across all three statements without notes, this chapter is yours.
Chapter 07 — Valuation

Valuation, from first principles — why a business is worth what it’s worth.

Almost every interesting question in finance reduces, eventually, to a single question: what is this thing worth? Stocks, companies, bonds, real estate, your future career, even your time. The methods change; the question doesn’t. Once you understand valuation conceptually, the formulas feel obvious. Let’s build the intuition layer first, and then earn the math.

The one idea that underlies everything: time value of money

Okay, deep breath. This idea is the foundation of all of finance. So I want to slow down here and really walk through it, because once you really get this, everything else clicks. Most students think they understand it. Most don’t.

The thought experiment

I’m going to make you a deal. I’ll give you a hundred dollars. You just have to pick: do you want it now, or a year from now?

Now, obviously. Right? But take a second and really think about why. There are at least four reasons hidden in that “obviously,” and finance is built on each one.

Reason 1 — opportunity. If I give you $100 today, you can put it in a savings account, or a bond, or an index fund. In a year, that $100 has become something more than $100. Even at a tiny 4% annual rate, it’s now $104. So “$100 a year from now” isn’t equivalent to “$100 today.” It’s strictly worse, by at least the amount you could have earned in the meantime.

Reason 2 — inflation. The same $100 buys less stuff next year than this year. Prices drift up. If inflation is 3%, the $100 in a year only buys what $97 buys today. So even if I gave you the same $100, it’s worth less in real terms.

Reason 3 — uncertainty. I might forget. I might die. I might decide not to pay. The further out the promise is, the more things can go wrong between now and then.

Reason 4 — you might need the money now. Even if all the above weren’t true, having flexibility today is worth something. You could spend it on a flight home. You could fix your bike. You could just feel more secure. Optionality has value, and time forecloses optionality.

So the answer “a dollar today is worth more than a dollar tomorrow” isn’t actually obvious. It’s a stack of four reasons. And finance is the discipline of being careful about each one.

Putting numbers on it

So let’s say a year from now, I’ll definitely give you $100. What would you pay today for that promise?

If the “going rate” (the return you could otherwise earn safely) is 5% a year, you’d pay about $95.24 today. Why? Because $95.24 invested at 5% grows to $100 in a year. Above that price, you’re overpaying. Below that price, you’re getting a deal.

Let’s do another one. What if you’re getting the $100 not in one year but in three years? At 5% per year, your money would need to grow for three years to become $100. So the present value today is:

$100 ÷ (1.05)3 = $100 ÷ 1.158 ≈ $86.38

So that future $100, three years out, is worth about $86 today. The further out it is, the less it’s worth today. Time discounts.

Now let’s reverse this. If I have $100 today and I invest it at 5% for three years, what does it become?

$100 × (1.05)3 ≈ $115.76

$100 today is worth $115.76 in three years. That’s the same fact, viewed from the other direction.

The two formulas

Future Value = Present Value × (1 + r)n

Present Value = Future Value ÷ (1 + r)n

These are the same fact in two directions. r is the discount rate (the rate at which money grows or shrinks in value). n is the number of years. That’s it. That’s the whole formula.

The second one — discounting — is the single most important formula in finance. Almost every valuation question is some version of: “What is this future stream of cash worth today?” And the answer is always: discount each future dollar back to today, then add them up.

Why the discount rate matters so much

Here’s the part that confuses people. Notice that in the formula, the discount rate r can be anything. So which number do you use?

The answer is: r should reflect the risk and the alternatives. If we’re discounting a guaranteed Treasury cash flow, r is the Treasury yield. If we’re discounting a corporate bond, r is that bond’s yield (Treasury yield plus a credit spread). If we’re discounting the cash flows of a risky business, r has to be higher to reflect the risk that those cash flows might not actually materialize.

The thing to remember: a higher discount rate means you’re demanding more compensation for risk, which means future cash flows are worth less to you today. A lower discount rate means you trust the cash flows more, so they’re worth more today. The same future dollar can be worth wildly different amounts today depending on how risky you think it is.

The big-picture takeaway

This one idea — that a future dollar is worth less than a present dollar, and that how much less depends on the risk and the alternatives — underlies almost everything in finance:

  • It’s why bonds with longer maturity are more sensitive to interest rates.
  • It’s why high-growth stocks get crushed when rates rise — their value sits far in the future.
  • It’s why a DCF works: a business is a stream of future cash, discounted back to today.
  • It’s why insurance settlements often pay a lump sum smaller than the “face value” spread over time.
  • It’s why lottery winners can take a lump sum or an annuity, and the lump sum is always smaller.
  • It’s why retirement planning is so hard — you have to value cash flows decades away.

If this idea genuinely clicks for you — not just intellectually but viscerally, the way “a dollar today is worth more than tomorrow” feels in your gut — you’re ahead of probably 80% of finance students.

Why valuation exists

A business is, fundamentally, a machine for generating future cash. The value of the business today is the sum of all the future cash it will generate, each one discounted back to today using a rate that reflects how risky those cash flows are. That sentence is the entire DCF. Everything else — multiples, comparables, transactions — is a shortcut or a sanity check on that one idea.

Compounding — deeper than you probably think

Okay, so compounding is the other half of time value of money. Time value says: a dollar today beats a dollar later. Compounding says: by how much, exactly, and over a long enough period, the answer is “a stupid amount.”

Let me show you what I mean.

The basic engine

If you have $1,000 and it grows at 10% per year, after one year you have $1,100. After two years, $1,210. After three years, $1,331. Notice that the gain in year three ($121) is bigger than the gain in year one ($100). Why? Because in year three you’re earning 10% on a larger base. The interest itself is earning interest. That’s the “compounding” part.

Now let’s extend this. After 10 years at 10% per year, $1,000 becomes about $2,594. After 20 years, $6,727. After 30 years, $17,449. After 40 years, $45,259. After 50 years, $117,391.

Look at that last number. The same $1,000 you started with becomes $117,000 if you just leave it alone for 50 years at 10%. You didn’t do anything. Time and the rate did the work.

The Rule of 72

The fastest way to think about compounding in conversation is the Rule of 72. The number of years it takes for your money to double is roughly 72 ÷ (annual rate).

  • At 4%: 72 ÷ 4 = 18 years to double.
  • At 7%: 72 ÷ 7 ≈ 10 years to double.
  • At 10%: 72 ÷ 10 = 7.2 years to double.
  • At 15%: 72 ÷ 15 ≈ 5 years to double.

This is a great mental-math trick to have ready. If a client asks how fast their money grows at a 6% return, you can say “about every 12 years” on the spot without a calculator.

Why this is the most important concept in wealth management

Here’s the wild part. Every single percentage point of return, over a long enough time horizon, becomes massive. Let me show you with two clients.

Client A invests $100,000 for 40 years at 6% per year. Final value: $1,028,572.

Client B invests $100,000 for 40 years at 8% per year. Final value: $2,172,452.

Both clients put in the same $100,000. They had the same time horizon. The only difference was 2 percentage points of annual return. And their ending values differ by more than a million dollars.

This is why fees matter so much to long-term investors. A 1% expense ratio doesn’t sound like much. But over 40 years, that 1% compounded against your portfolio is hundreds of thousands of dollars of lost wealth.

It’s also why behavioral mistakes matter so much. The average investor underperforms the funds they own by about 2% per year due to panic-selling at bottoms and FOMO-buying at tops. That 2% compounded over a career is — you guessed it — hundreds of thousands of dollars of difference.

The advisor’s real job is to capture as much of the available compound return as possible, by keeping costs low, behavior steady, and the plan intact through every market cycle. That’s the entire game.

The negative side — debt compounds too

Compounding works in reverse on debt. A credit card with 20% APR doubles your debt in about 3.5 years if you don’t pay it down. Student loans at 6% double in 12 years. Mortgages at 7% double in 10 years on the interest side alone.

This is why advisors push clients to pay off high-interest debt before investing aggressively. A 20% APR balance is a 20% guaranteed return when you pay it down. That’s better than almost any investment you can find.

The big takeaway

If you really internalize compounding, two things happen. First, you start to see why patience and time horizon are the most valuable assets a young investor has. Second, you start to understand why advisors get protective about things that might seem trivial — a slightly higher fee, a slightly worse behavior, a slightly later start. Each one quietly eats compounding. And quiet eating, over decades, is what destroys wealth.

Annuities, perpetuities, and growing perpetuities — the math you’ll actually use

Once you understand time value of money and compounding, the next layer is the math of regular cash-flow streams. These show up everywhere in finance — bond pricing, mortgage payments, pension valuation, retirement planning, DCF terminal values, dividend valuation.

The annuity formula

An annuity is a stream of equal payments for a fixed number of periods. For example, $1,000 per year for 20 years.

PV of annuity = PMT × [1 − (1+r)−n] / r

Where PMT is the payment per period, r is the discount rate, n is the number of periods.

Worked example — the retirement annuity

A retiree wants to draw $50,000/year for 25 years starting next year. At a 5% discount rate, what’s the lump sum she needs today?

PV = $50,000 × [1 − (1.05)−25] / 0.05 = $50,000 × [1 − 0.2953] / 0.05 = $50,000 × 14.094 = $704,700.

So at 5% expected returns, she needs about $704K today to fund $50K/year for 25 years. This is essentially how pension valuations work.

Worked example — the mortgage payment

You want to borrow $400,000 on a 30-year fixed mortgage at 6.5%. What’s the monthly payment?

Convert: monthly rate = 6.5% / 12 = 0.5417%. Number of periods = 30 × 12 = 360.

Rearranged annuity formula for PMT: PMT = $400,000 × 0.005417 / [1 − 1.005417−360] = approximately $2,528/month.

Total paid over 30 years: $2,528 × 360 = $910,000. So the borrower pays $510,000 of interest on a $400,000 loan. That’s the bite of compound interest on long-duration debt.

The perpetuity formula

A perpetuity is an annuity that lasts forever — equal payments with no end. The math simplifies beautifully:

PV of perpetuity = PMT / r

So $100/year forever at a 5% discount rate is worth $100 / 0.05 = $2,000 today.

The growing perpetuity formula — the heart of the Gordon Growth model

What if the payments grow over time at a constant rate g? Then:

PV of growing perpetuity = PMTyear 1 / (r − g)
