This is the formula behind Gordon Growth terminal value. If a company will generate $100M of FCF next year, growing at 3% forever, with a 10% WACC:

TV = $100 / (0.10 − 0.03) = $100 / 0.07 = $1,429M

Critical: the growth rate g must always be less than r, or the formula blows up to infinity. Economically, you can’t have a perpetuity growing forever above the discount rate — it would represent an asset of infinite value.

The Dividend Discount Model

For a dividend-paying stock with constant dividend growth, the same formula gives an intrinsic value:

Price = Dividendyear 1 / (cost of equity − growth rate)

Example: a stock paying $2/year next year, growing dividends at 4%, with 8% cost of equity: Price = $2 / (0.08 − 0.04) = $50.

If you can buy the stock for less than $50, it’s undervalued by this model. The Dividend Discount Model works best for mature, dividend-paying stocks with stable growth. Less useful for tech growth stocks with no dividends.

Modern Portfolio Theory — the math behind diversification

Harry Markowitz won the Nobel Prize for proving that diversification reduces risk without sacrificing expected return. Here’s the intuition.

The two-asset case

Imagine two assets with these characteristics:

  • Asset A: 10% expected return, 20% standard deviation (volatility).
  • Asset B: 8% expected return, 15% standard deviation.
  • Correlation between A and B: 0.3.

If you put 50% in each, the expected return is 9% (average). But the portfolio’s standard deviation is not the average of 20% and 15%. It’s lower — because the assets don’t move perfectly in sync.

The formula: σport2 = wA2σA2 + wB2σB2 + 2wAwBσAσBρ

For 50/50 with our numbers: σ2 = 0.25(0.04) + 0.25(0.0225) + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.20)(0.15)(0.3) = 0.01 + 0.005625 + 0.0045 = 0.020125.

σ = √0.020125 = 14.2%.

So combining a 20%-vol asset and a 15%-vol asset, weighted 50/50, gives a 14.2% portfolio. Lower than either alone. That’s the diversification benefit.

The efficient frontier

If you plot all possible portfolio combinations of multiple assets on a chart with risk (volatility) on the X-axis and expected return on the Y-axis, you get a curve called the efficient frontier. Portfolios on this curve give you the highest possible expected return for any given level of risk. Portfolios below the curve are inefficient — you can get more return for the same risk, or the same return for less risk.

The point at the top-left of the curve is the “minimum variance portfolio.” The optimal point along the curve, given the risk-free rate, is the “tangency portfolio” (where a line from the risk-free rate just touches the frontier).

The Capital Allocation Line

If you can borrow and lend at the risk-free rate, you can combine the risk-free asset with the tangency portfolio in any proportion. This gives you a straight line on the risk-return chart called the Capital Allocation Line. Every point on this line offers a better risk-return trade-off than any single risky asset alone.

This is why “100% in cash” or “100% in stocks” are almost never optimal. Some mix of cash and the tangency portfolio is almost always better.

The Sharpe ratio — the most-used risk-adjusted metric

Sharpe Ratio = (Portfolio Return − Risk-Free Rate) / Portfolio Standard Deviation

It tells you how much excess return you earned per unit of risk taken. Higher is better.

Rough scale

  • Sharpe < 0.5: poor risk-adjusted performance.
  • Sharpe 0.5 to 1.0: ok.
  • Sharpe 1.0 to 2.0: good. Most reasonable long-term portfolios target around 1.0.
  • Sharpe > 2.0: excellent and rare over long horizons.
  • Sharpe > 3.0: usually a sign of a flawed measurement, leverage hidden somewhere, or a strategy that hasn’t lived through a tail event yet.

Worked example

A portfolio earned 12% with 15% standard deviation in a year when the risk-free rate was 4%. Sharpe = (12 − 4) / 15 = 0.53. Decent but not great.

Another portfolio earned 9% with 8% standard deviation in the same year. Sharpe = (9 − 4) / 8 = 0.625. Lower absolute return but higher risk-adjusted return. By the Sharpe ratio, the second portfolio is “better.”

Variations

Sortino ratio: like Sharpe but only penalizes downside deviation. Says “upside volatility isn’t actually risk; only downside deviation is.” More appealing intuitively but harder to compute.

Treynor ratio: uses beta instead of standard deviation as the risk measure. Useful for evaluating diversified portfolios where systematic risk dominates.

Information ratio: excess return over a benchmark, divided by tracking error. Measures the consistency of an active manager’s outperformance.

Options basics — what every advisor should know

You don’t need to be an options trader. But options come up constantly in WM — for hedging concentrated stock positions, for employee stock options, for income generation through covered calls. Here’s the deep version.

Call options

A call option gives you the right (not the obligation) to buy a stock at a specified price (the “strike price”) on or before a specified date (the “expiration”).

Example: Apple is trading at $180. You buy a call option with a $190 strike, expiring in 3 months, for a premium of $5.

  • If Apple stays below $190 at expiration, the option expires worthless. You lose your $5.
  • If Apple goes to $200, you can exercise the option, buy at $190, sell at $200, earning $10. After the $5 premium, your net is $5 profit per share.
  • If Apple goes to $250, you earn $250 − $190 − $5 = $55 per share. The upside is unbounded.

Put options

A put option gives you the right to sell a stock at a specified price by a specified date. Used for downside protection.

Example: you own 1,000 shares of Apple at $180. You buy a put with $170 strike expiring in 3 months for $4 premium.

  • If Apple stays above $170, the put expires worthless. You lose your $4 (per share).
  • If Apple falls to $150, the put is worth $170 − $150 = $20. Net of premium, $16 of gain. This offsets some of your stock loss.
  • The put effectively gives your shares a floor of $170 minus the premium = $166.

The collar — the most common WM use of options

An options collar combines buying a put with selling a call, often structured at zero cost.

Example: a tech executive has 10,000 shares of her company at $50. She buys $45-strike puts and sells $55-strike calls.

  • If stock falls below $45, the put protects her at $45.
  • If stock rises above $55, the call she sold gets exercised — she sells at $55.
  • If stock stays between $45 and $55, both options expire worthless and she keeps her shares.

The collar locks her into a $45–$55 range. She gives up upside above $55 to get downside protection below $45. Often the premiums offset so it costs nothing upfront.

Covered calls — the income-generating strategy

If you own a stock and sell call options against it, you collect option premiums as income. You give up some upside, but generate cash.

Example: you own 1,000 shares of Apple at $180. You sell call options at $200 strike expiring in 30 days for $3 premium each. You collect $3,000 in premium.

  • If Apple stays below $200, options expire worthless. You keep the $3,000 and the shares.
  • If Apple rises above $200, your shares get called away at $200. You miss the upside above $200 but collected the $3,000 premium plus $20/share of gain to $200.

Used by income-focused investors to generate cash flow from buy-and-hold positions. Many WM advisors run a small covered-call sleeve for income-oriented clients.

The Greeks — how options actually move

  • Delta: how much the option price moves for a $1 move in the underlying. Range: 0 to 1 for calls, −1 to 0 for puts.
  • Gamma: how much delta changes for a $1 move in the underlying. Highest for at-the-money options.
  • Theta: time decay. How much the option loses per day as expiration approaches. Always negative for option buyers.
  • Vega: sensitivity to implied volatility. Higher volatility = more valuable options.
  • Rho: sensitivity to interest rates. Usually small for short-dated options.

Equity compensation deep dive — what tech executive clients actually hold

WM advisors who serve tech executives spend significant time on equity compensation. Here’s the alphabet soup explained.

RSUs (Restricted Stock Units)

Promises to deliver shares at a future date when vesting conditions are met. Typical vesting: 4 years with a 1-year cliff (no shares vest for the first year, then 25% vest, then monthly or quarterly thereafter).

Tax treatment: at vesting, the fair market value of the shares is ordinary income to the employee. Income and FICA tax owed at vesting. If she holds the shares and they appreciate further, additional gain is capital gain. If they depreciate, she could end up paying tax on more value than she ultimately keeps.

WM strategy: most advisors recommend selling RSU shares at vesting and diversifying. The rationale: she’s already concentrated in the company’s stock through future RSU grants and her job. Holding vested shares compounds that concentration.

ISOs (Incentive Stock Options)

Tax-advantaged options. You get the right to buy stock at a fixed strike price (set at the grant date, usually fair market value at the time).

Tax treatment: at exercise, no regular tax is owed if you hold the shares. But the “spread” between strike and current price is added to your Alternative Minimum Tax calculation, which can trigger AMT. If you hold the shares for 1 year after exercise and 2 years after grant, any gain at sale is long-term capital gain.

The AMT trap: many tech employees in the dot-com bubble exercised ISOs, didn’t sell, and the stock crashed. They owed AMT on a paper gain that vanished. WM advisors specifically guard against this.

NSOs (Non-qualified Stock Options)

Like ISOs but without the favorable tax treatment. At exercise, the spread is taxed as ordinary income immediately, plus FICA. Subsequent appreciation is capital gain.

NSOs are simpler than ISOs from a tax-planning perspective, though they generate higher current-year tax.

ESPP (Employee Stock Purchase Plan)

Lets employees buy company stock at a discount (typically 15%) through payroll deductions. The discount is essentially free money (plus a small risk of holding-period requirements).

Qualified ESPPs have favorable tax treatment if held for required periods. Most advisors recommend always maxing out the ESPP if available.

Founder’s shares / early-stage equity

For startup founders and very-early employees, the shares were granted with a very low strike price or cost basis. The lifetime gain potential is enormous. The tax planning around these — 83(b) elections, QSBS (qualified small business stock for the section 1202 exclusion that can shield up to $10M of gain), early exercise of options — is one of the most-valuable services a sophisticated advisor can provide to early-stage tech clients.

Tax brackets and how they actually work

Most people fundamentally misunderstand US federal income tax. Let me clear it up.

The marginal rate vs the average rate

The US uses a progressive tax system. Income is taxed in brackets. A higher bracket only applies to the income above the bracket threshold, not to all your income.

For 2024 single filers, the brackets are approximately:

  • 10% on first $11,600
  • 12% on $11,600 to $47,150
  • 22% on $47,150 to $100,525
  • 24% on $100,525 to $191,950
  • 32% on $191,950 to $243,725
  • 35% on $243,725 to $609,350
  • 37% on $609,350+

Worked example

Someone earning $200K of taxable income:

  • First $11,600 × 10% = $1,160
  • Next $35,550 (to $47,150) × 12% = $4,266
  • Next $53,375 (to $100,525) × 22% = $11,742
  • Next $91,425 (to $191,950) × 24% = $21,942
  • Last $8,050 (to $200K) × 32% = $2,576
  • Total federal income tax: $41,686

Marginal rate: 32% (the rate on the next dollar earned). Average (effective) rate: 41,686 / 200,000 = 20.8%.

The marginal rate is what matters for planning decisions (Roth conversions, tax-loss harvesting, charitable contributions). The average rate is what you actually paid as a share of income.

Long-term capital gains and qualified dividends

Taxed at lower rates than ordinary income:

  • 0% for income up to ~$47K single / ~$94K joint
  • 15% for income up to ~$518K single / ~$583K joint
  • 20% above those thresholds

Plus the 3.8% Net Investment Income Tax for income over $200K single / $250K joint.

State income tax

On top of federal. Highest states: California (up to 13.3%), New York (up to 10.9%), New Jersey (up to 10.75%), Hawaii. No state income tax: Texas, Florida, Washington, Nevada, Tennessee, Wyoming, Alaska, South Dakota, New Hampshire (no wage tax).

For a high earner in California, the combined federal + state marginal rate can exceed 50%. This drives a lot of muni-bond investing, charitable strategies, and the “California exit” phenomenon.

The Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)

The AMT is a parallel tax calculation that limits certain deductions and exemptions to ensure high earners pay at least a minimum tax. It was created in 1969 to catch wealthy taxpayers who used loopholes to pay nothing.

How it works in practice

You calculate your regular tax. Then you calculate your AMT — with some deductions disallowed, ISO exercise spreads added in, and an AMT exemption applied. You owe whichever is higher.

Most middle-class taxpayers don’t hit AMT. But ISO exercises, large state-tax deductions in high-tax states, and certain other items can trigger it. WM advisors with tech-executive clients run AMT projections constantly.

The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

The TCJA reduced AMT exposure significantly — the exemption was raised, the phase-out threshold was raised, and the state-and-local-tax (SALT) deduction was capped at $10K. These provisions are scheduled to sunset at the end of 2025, so AMT could re-expand for many taxpayers in 2026 absent congressional action.

Three big-picture valuation methods

There are essentially three ways professionals value businesses, and almost every interview will ask you about all three.

Intrinsic
Discounted Cash Flow (DCF). Build out future cash flows; discount them. The most theoretically correct, the most assumption-heavy.
Relative
Comparable companies (“trading comps”). Use multiples from similar public companies as a benchmark.
Transaction
Precedent transactions. Use multiples from past M&A deals on similar companies.

Real-world valuation uses all three in combination. A DCF tells you what you think the business is intrinsically worth; trading comps tell you what the market is paying for similar businesses; precedent transactions tell you what acquirers have actually paid. Triangulating across the three gives you a defensible range.

Enterprise Value vs. Equity Value — nail this distinction

This is the trap question interviewers love because most candidates blur the two.

Equity Value (also called market capitalization for a public company) = price per share × diluted shares outstanding. It’s the value of the business that belongs to shareholders.

Enterprise Value = Equity Value + Debt − Cash + Preferred Stock + Minority Interest. It’s the value of the operating business to all capital providers — equity holders, debt holders, preferred holders — combined.

Why subtract cash? Because cash isn’t part of the operating business; it’s a separate asset. If you buy the company, you get the cash back, so effectively you’re only paying for everything except the cash.

Why add debt? Because if you buy the equity, you have to either pay off the debt or assume responsibility for it. Either way, the “true” cost of buying the business is the equity price plus the debt you take on.

The intuition

Imagine buying a house worth $500K with a $300K mortgage. If you pay the seller $200K in cash and assume the mortgage, your equity outlay was $200K but the “enterprise value” of the house is $500K. Buying a company is the same.

Multiples — quick valuation shorthand

A multiple is just one financial metric divided by another. They’re useful because they let you compare valuations across companies of different sizes.

P/E (Price-to-Earnings)

Stock price divided by earnings per share. Equity-side multiple. Sensitive to capital structure (because earnings are after interest) and to non-recurring items. Easy to compute, easy to manipulate.

EV/EBITDA

Enterprise value divided by EBITDA. The most-used valuation multiple in finance. EV/EBITDA is capital-structure neutral (numerator and denominator both ignore debt vs. equity mix) and largely accounting-neutral on D&A. Useful for comparing companies with different leverage or different capex patterns.

EV/Sales

Used for companies that don’t have stable EBITDA yet — high-growth tech, biotech, etc. Less precise but more robust to early-stage volatility.

P/B (Price-to-Book)

Market cap divided by book value of equity. Used most heavily for banks, where assets are mostly financial and marked to market or to amortized cost. Less relevant for asset-light businesses.

Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), built step by step

Okay, this is the most-asked technical question in finance interviews, especially for IB and ER but also for serious WM seats. So let’s really build this from the ground up — not as a formula to memorize, but as a process that actually makes sense.

The big idea, before any math

Imagine you’re thinking about buying a small business — let’s say a laundromat for sale on Craigslist. The owner is asking $300,000. Should you pay it?

The right question isn’t “is $300K a lot of money?” The right question is: how much cash will this laundromat generate, year after year, for as long as you own it? And what would I have to put in the bank today, at a reasonable interest rate, to generate that same stream of cash?

Say the laundromat clears $50K of free cash per year and you expect it to grow modestly. If you can earn 8% somewhere else at similar risk, then $300K invested elsewhere would also generate around $24K per year. The laundromat’s $50K is much better than $24K. So $300K is probably a steal.

That’s the entire intuition of a DCF. The value of a business is the sum of its future cash flows, discounted back to today at a rate that reflects the risk. No more, no less.

Every line item in the formal model is just a careful version of this intuition. Don’t lose sight of it. When you walk through a DCF in an interview, you should sound like someone explaining a laundromat, not someone reciting a CFA textbook.

The four steps, before we go into each one

  1. Forecast the company’s cash flows for some explicit period (usually 5–10 years).
  2. Calculate a terminal value — an estimate of all cash flows beyond the forecast period.
  3. Discount both the explicit cash flows and the terminal value back to today at the company’s WACC.
  4. Sum everything to get enterprise value, then bridge to equity value to get per-share intrinsic value.

That’s the whole skeleton. Now let’s flesh it out.

Step 1 — Project free cash flow

Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF), also called unlevered free cash flow, is the cash the business generates before paying interest. The standard build:

  1. EBIT
  2. × (1 − tax rate) = NOPAT (Net Operating Profit After Tax)
  3. + Depreciation & Amortization (add back non-cash)
  4. − Capital expenditures
  5. − Change in working capital
  6. = Free Cash Flow to the Firm

Project FCFF for typically 5–10 years, year by year. Then we need a value for everything beyond that horizon, which leads to…

Step 2 — Terminal value

Almost all of a company’s value sits beyond the explicit forecast window. To capture it, you compute a terminal value: the value of all cash flows past year 10 as of year 10. Two common methods:

  • Gordon Growth / Perpetuity Growth Method: TV = (FCFyear 11) ÷ (WACC − g), where g is the perpetual growth rate (usually around long-term GDP, 2–3%).
  • Exit Multiple Method: TV = EBITDAyear 10 × assumed exit multiple. Tied more directly to market behavior.

Then you discount that terminal value back to today.

Step 3 — Discount everything back to today using WACC

Okay, let’s slow down on this one because most students treat WACC as a magic formula instead of really understanding it.

The intuition first

Imagine you’re running a company. You need money to operate. You can get money in two ways:

  • Borrow it — issue bonds, take a bank loan. You promise to pay interest, and you get tax savings on the interest (the “tax shield”).
  • Raise equity — sell shares of the company. You don’t owe interest payments, but you give up a piece of future profits forever.

Now, both sources of money cost something. The debt costs interest. The equity costs the return shareholders expect to earn on their investment. Neither is free.

WACC is just the average cost of these two sources, weighted by how much of each the company uses. If a company is 60% equity-financed and 40% debt-financed, and equity costs 9% and debt costs 5%, then WACC roughly equals 60%×9% + 40%×5% = 7.4%.

That number — the “cost of capital” — is the hurdle rate for the company. Any investment they make has to earn more than 7.4% to actually create value for the firm. Anything less and they’re destroying value.

And critically, that’s also why WACC is the discount rate in a DCF. When you’re trying to value the cash flows the company generates, you discount them at the cost of capital. Because that’s what those cash flows are competing against in the eyes of the firm.

The formula, expanded

WACC = (E/V) × re + (D/V) × rd × (1 − t)

Where:

  • E: market value of equity (share price times shares outstanding).
  • D: market value of debt (close to book value of debt for most companies).
  • V: E + D (total capital).
  • re: cost of equity. Usually estimated with CAPM — we’ll get to that.
  • rd: cost of debt — the yield the company pays on its debt.
  • t: corporate tax rate. We multiply the debt cost by (1 − t) because interest expense is tax-deductible, so the “effective” cost of debt is lower than the headline rate.

Cost of equity, slowly

The cost of equity is what shareholders expect to earn from holding the stock, given the risk. Way more abstract than the cost of debt (because nobody’s issuing you a contract specifying it). The standard approach is the Capital Asset Pricing Model, CAPM:

re = risk-free rate + beta × equity risk premium

In English: equity investors expect (1) some baseline return that they could get for free (the risk-free rate, usually the 10-year Treasury yield), plus (2) extra compensation for taking on market risk (the equity risk premium, usually 5–7%), scaled by (3) how much this specific stock moves relative to the market (beta).

If beta = 1, the stock is as volatile as the market. A risk-free rate of 4% and an ERP of 6% would give re = 10%. If beta is 1.5 (a more volatile stock, like a high-growth tech name), then re = 4% + 1.5×6% = 13%. If beta is 0.7 (a utility, low volatility), then re = 4% + 0.7×6% = 8.2%.

The honest caveat: CAPM is a useful first pass but it’s far from perfect. Beta moves around depending on the period you measure. The ERP is a contested number. Many practitioners adjust the output upward for small-cap risk, country risk, or company-specific factors. It’s a starting point, not the final answer.

Cost of debt, slowly

The cost of debt is easier — just look at the yield on the company’s outstanding debt. If a company’s bonds trade at a 6% yield, that’s their cost of debt before tax. After tax, at a 25% corporate rate, the effective cost is 6% × (1 − 0.25) = 4.5%.

The reason we adjust for tax: interest payments are deductible. So every dollar of interest expense saves the company 25 cents (at a 25% rate) in tax. The economic cost of borrowing isn’t the full 6% — it’s 4.5%. This is the famous “tax shield” that makes debt structurally cheaper than equity.

Weights — market value, not book value

One subtle point that interviewers test: when you compute the weights of equity and debt in the WACC formula, you use market values, not book values. The market value of equity is the current share price times shares outstanding. The market value of debt is roughly close to book value for most companies but can diverge for distressed debt.

A worked example

Take a real-ish company: $80B market cap of equity, $20B in debt. Risk-free rate 4%, beta 1.1, equity risk premium 6%. Cost of debt 5%. Tax rate 25%.

  • Cost of equity: 4% + 1.1 × 6% = 10.6%.
  • After-tax cost of debt: 5% × (1 − 0.25) = 3.75%.
  • Equity weight: $80B / $100B = 80%.
  • Debt weight: $20B / $100B = 20%.
  • WACC: 0.8 × 10.6% + 0.2 × 3.75% = 8.48% + 0.75% = 9.23%.

That 9.23% is the rate we’d use to discount this company’s future cash flows in a DCF.

What changes WACC

This matters because in a DCF, small changes in WACC produce big changes in valuation. The things that move WACC:

  • Interest rates rise → risk-free rate rises → cost of equity rises → WACC rises → DCF value falls.
  • Beta rises (the stock becomes more volatile) → cost of equity rises → WACC rises → DCF value falls.
  • Credit spreads widen → cost of debt rises → WACC rises → DCF value falls.
  • Capital structure shifts toward equity (more equity, less debt) → you’re weighting the more expensive source more → WACC tends to rise.
  • Tax rates fall → the tax shield on debt shrinks → effective cost of debt rises → WACC rises slightly.

This is also why the “higher for longer” rates environment of 2022–2024 was so painful for growth stocks — their WACCs spiked and their long-dated cash flows got discounted much more heavily, compressing their valuation multiples.

Step 4 — Sum and bridge to equity value

Sum the discounted FCFs and the discounted terminal value. You get the enterprise value. To go from enterprise value to equity value (price per share), subtract debt and preferred and minority interest, add cash. Divide by diluted shares outstanding to get implied per-share value. Compare to current share price.

Why a DCF is both powerful and dangerous

A DCF is powerful because it forces you to think about a business from first principles — what does it actually generate, what could change, what is the risk. It’s dangerous because tiny tweaks to growth rate, terminal multiple, or discount rate produce wildly different valuations. Hence the joke: “Build a DCF, get the number you want.” In real life, always do a sensitivity analysis — show the valuation under a range of WACCs and growth rates — rather than presenting a single point estimate.

Comparable companies analysis (“trading comps”) — the deep version

The big idea

Comparable companies analysis says: similar companies should trade at similar multiples. If we know how a peer group of public companies is being valued by the market, we can apply those multiples to our target company’s financials to get an implied valuation.

It’s essentially saying “the market is pricing these similar businesses at X times EBITDA. Our target should be priced at roughly X times its EBITDA too, with some adjustments for differences.”

The actual process, step by step

Step 1: Pick the peer set. This is the most important and most subjective decision. You want public companies that are similar to your target across:

  • Industry (same end-markets, similar business model).
  • Size (within a reasonable revenue and market-cap range).
  • Growth profile (similar revenue growth rates).
  • Margin profile (similar gross and operating margins).
  • Geographic mix.
  • Capital intensity.

A good comp set is 5 to 12 companies. Too few and the median is noisy. Too many and you’ve diluted the analysis with weaker comparisons.

Step 2: Pull the financials and multiples. For each peer, you need market cap, debt, cash, and minority interest to calculate Enterprise Value. Then you need EBITDA, revenue, EPS, and free cash flow to calculate the multiples. Look at both the last 12 months (LTM) and next 12 months (NTM) versions.

Step 3: Choose which multiples matter. For most operating businesses, the standard multiples are EV/EBITDA and P/E. For high-growth or unprofitable companies, EV/Revenue may be more relevant. For banks, P/B. For REITs, P/FFO. The right choice depends on the industry.

Step 4: Compute the median or mean across the peer set and apply it to your target. For example, if the peer median EV/EBITDA is 10x and your target’s EBITDA is $100M, the implied EV is $1B. Then subtract net debt to bridge to equity value.

Step 5: Sanity check. Compare your implied valuation against the company’s actual trading price (if public) or against precedent transactions and DCF (if private). If your comps give you $20 per share but the stock trades at $50, something’s off — either your comp set is wrong, the market knows something you don’t, or the target genuinely deserves a premium.

What makes comps strong or weak

Strong comps happen when:

  • The peer set is genuinely similar across the dimensions that matter.
  • The market is normal — not in a bubble or panic.
  • The multiples are stable across the peers (low standard deviation around the median).

Weak comps happen when:

  • You had to stretch the definition of “peer” to fill out the list (e.g., a small specialty retailer compared to Walmart because there’s nothing closer).
  • The market is in an extreme moment (multiples got crushed in 2008; were inflated in 1999).
  • The peers have very different growth rates or margins from your target.

The judgment piece — why valuations have ranges

In practice, you’ll often see comps give a wide range. The median might be 10x EV/EBITDA, but the range across the peer set might be 7x to 14x. So is your target a 7x or a 14x? That’s judgment.

Considerations that push toward the high end of the range:

  • Faster growth than peers.
  • Higher margins than peers.
  • Better balance sheet.
  • Stronger management.
  • Better strategic positioning.

Considerations that push toward the low end:

  • Slower growth.
  • Lower margins.
  • More leverage.
  • Strategic risks (regulatory, competitive, technological).

So a comps-based valuation isn’t a single number — it’s a thoughtful range with reasoning.

When comps work well vs. badly

Comps work well for mature, established industries with many similar players and stable economics. Software, restaurants, retail, financial services, consumer products.

Comps work badly for unique or transformative companies. Tesla, when it was first growing, had no clear comps. Amazon for years had no profitable e-commerce comps. Companies in transitioning industries (utilities going through decarbonization, traditional media facing streaming) are hard to comp.

In those cases, you lean more on DCF or on industry-specific frameworks rather than comps.

Precedent transactions — the deep version

The big idea

Precedent transactions analysis is the same idea as comps, but using past M&A deals instead of current public-market trading.

The reasoning: when one company buys another, the price paid reveals what an actual acquirer was willing to spend. If you’re valuing a private company that might be sold, or trying to figure out what a strategic buyer would pay for a public company, looking at recent transactions in the same space is informative.

Why precedent multiples are usually higher than trading multiples

Two reasons.

Control premium. When you buy 100% of a company, you can change strategy, replace management, integrate operations, capture synergies. That ability to control is worth something. The standard control premium runs 20 to 40% above the public-market trading price.

Synergy value. A strategic acquirer often captures cost synergies (consolidating overhead, closing duplicate offices) or revenue synergies (cross-selling, geographic expansion) that a passive investor can’t. So the acquirer can rationally pay more for a company than its current trading price.

How to actually pull precedent transactions

Step 1: Build a list of comparable deals. Use S&P Capital IQ, FactSet, Refinitiv, or similar databases. Filter for industry, deal size, geography, and time period (usually the last 3 to 5 years — older deals get stale).

Step 2: Pull the deal values and target financials. For each transaction, you need the announcement price, the deal’s implied EV, and the target’s financial metrics in the period before announcement (LTM EBITDA, LTM revenue).

Step 3: Compute the deal multiples. EV/EBITDA, EV/Revenue, P/E. Compare the spread across deals.

Step 4: Apply to your target. Same as comps — multiply your target’s metrics by the precedent multiple to get an implied valuation. Adjust for differences.

The honest limitations of precedent transactions

Three big caveats:

  • Deals are situational. A merger in 2021 happened in a different macro environment than a merger in 2009. The implied multiples reflect the era’s sentiment, not necessarily timeless value.
  • Synergies vary wildly. Each deal had its own synergy potential that was specific to the acquirer. Applying a 14x EV/EBITDA multiple from a deal with big synergies to a target with no obvious synergy source overstates the value.
  • Stale data. M&A deals are infrequent in most industries, so the “recent precedent” list might include 5-year-old deals that aren’t really relevant to today.

When precedent transactions are most useful

When you’re actually thinking about whether to sell a company, or trying to estimate what a strategic acquirer would pay. They’re less useful when you’re just trying to figure out what a public company is worth on the open market — trading comps are the more direct comparison for that.

In practice, banker valuation decks usually include both trading comps and precedent transactions side by side, so the client can see both the “market view” and the “control view.” The two together bracket the answer.

Accretion / dilution analysis

When one public company buys another, the deal is described as “accretive” if it raises the acquirer’s EPS and “dilutive” if it lowers EPS. The key drivers:

  • Purchase method: cash, stock, or a mix.
  • Yield comparison: the acquirer’s P/E versus the target’s, and the cost of any new debt or new shares used.
  • Synergies: cost savings or revenue gains the combined business expects to realize.

Rough rule of thumb in interviews: stock deals tend to be dilutive when the acquirer’s P/E is below the target’s; cash deals tend to be accretive when the after-tax cost of debt is below the inverse of the target’s P/E.

LBO basics

A leveraged buyout is when a financial sponsor (a private equity firm) buys a company using a lot of borrowed money. The math relies on the company generating enough cash to service the debt while the sponsor pays down debt over the holding period and eventually sells the company at a higher value.

Key levers of an LBO return:

  • Multiple expansion: selling at a higher EBITDA multiple than you bought at.
  • EBITDA growth: improving the underlying business.
  • Debt paydown: using free cash flow to pay down debt, increasing the equity portion of enterprise value over time.
  • Multiple of money invested (MOIC) and IRR are the standard return metrics.

The football field — how bankers visualize valuation

In a real M&A or capital-markets process, no single valuation method produces “the answer.” Instead, bankers produce a football field — a horizontal bar chart where each row is a valuation methodology and each bar shows the range that method produces. The client sees, at a glance, that:

  • The DCF (base case) implies a valuation of $X billion.
  • Trading comps imply $Y billion.
  • Precedent transactions imply $Z billion.
  • An LBO floor (what a PE buyer could pay) sits at $W billion.
  • 52-week trading range and analyst price targets bracket the public-market view.

The art is in the overlap. When several independent methods converge on a similar range, the banker has a defensible answer. When they diverge widely, the story has to explain why. A high DCF with low comps usually means the market is skeptical of the projections; a high precedent transactions number with low trading comps usually means historical deals carried unusual synergies.

A worked LBO example, step by step

Let’s walk through an LBO that even a freshman can follow. A PE firm wants to buy a company called Bramble Industries, a stable, slow-growing maker of industrial fasteners. The deal:

  • Year 0 EBITDA: $100M.
  • Purchase multiple: 8.0x EBITDA. So enterprise value at entry = $800M.
  • Debt: 5.5x EBITDA = $550M. The sponsor finances the rest with $250M of equity.
  • EBITDA grows at 5% per year for 5 years.
  • Free cash flow each year after interest and capex is used to pay down debt.
  • Exit multiple: 8.0x (same as entry). At the end of year 5, EBITDA = $100M × 1.055 ≈ $128M. Exit EV = $1.02B.
  • By year 5, debt has been paid down to roughly $300M (because each year’s FCF was about $50M, accumulated over 5 years).
  • Equity at exit = $1.02B − $300M = $720M.

The math on returns:

  • Equity invested: $250M.
  • Equity returned: $720M.
  • Multiple of money (MoM): $720M / $250M = 2.88x.
  • Annualized IRR: approximately 24% per year.
The three drivers of LBO return
  1. EBITDA growth: a bigger EBITDA at exit, valued at the same multiple, means more enterprise value.
  2. Multiple expansion: if the exit multiple exceeds the entry multiple, you get a free boost. The reverse is brutal — this is why “multiple compression” is the great fear of LBO sponsors.
  3. Debt paydown: each dollar of debt repaid is a dollar of equity created. This is the leverage magic, and it’s where the “L” in LBO does most of its work.

What can break the deal: a recession that crushes EBITDA, a refinancing problem that forces an unfavorable debt restructuring, an unexpected capex requirement that absorbs cash that would otherwise pay down debt, or multiple compression at exit. The reason PE firms target stable, recurring-revenue, asset-light businesses with strong cash conversion is precisely that those businesses are the most reliable repayers of debt.

Private-company valuation — the haircuts everyone forgets

Public companies have continuous price discovery. Private companies don’t, so valuation involves adjustments that aren’t needed for listed comps:

  • Illiquidity discount: typically 20–35%. You can’t sell a private-company stake instantly without major price concessions.
  • Minority-interest discount: if you’re valuing a minority stake without control rights, you usually mark it down further.
  • Control premium: conversely, when valuing a majority stake or full takeover, you add a control premium reflecting the buyer’s ability to direct strategy and capture synergies. Historically 20–40% over the public-market trading price.
  • Small-cap discount: smaller companies generally trade at lower multiples because they’re less diversified, less liquid, and have higher idiosyncratic risk.

Multiples in detail — how to actually use them

You learned the basic multiples. Here’s how a banker actually thinks about them.

Choosing the right multiple for the right business

  • Mature businesses with steady earnings: P/E and EV/EBITDA.
  • Capital-intensive businesses: EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT (EBITDA can flatter cap-intensive businesses).
  • Asset-light, growing: EV/Revenue can be useful early; once profitable, transition to EV/EBITDA.
  • Pre-profit tech / SaaS: EV/ARR (annual recurring revenue), EV/Revenue, or Rule of 40 (growth + free cash flow margin > 40%).
  • Banks: P/B (price-to-book) and P/TBV (price-to-tangible-book). Returns on book equity drive the multiple.
  • REITs: P/FFO (price-to-funds-from-operations) instead of P/E, because real-estate accounting is unusual on depreciation.
  • Insurance: P/B, ROE-driven.
  • Commodities / cyclicals: use mid-cycle earnings or normalized EBITDA; a trailing P/E at a peak or trough is misleading.

What to adjust for

  • Non-recurring items: restructuring charges, asset write-downs, legal settlements. Strip them out.
  • Capital structure differences: be careful comparing levered P/E across companies with very different leverage.
  • Accounting differences: especially across geographies (IFRS vs GAAP) and depreciation policies.
  • Fiscal year-end timing: align periods so you’re comparing the same window.

Sensitivity, scenario, and Monte Carlo — how to honor uncertainty

A single-point valuation is almost always wrong. The mature output is a range, calibrated to assumptions.

  • Sensitivity tables: show how the valuation moves as you change one variable at a time (e.g., WACC and terminal growth in a 2D grid).
  • Scenario analysis: build “bull,” “base,” and “bear” cases with internally-consistent assumption sets, then evaluate each.
  • Monte Carlo simulation: assign probability distributions to each input, run thousands of trials, and output a distribution of valuations. Useful for highly uncertain businesses; rarely used in practice for routine equities because it can create false precision.

Common DCF mistakes — the ones that get freshmen in trouble

  • Using book debt instead of market debt in WACC weights. Use market values.
  • Forgetting the tax shield on debt. After-tax cost of debt = pre-tax rate × (1 − tax rate).
  • Picking a terminal growth rate higher than long-run GDP. The business cannot, in perpetuity, grow faster than the economy it lives in.
  • Inconsistent treatment of inflation. Project cash flows in nominal terms and discount at a nominal rate, or do everything real. Don’t mix.
  • Mid-year vs end-of-year discounting. Most cash flows arrive throughout the year, not at year-end. The mid-year convention adjusts.
  • Forgetting to subtract net debt to get to equity value. The DCF gives you enterprise value; you have to bridge to equity.
  • Forgetting minority interest and preferred stock. Both are claims senior to common equity and have to be subtracted.
  • Using historical beta for a company that’s structurally changed. A company that just acquired a different-risk business has a different beta now than its trailing-5-year regression suggests.

Sum-of-the-parts — valuing conglomerates correctly

Some companies operate multiple distinct businesses with very different economics. Valuing them as a single entity at a single multiple misses the structure. Sum-of-the-parts (SOTP) values each segment separately, using the right comp set and multiple for each, then sums to a total enterprise value.

SOTP often reveals a “conglomerate discount” — the market values the whole at less than the sum of the parts — which is why activists frequently push for spin-offs and breakups.

CAPM, beta, and the cost of equity — the slow walk-through

The cost of equity is what shareholders expect to earn from holding the stock. It’s the single most subjective input in a DCF, so we use the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) as a disciplined starting point.

re = Rf + β × ERP

Three inputs. Let’s do each one carefully.

The risk-free rate (Rf)

The baseline return you can earn with essentially zero risk. In the US, we use the yield on a long-dated Treasury bond, typically the 10-year. (For very long-duration projects, some practitioners use the 20-year or 30-year.)

Why the 10-year? Two reasons. One, it matches the typical “long-term” forecast horizon in a DCF. Two, it’s the most liquid and observable long-term Treasury rate.

As of recent years, the 10-year Treasury has been roughly 4–5%. In 2020, it was below 1%. The value moves over time, so always use a current number when building a fresh DCF.

The equity risk premium (ERP)

The extra return that investors demand for taking on market risk over the risk-free rate. It’s the expected return on the broad equity market minus the risk-free rate.

Estimating it is famously contested. Three common approaches:

  • Historical method: look at the average actual ERP over a long period — say, 1926 to today. Comes out around 6–7%.
  • Implied method: back out what ERP the current S&P 500 price implies given expected earnings. Aswath Damodaran publishes this monthly — usually 4–6% recently.
  • Survey method: ask CFOs and investors what they expect. Tends to produce 5–7%.

Most analysts use somewhere in the 5–7% range. For interview purposes, defaulting to 6% is reasonable, but be ready to defend the choice.

Beta (β)

How sensitive a stock’s returns are to the broader market. A beta of 1 means the stock moves with the market on average. Beta of 1.5 means the stock moves 1.5x the market — up 15% on a 10% up day, down 15% on a 10% down day. Beta of 0.7 dampens market moves.

For a public company, you can pull beta from Bloomberg, FactSet, Yahoo Finance, or compute it yourself by regressing the stock’s returns against the index returns over a 2–5 year window.

For a private company — or one whose past beta isn’t informative because the business has changed — you use the “comparable companies” approach.

Unlevered beta and the relevering math

This is technical but worth understanding for an interview. Beta as observed in the market reflects two things: the underlying business risk, and the financial risk from leverage. Two otherwise-identical companies, one with no debt and one with a lot of debt, will have very different betas.

To compare them fairly, we “unlever” the observed beta — stripping out the leverage effect — to get a measure of pure business risk. Then we “relever” that unlevered beta for the target company’s specific capital structure.

The formulas (you should memorize these):

βunlevered = βlevered ÷ [1 + (1 − t) × (D/E)]

βrelevered = βunlevered × [1 + (1 − t) × (D/E)target]

Where t is the tax rate and D/E is the debt-to-equity ratio at market values.

Worked example

Imagine you want to value a private grocery chain. There are no good public comps for a private mid-size grocery, but there are large public peers (Kroger, Albertsons). The peers have:

  • Observed (levered) beta: 0.6
  • Debt-to-equity (market): 1.0
  • Tax rate: 25%

Unlever: 0.6 ÷ [1 + 0.75 × 1.0] = 0.6 ÷ 1.75 = 0.343 (unlevered).

Your target’s D/E is 0.5 (less leveraged than the comps). Relever: 0.343 × [1 + 0.75 × 0.5] = 0.343 × 1.375 = 0.471 (target levered).

So if you used 0.6 directly, you’d be overstating the target’s beta (because you’d be embedding the peers’ higher leverage). The proper levered beta is 0.471.

Cost of equity for the target: 4% (Rf) + 0.471 × 6% (ERP) = 6.83%.

A full worked DCF — with actual numbers

Most freshmen learn DCF as a recipe without ever seeing the math run end to end. Let’s actually do one.

Imagine a mid-size SaaS company called Atlas. Here’s the setup:

  • Year 0 (current) revenue: $500M
  • Revenue growth: 20% in year 1, declining by 2pts per year (Y1: 20%, Y2: 18%, ... Y5: 12%)
  • EBIT margin: 20% (constant for simplicity)
  • Tax rate: 25%
  • D&A: ~5% of revenue each year
  • Capex: ~5% of revenue (assume in steady state these roughly offset)
  • Change in working capital: ~2% of incremental revenue
  • WACC: 10%
  • Terminal growth rate: 3%

Step 1: Project revenue

YearGrowthRevenue
0—$500M
120%$600M
218%$708M
316%$821M
414%$936M
512%$1,048M

Step 2: Calculate EBIT, taxes, NOPAT, and free cash flow

YearRevenueEBIT (20%)NOPAT (after 25% tax)+D&A (5%)−Capex (5%)−ΔWC (2% ΔRev)FCF
1$600$120$90$30$30$2$88
2$708$141.6$106.2$35.4$35.4$2.2$104
3$821$164.2$123.2$41.1$41.1$2.3$120.9
4$936$187.2$140.4$46.8$46.8$2.3$138.1
5$1,048$209.6$157.2$52.4$52.4$2.2$155

Step 3: Discount each FCF to present value at 10% WACC

YearFCFDiscount factor (1/(1.10)n)PV of FCF
1$880.909$80.0
2$1040.826$85.9
3$120.90.751$90.8
4$138.10.683$94.3
5$1550.621$96.3

Sum of PV of explicit FCF: $80 + $85.9 + $90.8 + $94.3 + $96.3 = $447.3M

Step 4: Terminal value (using Gordon growth)

Year 6 FCF (projecting forward): $155 × 1.03 = $159.65M.

TV at end of Year 5 = $159.65 / (0.10 − 0.03) = $159.65 / 0.07 = $2,280.7M.

PV of TV = $2,280.7 × 0.621 = $1,416.3M.

Step 5: Sum to enterprise value, then bridge to equity

Enterprise value = $447.3 + $1,416.3 = $1,863.6M.

Notice: terminal value accounts for $1,416.3 / $1,863.6 = 76% of total DCF value. That’s normal for a DCF and exactly why terminal-value assumptions dominate the output.

Now bridge to equity. Assume Atlas has $200M of debt and $50M of cash on the balance sheet, with 50M diluted shares.

Net debt = $200 − $50 = $150M.

Equity value = $1,863.6 − $150 = $1,713.6M.

Per-share intrinsic value = $1,713.6 / 50 = $34.27.

If the stock is trading at $30, the DCF implies it’s about 14% undervalued. If it’s trading at $45, the DCF implies it’s overvalued. In practice you’d run a sensitivity grid (varying WACC by 0.5% increments, terminal growth by 0.5%) and look at the range.

Sensitivity table

Same Atlas model, varying the two most-important inputs:

Terminal growth →
WACC ↓		2%2.5%3%3.5%4%
9%$36.6$38.7$41.0$43.6$46.5
9.5%$33.6$35.4$37.4$39.5$41.9
10%$30.8$32.5$34.3$36.1$38.2
10.5%$28.4$29.8$31.4$33.0$34.8
11%$26.2$27.5$28.8$30.2$31.8

Look at the spread: depending on assumptions, “the” DCF value of Atlas ranges from $26 to $46. That’s a 75% range from low to high. This is exactly why a DCF output is properly presented as a range, not a single number.

Terminal value — three methods compared

Method 1: Gordon Growth (Perpetuity Growth)

TV = FCFyear n+1 / (WACC − g)

Assumes free cash flow grows at a stable perpetual rate forever after year n. The growth rate g should never exceed long-run nominal GDP growth (US: ~3–4%). Above GDP growth in perpetuity is mathematically impossible — the company would eventually be bigger than the economy.

Best when: stable mature business, predictable growth profile.

Method 2: Exit Multiple

TV = EBITDAyear n × assumed exit multiple

Applies a market-based multiple (usually EV/EBITDA) to the terminal year. Anchored in real market valuations rather than perpetuity math.

Best when: industry has stable multiples, recent comparable transactions exist, the analyst is bridging to a future market exit.

Method 3: H-Model (Two-Stage Growth)

For companies still growing fast in years 5–10 but expected to mature to a steady-state growth rate by year 15–20, the H-Model splits terminal value into a transition period plus a steady-state perpetuity. More technical, used less often, but mathematically cleaner for high-growth-to-mature transitions.

Which to use

Best practice: compute both Gordon Growth and Exit Multiple, then check for convergence. If they agree within 15–20%, you have confidence in the answer. If they wildly disagree, your assumptions about growth, multiples, or both are inconsistent and need to be revisited.

Mid-year discounting — the technicality that comes up

In our Atlas DCF, we used end-of-year discounting — each year’s cash flow was discounted as if it arrived at the end of the year. In reality, cash flows arrive throughout the year. So strictly speaking we’re slightly undervaluing the business.

The fix is the mid-year convention. Each year’s FCF is discounted as if it arrived at the mid-point of the year, so the discount factor becomes 1/(1+r)n−0.5 instead of 1/(1+r)n.

For Year 1 at 10% WACC: end-of-year factor = 0.909, mid-year factor = 0.953. Each year’s cash flow is worth ~5% more under mid-year discounting. So mid-year typically increases DCF output by 3–5% relative to end-of-year discounting.

Many bank models use mid-year. Some don’t. It’s a small convention that you should know exists.

Each major multiple — deep dive

P/E (Price-to-Earnings)

Stock price ÷ earnings per share. The most widely-quoted multiple in retail investing.

Forward P/E vs trailing P/E: forward uses next-12-month estimated EPS; trailing uses last-12-month actual. Forward is more commonly used in valuation.

Strengths: easy to compute and compare; intuitive (it’s the “years of earnings to pay back the stock” metaphor).

Weaknesses: sensitive to capital structure (after-interest, after-tax), distorted by accounting (depreciation policies, one-time items), meaningless for unprofitable companies (negative EPS).

Industry context: tech → 25–40x typical, value → 10–15x, utilities → 15–20x. Recessions compress P/Es; bubbles expand them.

EV/EBITDA

Enterprise value ÷ EBITDA. The most-used valuation multiple in serious finance.

Strengths: capital-structure neutral (numerator and denominator both pre-leverage), accounting-neutral on D&A, comparable across firms.

Weaknesses: ignores capex needs and working-capital changes, which are real cash. So overstates the true cash-generating power of capital-intensive businesses.

Industry context: software → 15–25x, consumer staples → 12–18x, financials → not used (use P/B instead), telecoms → 6–10x.

EV/Revenue (EV/Sales)

Enterprise value ÷ revenue. Useful for unprofitable or pre-profit companies.

Strengths: works for negative-earnings companies; less manipulable than earnings multiples.

Weaknesses: ignores margins entirely — a 5x EV/Revenue company with 50% margins is much cheaper than a 5x EV/Revenue company with 10% margins, but the multiple looks the same.

Industry context: software → 5–15x typical; biotech → can be 20–100x; retail → 1–3x.

P/B (Price-to-Book)

Market cap ÷ book value of equity. Mostly used for financial companies.

Why financials use P/B: bank balance sheets are mostly financial assets marked to market or to amortized cost — book value is much closer to economic reality than at industrial companies. ROE drives P/B (higher ROE = higher P/B).

Bank P/B rules of thumb: trades above 1.0x means market thinks the bank earns above its cost of equity; below 1.0x means the market thinks the bank is destroying value.

P/FFO (Price to Funds From Operations)

For REITs. FFO = net income + depreciation (REIT-specific because real estate “depreciation” is largely a non-economic accounting concept — properties usually appreciate, not depreciate).

REIT P/FFO rules of thumb: high-quality REITs trade 15–25x, lower-quality 8–14x.

P/AUM (for asset managers and wealth managers)

Market cap ÷ assets under management. Used to compare asset managers.

Rules of thumb: traditional asset managers trade 1–3% of AUM (so a $100B-AUM firm has $1B–$3B market cap). Wealth managers and alternative-asset managers (with higher fees) command higher P/AUM. Direct-indexing platforms and tech-forward firms get premium multiples.

EV/EBIT

Used when D&A matters — capital-intensive businesses where ignoring depreciation would overstate true earnings.

PEG (Price/Earnings to Growth)

P/E divided by the growth rate. Used to compare companies with different growth profiles. A PEG of 1.0 is roughly “fair”; below 1.0 is potentially cheap; above 2.0 is potentially expensive. Peter Lynch popularized it.

The full LBO worked example — with actual numbers

Earlier we did a quick LBO example. Let’s now do a fuller one with the cash-flow detail.

Setup: a PE firm wants to acquire ResilientCo, a stable B2B services company. Here’s the deal:

  • Purchase price (enterprise value): $1,000M
  • Purchase multiple: 10x current EBITDA, so EBITDA at entry = $100M
  • Capital structure: 60% debt ($600M), 40% equity ($400M)
  • Interest rate on the debt: 8% (this is the “blended” rate across the debt stack)
  • EBITDA growth: 8% per year for 5 years
  • Capex: 4% of revenue (assume revenue = EBITDA ÷ 0.25 EBITDA margin)
  • D&A: roughly equals capex (steady-state assumption)
  • Tax rate: 25%
  • Working capital: roughly flat
  • Exit multiple: 10x (same as entry, no expansion or compression)

Year-by-year cash flow available to pay down debt

Year 1:

  • EBITDA: $100M → Revenue (at 25% EBITDA margin): $400M
  • Less D&A: $16M (4% of revenue)
  • EBIT: $84M
  • Less interest: $600M × 8% = $48M
  • Pre-tax income: $36M
  • Tax (25%): $9M
  • Net income: $27M
  • Plus D&A: $16M
  • Less capex: $16M
  • Less change in WC: $0
  • Free cash flow available to pay down debt: $27M

Year 2 (similar logic, but EBITDA grew 8%):

  • EBITDA: $108M, Revenue: $432M
  • D&A: $17.3M, EBIT: $90.7M
  • Interest (on reduced debt of $573M): $45.8M
  • Pre-tax: $44.9M, Tax: $11.2M, Net income: $33.7M
  • +D&A − capex = 0
  • FCF for debt paydown: $33.7M

Continuing through Year 5, the cumulative debt paydown approaches roughly $190M. So by exit:

  • Year 5 EBITDA: $100M × 1.085 = $147M
  • Exit EV at 10x: $1,470M
  • Remaining debt: $600M − $190M ~ $410M
  • Equity at exit: $1,470M − $410M = $1,060M

The return math

  • Original equity invested: $400M
  • Equity at exit: $1,060M
  • MOIC: $1,060M / $400M = 2.65x
  • 5-year IRR: approximately 21.5% per year

What if the multiple compresses?

Same model but exit multiple is 8x instead of 10x:

  • Exit EV: $147M × 8 = $1,176M
  • Equity at exit: $1,176M − $410M = $766M
  • MOIC: 1.92x
  • IRR: roughly 13.8%

A 2-turn multiple compression cuts returns nearly in half. That’s the “sponsor’s worst fear” in LBO modeling.

What if EBITDA growth is half?

Same model but EBITDA grows 4% per year instead of 8%:

  • Year 5 EBITDA: $122M instead of $147M
  • Exit EV at 10x: $1,220M
  • Equity at exit: $1,220M − $410M = $810M
  • MOIC: 2.03x
  • IRR: roughly 15.2%

The three engines, recapped

The IRR sensitivity demonstrates the three drivers of LBO returns concretely:

  • EBITDA growth: 8% vs 4% = ~6 percentage points of IRR
  • Multiple expansion (or compression): 10x vs 8x exit = ~8 percentage points
  • Debt paydown: implicit in the model — without leverage, the same EBITDA growth at the same multiples would produce ~13% IRR (just the unlevered business return)

The leverage magnifies all three. Good sponsors target deals where they can be reasonably confident in EBITDA growth and where multiple compression risk is contained.

The football field — what it looks like

For our Atlas DCF example, suppose:

  • DCF (base case): $34 per share
  • DCF (sensitivity range): $26 to $46
  • Trading comps (peer median EV/EBITDA): implies $32 to $38
  • Precedent transactions (recent acquisitions in space): implies $40 to $50
  • LBO floor (what a PE buyer could pay): implies $28 to $33
  • 52-week trading range: $25 to $45
  • Analyst price target range: $32 to $42

The football field shows all of these as horizontal bars on a single chart. Bankers look for overlap:

  • Trading comps, base DCF, and analyst targets all converge in the $32–$42 range. That’s a defensible “trading value” range.
  • Precedent transactions sit higher ($40–$50). The gap reflects the control premium and synergies.
  • LBO floor at the bottom (~$30) tells you what a financial buyer could pay if the strategic premium doesn’t materialize.

For an M&A sell-side process, the banker would advise the client to target $42–$50 in negotiations — near the precedent-transaction range — while having $32 as the absolute floor (below which it’d make more sense to walk away).

M&A accretion / dilution — the worked example

You should be able to answer “will this deal be accretive or dilutive?” quickly. Here’s the math.

Setup: BigCo wants to acquire LittleCo for $1,000M. Both are public. Key data:

  • BigCo: $5,000M market cap, 100M shares, $400M net income, $4 EPS
  • BigCo P/E: 12.5x
  • LittleCo: $1,000M market cap, 20M shares, $80M net income
  • LittleCo P/E: 12.5x (same as BigCo)
  • Cost of debt for BigCo (after tax): 4%
  • Inverse of acquirer P/E (= earnings yield): 1/12.5 = 8%
  • Synergies: $20M annual cost savings (assume fully realized in year 1 for simplicity)

Case 1: 100% cash deal (financed with debt)

BigCo issues $1,000M of new debt at 4% after-tax cost. New annual interest expense: $40M.

Pro forma net income: $400M (BigCo) + $80M (LittleCo) + $20M (synergies) − $40M (new interest) = $460M.

Pro forma shares: still 100M (no new shares issued).

Pro forma EPS: $460M / 100M = $4.60.

Accretion: $4.60 / $4.00 − 1 = +15% accretive.

The rule of thumb: cash deals are accretive when after-tax cost of debt is lower than the target’s earnings yield (inverse of P/E). Here: 4% cost of debt vs 8% target earnings yield → accretive. Big spread = big accretion.

Case 2: 100% stock deal

BigCo issues $1,000M of new stock to LittleCo shareholders. At BigCo’s $50/share price ($5,000M / 100M), that’s 20M new shares issued.

Pro forma net income: $400M + $80M + $20M = $500M.

Pro forma shares: 100M + 20M = 120M.

Pro forma EPS: $500M / 120M = $4.17.

Accretion: $4.17 / $4.00 − 1 = +4.3% accretive.

Less accretive than the cash deal, but still accretive because of the synergies. Without the $20M of synergies, it would have been roughly neutral.

Case 3: 100% stock, but BigCo’s P/E is below LittleCo’s

Say BigCo trades at 10x P/E and LittleCo at 15x. BigCo has to issue more shares to buy LittleCo’s pricier-per-dollar earnings.

BigCo price: $40 ($400M / 100M shares at 10x P/E times $40 stock price = $400M earnings). New shares to issue for $1,000M: 25M.

Pro forma shares: 125M. Pro forma EPS: $500M / 125M = $4.00. Net change: 0%.

Without synergies: $4.00 / 100M × 1.25 = $3.84 = 4% dilutive.

This shows the general rule: stock deals are dilutive when the acquirer’s P/E is below the target’s P/E, unless synergies are big enough to bridge the gap.

Synergies — what they actually are

Synergies are the value-creation an acquirer expects from combining with a target. Two main types:

Cost synergies (“hard” synergies)

Cost savings from eliminating duplication. Examples:

  • Consolidating headquarters and back office (HR, finance, IT) — eliminating duplicate executives, real estate, software licenses.
  • Combining procurement — bigger orders, better supplier discounts.
  • Manufacturing consolidation — closing duplicate plants.
  • Sales-force optimization — eliminating overlapping coverage.

Cost synergies are “hard” because they’re relatively predictable. Bankers and analysts believe them. Typical realization: 70–90% of announced cost synergies actually materialize.

Revenue synergies (“soft” synergies)

Revenue gains from cross-selling or strategic positioning. Examples:

  • Selling the acquirer’s products through the target’s customer relationships.
  • Geographic expansion — entering markets where one side was strong.
  • Bundling products that previously competed.
  • Pricing power from increased market share.

Revenue synergies are “soft” because they depend on customer behavior, integration execution, and competitive response. Typical realization: 20–40% of announced revenue synergies actually materialize. Sophisticated investors heavily discount revenue-synergy projections.

Negative synergies (“dis-synergies”)

The unfortunate flip side. Integration disruption causes lost productivity, customer attrition, key-employee departure, cultural conflict. Often under-modeled but very real.

Sources & uses table — how deals are actually paid for

Bankers structure deal financing with a “sources & uses” table. Sources = where the money comes from. Uses = where the money goes.

For our $1,000M acquisition of LittleCo (cash deal):

SourcesAmountUsesAmount
New debt issuance$800MPurchase price (equity)$1,000M
Cash on hand$200MRefinancing of target’s existing debt$50M
Existing revolver draw$80MTransaction fees (banking, legal)$30M
Total sources$1,080MTotal uses$1,080M

Sources have to equal uses, exactly. The construction of the sources & uses table is one of the first things a junior banker learns.

The complete valuation interview question bank

Walk me through a DCF.
Can you describe a DCF the way someone who actually understands it would — not the way someone who memorized a script would?
Variant 1 — the intuitive version (most human)

“Yeah, so the big idea of a DCF is that a business is worth the cash it’ll generate in the future, discounted back to today at a rate that reflects the risk.

The way you actually build it: you project free cash flow for like 5 to 10 years. That’s EBIT times one minus the tax rate, plus depreciation, minus capex, minus the change in working capital. So — operating cash, basically, after the company has paid for its own growth.

Then you need to capture everything beyond year 10, which is called terminal value. There are two main ways to do it — Gordon growth, where you assume a perpetual growth rate, or an exit multiple, where you slap an EBITDA multiple on the final year. Most people compute both and look for them to agree.

Then you discount everything back to today at WACC — the weighted cost of debt and equity. Sum it up and you get enterprise value. Subtract net debt, divide by shares, and you have intrinsic value per share.

The honest caveat I’d add — DCFs are super sensitive to your assumptions on growth and discount rate. You can move the output by 50% with small tweaks. So in practice you do a sensitivity grid and you treat the output as a range, not a number.”

Variant 2 — the structured version (good for analytical interviewers)

“Sure. A DCF has four main steps.

Step one is projecting free cash flow. You take operating income, tax-effect it, add back depreciation since it’s non-cash, subtract capex because that’s real cash leaving the business, and subtract the change in working capital because growing businesses absorb cash into receivables and inventory. That gives you unlevered free cash flow for each year of the forecast.

Step two is terminal value, which captures the value of cash flows beyond the explicit forecast period. The most common method is Gordon growth — final-year FCF divided by (WACC minus perpetual growth rate). Alternatively, exit multiple on year-N EBITDA.

Step three is discounting. You use WACC, the weighted average cost of capital, which blends the after-tax cost of debt with the cost of equity weighted by the capital structure. Each year’s cash flow gets divided by one-plus-WACC raised to the appropriate power.

Step four is bridging to equity. The sum of discounted cash flows plus discounted terminal value gives enterprise value. Subtract net debt and minority interest, add back any non-operating cash, divide by diluted share count, and you have intrinsic value per share.

In practice, you always sensitize on WACC and terminal growth or exit multiple, because the output is highly dependent on those assumptions.”

Variant 3 — the simple version (when you’re nervous or short on time)

“Okay, a DCF. Forecast the company’s free cash flow for, say, ten years. Then estimate a terminal value for everything beyond that. Then discount all of it back to today at the company’s cost of capital. Sum it up — that’s enterprise value. Subtract net debt, divide by shares, and you have a per-share value you can compare to where the stock is trading.

The whole thing is just ‘how much cash will the business generate, what’s that worth today given the risk.’ Everything else is the careful version of that one sentence.”

Variant 4 — the honest version (for senior or skeptical interviewers)

“Sure, I can walk through one. But honestly, the part I find most interesting about DCFs is how dangerous they are if you trust the output.

The mechanics are: project free cash flow for some years, calculate a terminal value, discount everything back to today at WACC, sum it up, bridge from enterprise to equity value, divide by shares. The math is straightforward.

But the assumptions hide everything. Terminal value alone is usually 60 to 80% of total DCF value, which means your perpetual growth rate or exit multiple basically is the answer. WACC is itself a stack of assumptions — beta, risk-free rate, equity risk premium — each of which is debatable. Growth assumptions in the first few years often don’t survive contact with reality.

So the way I’d actually use a DCF is as a discipline rather than a verdict. It forces you to think about what the business needs to do to justify a given price. You don’t walk away with a single number; you walk away with a feel for whether the market’s expectations are reasonable.”

Variant 5 — the laundromat version (most memorable)

“Yeah. So the cleanest way to think about it is — pretend you’re buying a laundromat. The seller wants three hundred grand. The way you’d figure out if that’s a fair price is to project how much cash the laundromat will throw off each year for the next ten years — rent, machines, washer revenue minus utilities, all of it. Then you’d estimate what it’s worth after year ten, when you might sell it. Then you’d discount all of that back to today, because money next year is worth less than money today.

A DCF on a public company is literally just that, with more line items. EBIT minus taxes plus depreciation minus capex minus working-capital change gives you free cash flow. Terminal value captures the post-forecast years. WACC is the discount rate. Sum it all up, subtract debt, divide by shares.

Whenever I’m doing one, I try to remember it’s really about the laundromat — how much cash, when, how risky. Everything else is plumbing.”

Why each variant works

Variants 1, 4, and 5 sound like someone understands the DCF. Variants 2 and 3 sound like someone who has been trained on it. Most interviewers prefer the “understanding” voice, but some — especially old-school IB people — will respect the “structured” voice. Read the room. If they’re smiling and conversational, give them Variant 1 or 5. If they’re pulling out a notebook, give them Variant 2.

Why does enterprise value subtract cash?
Elite answer

“Because cash on the balance sheet isn’t part of the operating business — it’s a separate asset. If you buy the company, you essentially get the cash back, so the effective cost of acquiring the operating business is reduced by that amount. Said differently, enterprise value is supposed to represent the value of the business’s ability to generate cash, separate from the cash it already happens to have lying around.”

Why is EV/EBITDA preferred over P/E for many comparisons?
Elite answer

“EV/EBITDA is capital-structure neutral — both the numerator and denominator strip out the effects of how a company is financed. P/E is calculated on equity earnings, which are after interest, so two otherwise identical companies with different leverage will look very different on P/E. EV/EBITDA also strips out non-cash D&A and accounting choices, which makes it more comparable across firms with different capex profiles or different accounting policies. The downside is that EBITDA ignores real capex and working capital needs, so it’s a less useful metric for capital-intensive businesses than for asset-light ones.”

What is WACC, and why do we use it as the discount rate?
Elite answer

“WACC is the weighted average cost of capital — the blended cost of a company’s debt and equity financing. We use it as the discount rate in a DCF because we’re valuing the unlevered cash flows that are available to all capital providers, so we need a discount rate that reflects the required return of all capital providers. The cost of equity is typically estimated with CAPM — risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium. The cost of debt is the after-tax yield the company pays on its debt. The weights are the market-value weights of equity and debt in the capital structure.”

What is the terminal value, and which method do you prefer?
Elite answer

“Terminal value represents the present value at the end of the explicit forecast period of all future cash flows beyond that point. The two standard methods are Gordon growth, which assumes free cash flow grows in perpetuity at a stable rate (usually pegged near long-term GDP), and the exit multiple method, which applies a market-based EBITDA multiple to the terminal year. I tend to prefer Gordon growth for the theoretical purity, but in practice analysts usually compute both and look for convergence. Both methods are extremely sensitive to assumptions, which is why terminal value typically accounts for 60–80% of total DCF value — and why DCF outputs are best presented as a range.”

What is CAPM?
Elite answer

“The Capital Asset Pricing Model estimates the expected return on equity as the risk-free rate plus beta times the equity risk premium. The intuition is that an investor demands a return equal to a risk-free baseline plus extra compensation for taking on systematic market risk, measured by beta. CAPM is imperfect — the assumptions are strong and the inputs are noisy — but it’s the standard starting point for cost-of-equity in a DCF.”

What is beta, and where do we get it?
Elite answer

“Beta measures the volatility of a stock relative to the overall market. A beta of 1 moves in line with the market; above 1 amplifies market moves; below 1 dampens them. For a public company, you regress historical returns against a market index. For a private company or a new business, you typically use the median beta of comparable public companies, ‘unlever’ it to strip out their capital structures, and ‘relever’ it for your target’s capital structure.”

What are typical drawbacks of comparable companies analysis?
Elite answer

“Three big ones. First, no two companies are truly identical — growth, margins, geographic mix, and capital intensity all diverge. Second, multiples reflect current market sentiment, which can be irrationally high or low. Third, multiples don’t capture business-specific catalysts that should affect valuation. Comps are useful as a sanity check on a DCF, but they shouldn’t be the only method.”

Why are precedent-transaction multiples typically higher than trading-comp multiples?
Elite answer

“Precedent transactions include a control premium — the extra amount acquirers pay to take full ownership, plus the strategic value of synergies the acquirer expects to realize. Trading comps just reflect the marginal market price of a single share, which is a minority stake.”

How does an acquisition create value?
Elite answer

“Theoretically, an acquisition creates value when the combined business is worth more than the sum of its parts. Sources of value include cost synergies (eliminating duplicate functions), revenue synergies (cross-selling, geographic expansion), tax benefits, financial synergies from a more efficient capital structure, or strategic positioning. In practice, most acquisitions destroy value because acquirers overpay or fail to realize the projected synergies. The literature suggests roughly half of all M&A deals destroy shareholder value over the long run.”

Why might a company trade at a discount to its peers?
Elite answer

“Lots of reasons: lower growth, weaker margins, higher leverage, weaker management, less attractive end-markets, regulatory risk, governance concerns, smaller scale, or simply being misunderstood by the market. As an analyst your job is to figure out whether the discount is justified or whether it represents an opportunity.”

If interest rates rise, what happens to valuations?
Elite answer

“Other things equal, higher rates raise the discount rate, which lowers the present value of future cash flows. The effect is biggest on long-duration cash flows — high-growth companies whose value lies far in the future — which is why growth stocks tend to underperform when real yields rise. Higher rates also raise the cost of debt for companies, compress consumer spending, and slow the economy, which can affect cash flows themselves.”

What’s a basic LBO model in plain English?
Elite answer

“A PE firm buys a company, financing the purchase mostly with debt. Over the next five years or so, the company generates cash, pays down some of that debt, ideally grows EBITDA, and the firm eventually sells. The exit value is some multiple of the now-larger EBITDA. The equity proceeds at exit, minus the original equity check, divided over the years, give you the IRR and the multiple of money. The math works because debt magnifies equity returns when the underlying business performs, but it also magnifies losses if it doesn’t. The art is choosing companies with steady cash flows that can support meaningful leverage.”

What’s the difference between equity value and enterprise value if a company is cash-rich and debt-free?
Elite answer

“If the company has no debt, no preferred, and no minority interest, and it has, say, $5B of cash, then enterprise value equals equity value minus $5B. Equity value reflects the entire claim of shareholders — including their pro rata interest in the cash on the balance sheet — while enterprise value isolates the operating business. Apple a few years ago was the classic example: huge equity value, but a meaningful portion of that was simply the cash pile.”

How would you value a private company with no comparable public peers?
Elite answer

“You’d lean more heavily on a DCF, using carefully chosen assumptions and explicit sensitivities. You’d also look at precedent transactions in adjacent industries, applying a discount or premium for the differences. For a stable cash-flow business, you might also use a leveraged buyout style valuation, working backward from what a PE buyer could pay given typical leverage and target returns. The output is always a range, and you’d note the wider uncertainty driven by less comparable data.”

Why do investment bankers care so much about whether a deal is accretive or dilutive?
Elite answer

“Because public-market shareholders react to short-term EPS impact, and CEOs and CFOs need to defend deals to those shareholders. A dilutive deal is harder to sell, even if the long-term value creation is real. So bankers structure deals — the mix of cash and stock, financing terms, synergy phasing — to manage the accretion/dilution profile. That said, accretion alone doesn’t mean a deal is good; it’s a signal, not the substance.”

When you can build the DCF logic out loud in 90 seconds and explain WACC without notes, this chapter is yours.
Chapter 08 — Markets & Macro

Markets and the macro economy, made visceral.

In a coffee chat, the moment that decides everything is often when an advisor says, casually, “what do you think is happening in markets right now?” This chapter is so you can answer that without panic, without bluffing, and without sounding like a textbook. We’re going to build a working model of how the macro machine runs, so that the news headlines start fitting into a frame instead of feeling like a chaos of acronyms.

Why a wealth manager needs to understand macro

You won’t be a macroeconomist. You don’t need to be. But you need enough of the picture to (a) speak coherently when a client asks, “what about the Fed?” and (b) understand why your firm’s asset allocation is what it is. Most advisors don’t need to predict the macro — they need to explain it so that clients stay invested through whatever it does next.

Interest rates — the price of money

An interest rate is the price of borrowing money for a period of time. It’s also, viewed from the other side, the return for lending money. Everything else in macro rotates around this idea.

Higher rates mean borrowing is more expensive. People take out fewer mortgages, businesses delay capex, the economy cools. Lower rates make borrowing cheap; activity accelerates.

The most important rate to know is the federal funds rate: the overnight rate at which US banks lend reserves to each other. The Federal Reserve sets a target for this rate. Through that single lever, the Fed influences virtually every other interest rate in the economy — mortgages, credit cards, business loans, Treasury yields, savings rates — with a lag.

The Fed, in plain English — a deep walkthrough

Okay, so this is the most-asked macro topic in any finance interview, and most freshmen describe the Fed in a way that sounds memorized. Let’s really build the picture so you can talk about it like a normal person.

What is the Fed, really?

The Federal Reserve is the US central bank. It was created in 1913 after a series of banking panics convinced Congress that the country needed a lender of last resort. Today it does roughly three things:
