  • It sets short-term interest rates to manage the economy.
  • It serves as the “banker to the banks” — clearing payments, regulating banks, providing emergency liquidity in crises.
  • It issues and manages US currency.

For interviews, you really only care about the first one.

The dual mandate

Congress gave the Fed two equal-weight goals, called the “dual mandate”:

  • Maximum employment — basically, “keep unemployment low.”
  • Stable prices — the Fed interprets this as keeping inflation around 2% per year on average.

These two goals are usually in conflict. Pushing unemployment down too far creates wage pressure that lifts inflation. Pushing inflation down too hard usually causes some unemployment because the economy has to slow. So the Fed’s job is constant balancing — lean against inflation when it’s too high, lean against unemployment when it’s too high.

The main lever — the federal funds rate

The Fed sets a target range for one specific interest rate: the federal funds rate. It’s the rate at which US banks lend to each other overnight to meet their reserve requirements.

That rate sounds incredibly specific and obscure, but it’s the gravitational center of virtually all other interest rates in the US economy:

  • Short-term Treasury yields move closely with the fed funds rate.
  • The prime rate (the rate banks charge their best customers) is typically fed funds + 3%.
  • Credit card rates are prime + a margin.
  • Mortgage rates are influenced by it (mostly indirectly, through the 10-year Treasury).
  • Business loan rates, auto loans, student loans — all influenced.

So when the Fed moves the funds rate, it’s essentially turning a knob that ripples through everyone’s borrowing costs. That changes their behavior. That changes the whole economy.

How rate changes actually affect the economy — the transmission

Let’s say the Fed wants to slow the economy. They raise rates. Then what?

  1. Mortgages get more expensive. Homebuyers either delay purchases or buy smaller homes. Housing demand drops. Construction slows.
  2. Auto loans get more expensive. Car sales drop.
  3. Business borrowing gets more expensive. Companies delay capex projects, hire fewer people, sometimes lay people off.
  4. Credit card rates rise. Households carrying balances cut back on discretionary spending.
  5. Stock multiples compress because the discount rate (WACC) for valuing future cash flows is higher.
  6. Bond prices drop. Anyone holding existing bonds at lower rates is sitting on losses.
  7. The dollar tends to strengthen because higher US yields attract foreign capital.

All of this together cools the economy. Less spending, less investment, slightly more unemployment, lower inflation pressure.

The reverse happens when the Fed cuts rates. Everything goes the other way.

The huge wrinkle — lags

Here’s the thing most people miss. Monetary policy works with long and variable lags. When the Fed raises rates today, the full economic impact doesn’t show up for 12–18 months. The economy is like an oil tanker — you turn the wheel and it takes a long time to actually change direction.

This is why the Fed often appears to over-tighten or over-loosen. They’re trying to steer an oil tanker by looking at the wake. Sometimes they catch the inflation wave too early; sometimes too late.

The 2022–2024 tightening cycle is a textbook example. Inflation peaked in mid-2022, but the Fed kept raising rates aggressively through early 2023, and the full impact on the economy — the unemployment ticking up, the credit-card delinquencies, the commercial-real-estate stress — didn’t fully arrive until 2024.

The unconventional tools

Sometimes the fed funds rate isn’t enough — either because it’s already at zero (the “zero lower bound”) or because financial markets are seizing up. The Fed has additional tools:

  • Quantitative easing (QE): the Fed creates new reserves (just an accounting entry) and uses them to buy bonds — usually Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities. This pushes down long-term rates and injects liquidity into the financial system. Used heavily after 2008 and during COVID. The Fed’s balance sheet ballooned from about $900B pre-2008 to nearly $9 trillion at the COVID peak.
  • Quantitative tightening (QT): the opposite. Let bonds mature without replacing them, gradually shrinking the balance sheet. The Fed has been doing this since mid-2022.
  • Forward guidance: communicating the Fed’s intentions about future rates, to shape market expectations. Powell saying “higher for longer” isn’t just words — it’s a policy tool.
  • Emergency lending facilities: when banks or markets are in trouble, the Fed can create targeted programs (the discount window, the Primary Dealer Credit Facility, the Bank Term Funding Program created after SVB’s failure) to provide liquidity to specific parts of the system.

The FOMC and how decisions get made

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is the body that actually sets policy. It meets eight times a year for two days at a time. The committee has 12 voting members: the seven governors of the Federal Reserve Board (including the chair), the president of the New York Fed (permanent voter), and four of the other 11 regional Fed presidents on a rotating basis.

The schedule of meetings is published a year ahead of time, and they typically fall on a Tuesday/Wednesday. Markets watch them obsessively because:

  • The Wednesday afternoon press conference (the Chair’s 30-minute presser) is the single most-watched financial event in any given week with a meeting.
  • Every quarter, the FOMC publishes a “Summary of Economic Projections,” including the famous “dot plot” — each member’s anonymous forecast for where rates will be at year-end for the next several years.
  • The official statement gets parsed word-by-word for shifts in language.

An interview-grade insight: pay attention to the gap between what the dot plot is signaling and what the futures market is pricing. When they diverge significantly, it usually means someone is wrong, and the divergence is going to resolve — sometimes with violent market moves.

How Fed talk affects portfolios in real life

For a WM advisor, the Fed is not an academic exercise. Every rate move and statement reshapes client portfolios:

  • Rising rates: bond holdings take a price hit. Long-duration tech stocks compress. Lending rates climb for clients with floating-rate debt. Cash and short-duration Treasuries suddenly become attractive.
  • Falling rates: bonds rally. Growth stocks tend to do well. Cash returns drop. Refinancing windows open for clients with mortgages or commercial debt.
  • Forward guidance shifts: even signaling a coming rate change moves markets before the actual change. Advisors often have to explain to clients why the bond market dropped when the Fed didn’t actually do anything — the answer is usually “the Fed signaled they would do something, and the market repriced ahead.”

Why the Fed gets criticized constantly

Three persistent criticisms, useful to know about because they come up in interviews:

  • The Fed is too political (or not political enough).
  • The Fed’s easy-money policies create asset bubbles that benefit the wealthy.
  • The Fed reacts too late to inflation and then over-tightens.

You don’t need to take a side. You just need to know the criticisms exist and be able to acknowledge them thoughtfully when they come up.

The Fed’s decisions are made by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC), which meets eight times a year. Markets obsessively parse the chair’s press conferences and the published “dot plot” (each FOMC member’s view of where rates will be in coming years).

Inflation — what it is, why it happens, why it matters — the deep version

What inflation actually is

Inflation is the general rise in prices across the economy over time. If a gallon of milk cost $3.00 a year ago and now costs $3.10, that’s 3.3% inflation in milk. If most things in the economy did similar small price rises, the overall inflation rate would be around 3%.

Why we measure it: inflation matters because it’s essentially a tax on cash and on fixed cash flows. If you held $100,000 in cash for a year during 3% inflation, you didn’t literally lose money — you still have $100,000. But that $100,000 now buys 3% less stuff. Your real purchasing power has dropped.

For long-term investors, this is the most important consequence of inflation. Cash and bonds with fixed coupons lose real value to inflation. Real assets — real estate, commodities, equities with pricing power — generally hold up better.

How inflation is measured

The most important inflation measures:

  • CPI (Consumer Price Index): a basket of goods and services that’s tracked by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Includes food, energy, housing, transportation, medical care, education, etc. Released monthly. The headline number that most people see in the news.
  • Core CPI: CPI excluding food and energy, which are volatile. The Fed pays attention to this because it’s a better signal of underlying inflation trends.
  • PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures): a slightly different inflation measure produced by the Bureau of Economic Analysis. Has different weights than CPI and adjusts for substitution (if beef gets expensive and people buy more chicken, PCE captures that shift faster than CPI does). The Fed’s preferred measure.
  • Core PCE: PCE excluding food and energy. This is the Fed’s actual 2% target measure.

The numbers often diverge slightly. As of recent years, CPI tends to run a touch higher than PCE because of the different weights. When you read “the Fed is targeting 2% inflation,” they mean core PCE.

Why inflation happens — the two stories

Demand-pull inflation: too much money chasing too few goods. When the economy is booming, consumers and businesses have lots of money to spend, demand for goods and services exceeds the supply at current prices, and prices rise. Fiscal stimulus, easy monetary policy, credit booms, or just a strong labor market with rising wages can all contribute to demand-pull inflation.

The 2021–2022 inflation surge had a major demand-pull component — pandemic-era stimulus checks and forbearance gave consumers a lot of cash, and they spent it on goods (more than services because services were restricted), and prices rose.

Cost-push inflation: input costs rise, and businesses pass the costs through to consumers. Oil price spikes, wage growth, supply-chain disruptions, tariffs, all of these push costs up. The 1970s stagflation was largely a cost-push story driven by oil shocks.

The 2021–2022 inflation also had a major cost-push component — supply chains were disrupted, container shipping spiked, semiconductor shortages drove up prices for cars and electronics. Hence the famous “both demand and supply” framing of the era.

Why inflation matters for investors — the deep version

Inflation reshapes financial markets in several distinct ways:

1. It destroys cash purchasing power

This is the most direct effect. $100,000 in a checking account earning zero interest loses about 3% of real purchasing power per year in a 3% inflation environment. Over 20 years, that’s a 45% loss of real value. This is why advisors push clients to invest cash, not hoard it.

2. It pressures bond prices

If you bought a 10-year Treasury at a 4% coupon and inflation jumps to 6%, your real return on that bond is now negative. Other investors see the same thing and bid down the price of existing bonds. Long-duration bonds are most exposed.

3. It pressures growth stocks more than value stocks

Recall the DCF framework. A growth stock’s value is heavily back-loaded — most of the value sits in cash flows far in the future. When inflation rises, the discount rate (WACC) rises too. Future cash flows get discounted more heavily. Long-duration assets — growth stocks, long-duration bonds — get hit hardest. Value stocks, whose cash flows are more near-term, are relatively less affected.

4. It rewards companies with pricing power

Some companies can raise prices without losing customers — think Apple, Hermes, Coca-Cola. Their margins hold up in inflation. Other companies sell commoditized products and can’t raise prices — think airlines, grocery stores. Their margins compress in inflation because input costs rise but selling prices don’t keep pace.

5. It rewards real assets

Real estate, commodities, infrastructure, gold — these tend to hold or appreciate in inflationary environments because their values are denominated in real things that keep their value. Inflation-protected securities (TIPS) are an explicit hedge.

6. It complicates Fed policy

Persistent above-target inflation forces the Fed to raise rates, which slows the economy, which usually hurts asset prices in the short term. The 2022–2024 rate-hike cycle was the textbook example.

How advisors actually use this

For clients, inflation translates into a portfolio framework. A good advisor will:

  • Hold cash only for short-term needs and emergency reserves — never as a long-term asset.
  • Use I-Bonds and TIPS for the safe-asset sleeve in inflationary periods.
  • Tilt equity exposure toward companies with pricing power.
  • Allocate to real assets (real estate, infrastructure, sometimes commodities) for diversification.
  • Communicate clearly with clients about “real” vs. “nominal” returns. A 6% portfolio return in a 4% inflation year is only 2% real — meaningfully different from a 6% return in a 2% inflation year.

Bonds — deeper than you think

A bond is a tradable IOU. It has a face value (e.g. $1,000), a coupon (e.g. 4% per year, paid semi-annually), and a maturity (e.g. 10 years). When you buy a bond, you’re lending someone money in exchange for the right to receive those coupon payments and the face value at maturity.

Why bond prices move inversely to rates — explained slowly

Okay, this confuses everyone at first. Let me try to explain it the way it finally clicked for me, in really simple language. Stay with me.

Imagine you bought a 10-year Treasury a year ago. You paid $1,000 for it. It pays you 4% per year — so you get $40 a year, and at the end of 10 years you get your $1,000 back. Pretty simple.

Now imagine today, market rates have risen. New 10-year Treasuries are being issued at 5%. If you bought a new one today, you’d get $50 a year on $1,000, and the same $1,000 back at the end.

Question: if your friend wanted to buy a bond, would she want yours (paying $40 a year) or a new one (paying $50 a year)? Obviously the new one. Same risk, same maturity, more money.

So now you have a problem. You want to sell your old bond, but nobody wants it at $1,000. The only way someone will buy it is if you drop the price. You’d have to drop the price until your bond, after the discount, gives the buyer the same effective return as the new 5% bond. That math works out to roughly $922.

And that’s the inverse relationship. Rates went up — from 4% to 5%. Your bond’s price went down — from $1,000 to roughly $922. The coupon hasn’t changed. You’re still getting your $40 a year. But the market value of your bond went down by about 8%, because new bonds are paying more.

If rates had gone down instead — say to 3% — the opposite would happen. Suddenly your 4% bond would look great compared to new 3% bonds. People would bid for it. The price would go up.

Why this matters in real life

Picture a retiree client. She owns a big bond portfolio for income — let’s say $2M in Treasuries and investment-grade corporates. She bought most of them years ago when rates were low.

Now imagine 2022. The Fed raises rates very fast. Suddenly her bonds are being marked down in her brokerage statement. Her account is showing a 15% loss on the bond side. She calls her advisor in tears.

The advisor’s job in that conversation is to explain — clearly, calmly, in plain English — what actually happened:

  • You still own the bonds.
  • You’re still getting the same interest payments you were always getting.
  • If you hold them to maturity, you’ll get the full $1,000 back per bond. Not less.
  • The “loss” on the statement is the market price of the bond — what you’d get if you sold today. But you don’t need to sell today.
  • If you panic-sell now, the loss becomes real. If you hold, it isn’t.

That single conversation — the “don’t panic-sell, the price will come back if you hold” conversation — is honestly half the reason a retiree pays an advisor. Most people, left alone, would sell. The advisor talks them off the ledge.

The deeper point

Here’s a thing nobody told me when I was first learning this — the inverse relationship between rates and bond prices isn’t a quirk or a technicality. It’s the whole story of fixed income. When you understand it deeply, a lot of finance suddenly makes sense:

  • That’s why bonds are sometimes called “rate-sensitive.”
  • That’s why “duration” exists as a concept — to measure exactly how much a bond’s price moves when rates move 1%.
  • That’s why a 30-year zero-coupon Treasury can lose 30%+ in a year if rates spike.
  • That’s why pension funds, which hold huge bond portfolios, get crushed when rates spike unexpectedly.
  • That’s why “higher for longer” is such a stressful phrase for anyone who owns bonds.

One small idea — rates up, prices down — unlocks an enormous chunk of finance once you really get it.

Duration

Duration measures how sensitive a bond’s price is to interest rate changes. Longer-maturity bonds and lower-coupon bonds have higher duration. A 30-year zero-coupon Treasury can lose 30%+ in a year if rates rise by 1%; a short-term T-bill barely moves.

Credit risk vs. interest-rate risk

Two distinct risks in bonds:

  • Interest-rate risk: bond price moves with rates.
  • Credit risk: the borrower might not pay. Treasuries have essentially no credit risk. Investment-grade corporate bonds have some. High-yield (junk) bonds have meaningful credit risk in exchange for higher yields.

The spread between corporate yields and Treasury yields (the “credit spread”) widens when investors worry about default risk and narrows when they don’t. Credit spreads are one of the best real-time indicators of financial stress.

The yield curve, decoded — the deep version

What the yield curve actually is

The yield curve is a graph. On the X-axis, you have the maturity of US Treasury bonds — 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, 2 years, 5 years, 10 years, 30 years. On the Y-axis, you have the yield (annual interest rate) for each maturity.

The line connecting these data points is the yield curve. It shows how the market is pricing the cost of borrowing money for different time horizons.

Why this matters: the yield curve is one of the cleanest signals about what bond markets — the smartest, biggest, most-informed market in the world — think about future growth, inflation, and Fed policy.

The three classic shapes

Normal curve (upward sloping)

Long-term yields are higher than short-term yields. The 30-year Treasury yields more than the 10-year, which yields more than the 2-year, which yields more than the 3-month T-bill.

Why this is the default shape: longer-maturity bonds tie up your money for longer, so investors typically demand more compensation for that. Plus, long bonds bear more inflation risk, so a premium is built in. Empirically, the yield curve has been upward-sloping most of the time.

What a normal curve implies: markets expect modest growth, modest inflation, normal monetary policy. Boring, but boring is good.

Flat curve

Long-term and short-term yields are similar. Often a transition state between normal and inverted (or back from inverted to normal).

What it implies: uncertainty, or a turning point in the cycle. Either the short end is being pushed up by tightening Fed policy, or the long end is being pulled down by growth expectations.

Inverted curve

Short-term yields are higher than long-term yields. So a 2-year Treasury yields more than a 10-year, or a 3-month T-bill yields more than a 10-year.

Why inversion is unusual and important: investors are accepting a lower yield to lock up their money for a longer time. That only makes sense if they expect short-term rates to fall sharply in the future — which would happen if the Fed has to cut rates to fight a recession.

What it implies: markets are pricing in a slowdown or recession.

Why inversion is a recession indicator — the history

Every US recession in the modern era has been preceded by a yield-curve inversion. The 1990 recession, the 2001 recession, the 2008 recession, the 2020 recession — all had yield curves invert 6 to 24 months before the recession started.

The mechanism isn’t magic. It works because the bond market is forecasting Fed behavior. When the Fed is fighting inflation by hiking short-term rates, but the bond market thinks the economy is going to slow and the Fed will have to cut rates later, the long end of the curve drops below the short end. The bond market is essentially saying: “you’re tightening now, but the slowdown is coming, and you’ll be cutting rates within 12 to 24 months.”

The 2022–2024 inversion was the longest in modern history. That’s remarkable. And whether the historical pattern holds — meaning a recession follows in the next 12 to 24 months — is one of the most debated macro questions right now.

Which yields to actually watch

Different practitioners look at different points on the curve. Common spreads:

  • 10-year minus 2-year: the classic. Widely watched, easy to find on Bloomberg or FRED.
  • 10-year minus 3-month: favored by some economists; has been the most reliable recession predictor historically.
  • 30-year minus 5-year: useful for very-long-duration views.

The spreads have different mean values, different volatility, and different lead times. An inversion in the 10y2y might lag or lead an inversion in the 10y3m.

How the curve affects portfolios in real life

For a wealth advisor, the yield curve shapes several portfolio decisions:

  • When the curve is steep (long yields well above short), bonds at the long end offer attractive returns relative to short-term debt. Investors get paid to extend duration.
  • When the curve is inverted (short yields above long), short-duration bonds and even cash become competitive with long bonds. There’s less reward for extending duration. This is one reason why cash and short-term Treasuries became attractive in 2022–2024.
  • When the curve un-inverts (returns to normal), it often coincides with the early stages of a recession. The Fed has started cutting short rates, so the short end has dropped sharply. Long rates haven’t adjusted as fast. The un-inversion itself is a warning sign.

The big takeaway

The yield curve is one of the few macro indicators with a genuinely impressive track record over multiple cycles. It’s not infallible, the sample size is small (modern post-1950 cycles only), and the lead time is variable. But it’s real signal, not noise. Pay attention to it.

GDP and the business cycle

Gross Domestic Product is the total value of goods and services produced in a country over a period. GDP growth signals the size and direction of the economy. Real GDP (inflation-adjusted) is what economists care most about.

The business cycle is the rhythm of expansion and contraction. Common phases:

The business cycle, simplified
Expansion
Growing GDP, falling unemployment, rising corporate profits, often rising inflation.
→
Peak
Inflation pressures peak; Fed often tightens.
→
Contraction
Slowing growth or recession; rising unemployment; falling profits; central bank often eases.
→
Trough
Lowest point; eventually conditions stabilize and expansion resumes.

Recession — what it actually means

The textbook definition: two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth. In the US, the official call is made by the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) based on a broader set of indicators including employment, real income, industrial production, and retail sales.

Recession matters to investors because it’s usually correlated with bear markets in equities, widening credit spreads, falling corporate earnings, and (eventually) Fed easing. But markets are forward-looking — stocks usually bottom before the recession officially ends.

Unemployment — the human side of macro

The unemployment rate measures the share of the labor force actively looking for work who can’t find it. Low unemployment is good for workers, signals a strong economy, but often comes with wage pressure that fuels inflation. The Fed tracks unemployment as part of its dual mandate.

You should also know:

  • Labor force participation rate: the share of working-age people in the labor force at all. Demographic trends matter here.
  • JOLTS: Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey. The number of open positions per unemployed worker is a tightness indicator.
  • Initial jobless claims: weekly data on people filing for unemployment. A real-time pulse on the labor market.

Monetary policy vs. fiscal policy

Two big levers move the macro economy:

  • Monetary policy is the Fed. Tools: interest rates, balance sheet (QE/QT), forward guidance. Faster to deploy, less politically contentious.
  • Fiscal policy is Congress and the executive branch. Tools: government spending, taxes. Slower to deploy, more politically contentious, but powerful when it moves.

Big macro outcomes are usually the result of the two interacting. The COVID response was a classic example: huge fiscal stimulus combined with aggressive Fed easing, then huge inflation, then aggressive Fed tightening.

The major asset classes and how they typically behave

Asset classFriendsEnemies
EquitiesStrong earnings growth, falling rates, low credit spreads, optimismRising rates, recession, rising credit spreads, regulatory shocks
Investment-grade bondsFalling rates, stable credit, slow economyRising rates, inflation, credit fear
High-yield bondsStrong economy, falling default rates, risk-on sentimentRecession, credit-spread widening, defaults
Cash / T-billsRising rates, market stress, risk-offInflation, prolonged zero-rate environments
Real estateFalling rates, rising rents, supply-constrained marketsRising rates, oversupply, recession, structural shifts (e.g. office)
CommoditiesSupply shocks, inflation, geopolitical tensionRecession, oversupply, strong dollar
GoldFalling real rates, dollar weakness, geopolitical fearRising real rates, risk-on sentiment

Markets and psychology

The market is a voting machine in the short run and a weighing machine in the long run. Investors swing between greed and fear, often overshooting in both directions. Bubbles and crashes are not rare events — they’re normal features.

Useful mental models:

  • Mean reversion: extreme valuations tend to revert toward long-term averages.
  • Risk-on / risk-off: markets cycle between appetite for and aversion to risky assets.
  • The wall of worry: bull markets often climb in the face of constant pessimism. The fear itself prevents the bubble that ends the cycle.
  • Reflexivity (Soros): asset prices influence the fundamentals they’re supposed to reflect — rising stock prices make companies cheaper to finance, which boosts fundamentals, which justifies the higher prices, and so on, until something breaks.

Bond pricing math — from the ground up

You can’t really say you understand bonds until you can do the price math. Let’s build it slowly.

The basic bond cash flow

A bond has three things: face value (usually $1,000), coupon rate (the annual interest rate the bond pays, fixed), and maturity (when you get the face value back). Coupons are usually paid semi-annually.

Example: a 10-year Treasury at $1,000 face value with a 4% coupon. You get $40/year of coupon ($20 every six months) for 10 years, plus $1,000 back at maturity. Total cash received over the life of the bond: $400 of coupons + $1,000 principal = $1,400.

The bond pricing formula

A bond’s price equals the present value of all its future cash flows discounted at the market’s required yield (yield to maturity, or YTM).

Price = Σ [Coupon / (1 + YTM)t] + Face Value / (1 + YTM)n

For our 10-year, 4%-coupon bond at a 4% YTM:

Price = $40/1.04 + $40/1.042 + ... + $40/1.0410 + $1,000/1.0410 = $1,000.

When YTM equals the coupon rate, the bond trades at par ($1,000).

Now suppose market rates rise to 5%. Re-price the bond at 5% YTM:

Price = $40/1.05 + ... + $40/1.0510 + $1,000/1.0510 = approximately $922.78.

The bond’s market price dropped about 8% in response to a 1% rate increase. The contractual cash flows haven’t changed — the price moved to bring the effective yield up to the market’s required 5%.

If rates fall to 3%:

Price = $40/1.03 + ... + $40/1.0310 + $1,000/1.0310 = approximately $1,085.30.

Price up ~8.5%. Same cash flows, different market discount rate.

Why semi-annual vs annual matters

Most US bonds pay coupons semi-annually. So a 4% coupon on $1,000 face value means $20 every six months. The pricing convention treats each 6-month period as one discount period, with the semi-annual yield being YTM/2.

For most purposes this is a minor distinction. But it’s why bond yields are sometimes quoted as a “bond-equivalent yield” (semi-annual basis) rather than an annual compounding rate.

Duration — how much a bond’s price moves when rates change

Duration is a single-number measure of a bond’s interest-rate sensitivity. It tells you, roughly, how much the bond’s price moves for a 1% change in yield.

The intuition

A bond’s duration is the weighted average time until you receive its cash flows. A 30-year zero-coupon bond has a duration of 30 years — all the money comes at the end. A 30-year bond with high coupons has a shorter duration because much of your cash arrives along the way.

Modified duration

Modified duration is the practical version. The approximation:

ΔPrice % ≈ −Modified Duration × ΔYield (in %)

So a bond with modified duration of 7 will drop about 7% in price if yields rise by 1%. And rise about 7% if yields drop by 1%.

Duration rules of thumb

  • Longer maturity → higher duration.
  • Lower coupon → higher duration (less cash arrives early to dampen the long-tail exposure).
  • Higher yield → slightly lower duration (because discounting reduces the present value of later cash flows).
  • Zero-coupon bonds have duration = maturity.

Typical durations

  • 1-year T-bill: duration ~1
  • 5-year Treasury: duration ~4.5
  • 10-year Treasury (current coupon): duration ~7–8
  • 30-year Treasury: duration ~17–20
  • 30-year zero-coupon Treasury: duration ~30

Why duration risk crushed bondholders in 2022

The 30-year Treasury went from about 1.9% in late 2021 to about 4.0% in late 2022 — a 2.1 percentage point increase. At a duration of ~20, the price impact: −20 × 2.1 = about −42%. Long-duration bondholders lost roughly 40% of market value in a year. The worst bond bear market in modern history, driven by duration math working in reverse.

Convexity — the second-order effect

Duration is a linear approximation. The actual price-yield relationship is curved — bond prices fall less when rates rise, and gain more when rates fall, than duration alone predicts. That curvature is called convexity, and it’s a small positive feature for bondholders. For most interview purposes, knowing duration is enough; convexity is the bonus knowledge that signals depth.

Yield curve theories — why the curve has the shape it does

There are three main theories for why the yield curve takes the shapes it does. Understanding them gives you a real grasp of what the curve is telling you.

Expectations Theory

Long-term rates reflect the market’s expectation of average future short-term rates. So a 10-year yield is, roughly, the geometric average of the expected 1-year yields over the next 10 years.

Implication: if the curve is upward-sloping, the market expects short rates to rise. If inverted, the market expects short rates to fall (typically because of a coming recession that forces the Fed to cut).

Liquidity Preference Theory

Investors prefer holding shorter-maturity bonds because they’re less risky (less sensitive to rates, less default risk over time). So to get them to hold longer bonds, you have to pay a premium — a “term premium.” This explains why the curve tends to be upward-sloping even when expectations are neutral.

Market Segmentation Theory

Different investors care about different parts of the curve. Pension funds and insurers need long-duration assets to match their long-duration liabilities, so they bid up long yields. Banks prefer short-duration to match their short-duration deposits. This segmentation creates supply-demand dynamics that aren’t purely about expectations or risk preferences.

In practice

All three are partly true. The yield curve is shaped by a mix of expectations (the dominant story), term premium (especially at longer maturities), and segmentation (especially around technical events like quarter-end or Fed policy actions). The art of analyzing the curve is decomposing the shape into these three factors.

TIPS and breakeven inflation

TIPS (Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities) are Treasury bonds whose principal adjusts upward with inflation (specifically, with CPI). The coupon rate is fixed in real terms, but because principal grows with inflation, the dollar coupon grows too.

Why TIPS exist

To give investors a way to protect against inflation. A regular Treasury locks in a nominal return. If inflation surprises to the upside, the real value of those coupons drops. TIPS fix that.

Breakeven inflation

If a 10-year nominal Treasury yields 4.5% and a 10-year TIPS yields 2.0%, the “breakeven inflation rate” is 2.5%. That’s the inflation rate at which TIPS and nominal Treasuries give the same total return.

If you expect inflation to average above 2.5%, buy TIPS — you’ll do better. If you expect inflation below 2.5%, buy nominal Treasuries.

Breakeven inflation is one of the cleanest market-implied measures of inflation expectations and is watched closely by the Fed and by macro investors.

The bond market hierarchy — Treasuries to high-yield

US Treasuries

Issued by the US government. Lowest yield because lowest credit risk (the US has its own currency and can’t formally default). The benchmark against which everything else is priced.

  • T-bills (up to 1 year): zero-coupon, sold at discount.
  • T-notes (2 to 10 years): coupon-paying.
  • T-bonds (20 to 30 years): coupon-paying, long duration.
  • TIPS: inflation-protected.
  • I-Bonds: retail-focused inflation-protected savings bonds (limited annual purchase).

Agency bonds

Issued by US government-sponsored enterprises (Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, Ginnie Mae) and government agencies. Implicitly or explicitly backed by the US government. Yield a small premium over Treasuries (5–30 bps).

Municipal bonds (munis)

Issued by state and local governments. Most are tax-exempt at the federal level, and often tax-exempt at the state level for in-state buyers. This makes them especially attractive to high-bracket investors.

Tax-equivalent yield: if a muni yields 3% and you’re in the 37% federal bracket, your tax-equivalent yield is 3% / (1 − 0.37) = 4.76%. So a 3% muni beats a 4.5% Treasury for you on an after-tax basis.

Two main flavors: general obligation bonds (backed by the issuer’s taxing authority) and revenue bonds (backed by specific revenue streams like toll roads or stadium fees).

Investment-grade corporate bonds

Issued by companies rated BBB− or higher by S&P / Baa3 or higher by Moody’s. Yield a credit spread over Treasuries (~50 to 200 bps in normal markets).

High-yield corporate bonds (junk bonds)

Below investment grade. Yield much higher spreads — often 300 to 800 bps over Treasuries in normal markets, much wider in stress. Default risk is real; default rates run 2–4% per year through cycles, with spikes in recessions.

Emerging market bonds

Issued by EM governments or corporates, in dollars (hard currency) or local currency. Higher yields, higher volatility, currency risk on local-currency issues.

Credit spreads — what they really mean

Credit spread = yield on a corporate bond − yield on a same-maturity Treasury. The premium investors demand for taking credit risk.

Typical spread ranges (for 10-year corporates):

  • AAA-rated: 30–80 bps
  • AA: 50–100 bps
  • A: 70–130 bps
  • BBB: 120–200 bps
  • BB (high yield): 300–500 bps
  • B (high yield): 500–800 bps
  • CCC (deep distressed): 800+ bps

Spreads widen in periods of stress (investors demand more compensation). They tighten in calm markets (investors accept less compensation). Tracking credit spreads is one of the cleanest real-time gauges of financial stress.

The federal funds rate and its cousins

You’ve seen the federal funds rate. There are several closely-related short-term rates that all live near it:

Federal funds rate

The overnight rate at which US banks lend reserves to each other. Set by the Fed’s target range. As of recent years, the effective rate has hovered close to the target.

SOFR (Secured Overnight Financing Rate)

The successor to LIBOR. SOFR is based on actual transactions in the overnight repo market, where Treasuries are exchanged for cash overnight. It’s the new benchmark for floating-rate loans, derivatives, and lots of corporate debt.

SOFR was developed because LIBOR was based on bank survey responses that were manipulable (and were manipulated, leading to a major scandal). SOFR uses real transaction data.

Prime rate

The rate US banks charge their best corporate customers for short-term loans. Typically 3 percentage points above the federal funds rate target. Many consumer products (variable-rate credit cards, some mortgages, home equity lines) are priced as prime + X%.

Discount rate

The rate at which the Fed itself lends to banks at the discount window. Usually slightly above the federal funds target. Used in emergencies and for short-term liquidity needs.

Interest on reserves (IOER)

What the Fed pays banks on the reserves they hold at the Fed. Used as a tool to set the floor of the federal funds rate.

The money market — the plumbing of short-term cash

Beyond Treasuries, there’s an enormous “money market” of short-term instruments that companies and institutions use to park cash for a few days, weeks, or months.

  • T-bills: short-term US government debt (4-week, 13-week, 26-week, 52-week maturities). Risk-free.
  • Commercial paper: short-term corporate debt, usually issued by very large investment-grade companies. Matures in 1 day to 270 days.
  • Repos (repurchase agreements): short-term collateralized loans, where one party sells Treasuries to another with an agreement to buy them back the next day at a slightly higher price.
  • CDs (certificates of deposit): short-term bank deposits with fixed maturities. FDIC-insured up to $250K per depositor.
  • Money market funds: mutual funds that invest in T-bills, commercial paper, and other short-term instruments. They typically maintain a $1.00 NAV (though it can “break the buck” in extreme stress, as happened in 2008).

Why the money market matters

It’s where corporate treasurers, banks, and asset managers manage short-term cash. Disruptions in the money market — like the 2008 Reserve Primary Fund breaking the buck, or the September 2019 repo-rate spike — signal systemic stress that ripples through the rest of the financial system.

Currencies and FX — the deeper version

Why exchange rates move

Currency values are driven by a mix of:

  • Interest-rate differentials (capital flows toward higher real yields).
  • Inflation differentials (purchasing-power parity over the long run).
  • Growth differentials.
  • Trade and current-account balances.
  • Risk sentiment (the dollar, Swiss franc, and yen all rally in stress).
  • Central-bank intervention (rare for major currencies, common for some EM).

Interest-rate parity

A core FX concept: in equilibrium, the forward exchange rate should reflect the interest-rate differential between two currencies. If US rates are higher than Euro rates by 2%, the Euro should trade at a forward premium over the dollar by approximately 2% — otherwise an arbitrage exists.

The dollar specifically

The dollar is the world’s reserve currency. About 60% of global FX reserves are held in dollars. Most international trade is invoiced in dollars. Most cross-border debt is dollar-denominated.

The “dollar smile”: the dollar tends to strengthen in two regimes — (1) US growth outperformance with high real yields, attracting capital, and (2) global stress with safe-haven flows. It tends to weaken in moderate global expansions when investors rotate into riskier non-dollar assets.

EM currency risk

Emerging-market borrowers often take on dollar-denominated debt because dollar rates are typically lower than their local rates. But when the dollar strengthens, the local-currency value of that debt rises, sometimes catastrophically. This was the engine of the 1997 Asian crisis, the 2018 EM selloff, and the 2022 Turkey/Argentina/Egypt currency crises.

FX hedging for WM portfolios

When a US client holds international assets — a European stock or a Japanese bond — their total return includes both the local return and the currency move. A US investor in Japanese equities who got 10% in yen but the yen fell 5% against the dollar only earned ~5% in dollar terms.

Some funds “hedge” their currency exposure (using forwards or futures). Hedged funds remove the currency volatility but pay a hedging cost. For long-term diversified portfolios, the question of whether to hedge international equity is debated — over long periods, currency moves tend to wash out.

Equity returns decomposition — where the money actually comes from

The return on a stock over a period can be decomposed into three pieces:

Total Return = Dividend Yield + EPS Growth + Multiple Change

Dividend yield

The cash dividend as a percentage of price. Mature companies (consumer staples, utilities, financials) often pay 3–5% dividend yields. Growth companies pay 0–2%.

EPS growth

The growth in earnings per share over the period. This is the “fundamental” piece of return — the business actually getting bigger and more profitable.

Multiple expansion (or contraction)

The change in the P/E multiple. If a stock starts the year at 15x P/E and ends at 18x P/E, that 20% multiple expansion adds 20% to the return on top of any earnings growth.

The decomposition matters because the three pieces have very different durability. Earnings growth and dividends compound. Multiple changes are mostly mean-reverting and can’t be the source of returns forever.

The long-run breakdown

Over a 100-year horizon, US stock returns have been roughly:

  • Dividend yield: ~4% (much higher historically; lower now because of buybacks replacing dividends)
  • Real EPS growth: ~2%
  • Inflation: ~3%
  • Total nominal: ~9% (close to the often-cited 10% number)
  • Multiple change averaged near zero over the very long run

VIX and market sentiment indicators

The VIX

The Cboe Volatility Index. Measures the market’s expectation of S&P 500 volatility over the next 30 days, derived from options prices. Called the “fear gauge.”

  • VIX under 15: calm market.
  • VIX 15–25: normal range.
  • VIX 25–35: elevated stress.
  • VIX 35–50: serious crisis.
  • VIX 50+: extreme fear (March 2020 hit ~80, October 2008 hit ~80).

Put-call ratio

The ratio of put options (bearish bets) to call options (bullish bets). High put-call ratios signal bearish positioning; low ratios signal bullish positioning. Often viewed as a contrarian indicator at extremes.

AAII sentiment survey

The American Association of Individual Investors’ weekly survey of bullish vs bearish sentiment. Watched as a contrarian indicator — extreme bullishness often precedes corrections, extreme bearishness often precedes rallies.

Margin debt

Total margin loans against brokerage accounts. Rising margin debt = investors leveraging up = late-cycle indicator. Falling margin debt during a drawdown = forced deleveraging.

The leading economic indicators — what they tell you

The Conference Board publishes a Leading Economic Index (LEI) that combines several indicators believed to lead the broader economy. Components include:

  • Average weekly hours in manufacturing
  • Initial jobless claims
  • Manufacturers’ new orders for consumer goods
  • ISM new orders index
  • New private housing permits
  • S&P 500 stock index
  • Leading credit index
  • Interest-rate spread (10y minus fed funds)
  • Average consumer expectations for business conditions

When the LEI declines for several months consecutively, it’s typically a signal of an oncoming slowdown or recession. The LEI started declining in 2022 and stayed negative through most of 2023–2024, generating endless debate about whether the historical recession-signal pattern would hold.

NBER recession definition — the actual criteria

The textbook definition of a recession is “two consecutive quarters of negative real GDP growth.” The actual US definition, made by the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), is more nuanced.

NBER defines a recession as “a significant decline in economic activity that is spread across the economy and that lasts more than a few months.” They look at:

  • Real personal income less transfers
  • Nonfarm payroll employment
  • Real personal consumption expenditures
  • Wholesale-retail sales adjusted for price changes
  • Household survey of employment
  • Industrial production

NBER calls recessions after the fact (sometimes a year or more later). The 2020 COVID recession lasted just 2 months — the shortest on record — but it was severe enough that NBER still called it. Two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth happened in 2022 but NBER did not call a recession because employment and other indicators stayed strong.

Famous market events — the lessons

1929 crash and Great Depression

Stock market peaked in September 1929. By 1932, the Dow had lost 89%. The Federal Reserve’s mistakes (tight monetary policy in the face of falling money supply) deepened the depression. Lesson: monetary policy errors can turn a market crash into a decade-long economic catastrophe.

1987 Black Monday

October 19, 1987 — S&P down 20.5% in a single day. Largely driven by program trading and portfolio insurance feedback loops. Recovered within months. Lesson: structural / mechanical selling can crash markets even without fundamental deterioration. Led to circuit breakers and other market-stability mechanisms.

1997 Asian Crisis

EM currencies (Thailand, Indonesia, South Korea, Malaysia) collapsed against the dollar after years of pegged exchange rates and dollar-denominated debt. IMF bailouts followed. Lesson: dollar-denominated EM debt is a major source of fragility when the dollar strengthens.

2000 Dot-com bust

NASDAQ peaked in March 2000 at 5,048. Fell 78% over the next 2.5 years. Took 15 years to fully recover. Lesson: extreme valuations and narrative-driven momentum eventually mean-revert.

2008 Global Financial Crisis

Housing bubble + sub-prime mortgage securitization + bank balance-sheet leverage + interconnected derivatives = systemic banking crisis. Lehman’s bankruptcy on September 15, 2008 triggered a cascade. Fed and Treasury intervened massively (TARP, ZIRP, QE). S&P bottomed in March 2009, down 57% from peak. Lesson: financial leverage + opaque interconnections = systemic risk.

2010 Flash Crash

May 6, 2010 — the S&P fell ~9% intraday and recovered most of it within 20 minutes. Driven by an aggressive algorithmic sell order interacting with thin liquidity. Lesson: market structure matters; HFT-era liquidity can vanish.

2011 European sovereign debt crisis

Greece, then Italy, Spain, Portugal under pressure as bond yields spiked. ECB’s Mario Draghi delivered the famous “whatever it takes” speech in July 2012 that stabilized the situation. Lesson: central bank backstops can break panics if the political will exists.

2020 COVID crash

S&P fell 34% from February 19 to March 23, 2020 — the fastest bear market in history. Fed cut rates to zero, restarted QE, opened emergency facilities. Markets recovered to new highs by August. Lesson: aggressive policy response can turn a crisis into a rapid recovery, but at the cost of inflating asset prices everywhere.

2022 inflation and rate-hike cycle

Inflation hit 40-year highs in 2022. Fed raised rates from 0% to 5.25% in just 18 months — the fastest tightening cycle in decades. 60/40 portfolios had their worst year in modern history because stocks and bonds both fell. Lesson: in inflation regimes, the historical stock-bond correlation can flip from negative to positive, destroying the diversification benefit of bonds in a balanced portfolio.

2023 banking mini-crisis

Silicon Valley Bank, Signature, First Republic collapsed in March 2023, partly due to unrealized losses on long-duration bond portfolios from the rate-hike cycle, plus rapid deposit flight enabled by digital banking and social media. Fed created the Bank Term Funding Program to backstop the rest of the regional bank system. Lesson: in a digital-banking era, deposit flight happens faster than historical models assume.

Putting it together — the elite macro coffee-chat answer

When someone asks you, “what do you think is happening in markets right now?”, here’s the framework. Don’t deliver it as a script — deliver it as someone who’s actually thought about the question.

  1. One headline driver. Pick the dominant story — rates, inflation, earnings, a geopolitical situation, an AI capex cycle. One.
  2. Where you see the evidence. Tie it to something concrete: the 10-year yield, the latest CPI print, the dollar index, a specific stock or sector.
  3. What it implies for portfolios. The link to actual asset behavior — what’s outperforming, what’s lagging, where you’d be cautious.
  4. The uncertainty you respect. Show humility about what could change your view. This is the move that separates strong from elite.

Currencies and the dollar — the operating system of global finance

Exchange rates are simply the price of one currency in another. Why they matter:

  • A weaker domestic currency boosts exports (the country’s goods become cheaper abroad) and dampens imports.
  • A stronger domestic currency does the opposite — great for purchasing power, bad for exporters.
  • For an investor, foreign-currency returns get translated back to home currency. A 10% return in Japanese yen plus a 5% yen depreciation against the dollar is only a 4.5% return for a US investor.

Currency moves are driven by:

  • Interest-rate differentials: capital flows toward higher real yields.
  • Inflation differentials: high-inflation currencies tend to depreciate over the long run (purchasing power parity).
  • Growth differentials: stronger growth attracts capital.
  • Risk sentiment: the dollar, Swiss franc, and yen historically strengthen when investors get scared (“flight to safety”).
  • Trade balances: persistent deficits or surpluses pressure currencies over time.

Why the dollar is special

The US dollar is the world’s reserve currency — roughly 60% of global foreign exchange reserves, the dominant currency in international trade and global debt issuance, and the unit of account for most commodities. Two consequences for investors:

  • The “dollar smile”: the dollar strengthens both in periods of US economic outperformance (high real yields) and in periods of global stress (safe haven). It tends to weaken in moderate global expansions.
  • Dollar-denominated debt is a global stress vector: emerging markets that borrowed in dollars during easy-money cycles get squeezed when the dollar strengthens and refinancing becomes harder. This dynamic has triggered multiple EM crises (1997 Asian crisis, 2018 EM selloff).

The credit cycle — the rhythm under everything

Underlying the business cycle is a credit cycle — an oscillation between periods of easy credit (rates low, spreads tight, lending standards loose) and tight credit (rates high, spreads wide, lending standards strict). Howard Marks has written extensively on this; it’s a more durable framework than calendar-based business-cycle thinking.

The pattern:

  1. After a downturn, central banks ease and credit spreads narrow.
  2. Risk appetite returns. Investors stretch for yield. Underwriting standards loosen.
  3. Marginal borrowers get funded. Mispricings accumulate.
  4. An exogenous shock or central-bank tightening reverses sentiment.
  5. Credit availability collapses, defaults rise, distressed opportunities emerge.
  6. The cycle resets.

This is why credit spreads, lending standards (the Fed’s Senior Loan Officer Survey), and high-yield default rates are watched obsessively by macro investors. They’re often leading indicators of trouble that equity markets are slow to price.

Commodities — how they fit in a portfolio

Commodities are physical goods — oil, gas, gold, copper, wheat, cattle. Investors usually access them through futures contracts or futures-based ETFs.

Three reasons commodities show up in HNW portfolios:

  • Inflation hedge: in inflationary regimes, commodity prices tend to rise, providing real-return protection.
  • Diversification: commodity returns are weakly correlated with stocks and bonds over long periods.
  • Geopolitical hedge: oil and gold often spike on geopolitical shocks.

The challenge: holding commodities through futures involves roll yield — the cost or benefit of selling expiring futures and buying longer-dated ones. In contango (longer-dated higher than spot), the roll yield is negative; in backwardation (longer-dated lower than spot), it’s positive. This is why a long-only commodity index can underperform spot prices over time.

Gold, specifically

Gold doesn’t earn income; you can’t value it by discounting cash flows. So why do investors own it?

  • As a store of value when real yields are low or negative.
  • As a hedge against currency debasement or political tail risk.
  • As a portfolio diversifier with historically low correlation to stocks.

Gold underperforms when real yields rise sharply (because the opportunity cost of holding it goes up) and outperforms in stress regimes and weak-dollar environments. Most modern wealth advisors allocate 2–5% of multi-asset portfolios to gold or precious metals.

REITs — the deep version

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) are publicly traded companies that own and operate income-producing real estate. They’re a common holding in HNW portfolios for income and diversification. Let’s build the picture.

The REIT structure

To qualify as a REIT under the IRS code, a company must:

  • Invest at least 75% of total assets in real estate or related assets.
  • Derive at least 75% of gross income from real estate (rents, mortgage interest, real estate sales).
  • Pay out at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders as dividends.
  • Be widely held (no five investors can own more than 50% of shares).

In exchange for these rules, REITs are exempt from corporate income tax. This avoids the double-taxation problem — income flows from properties to investors with only one layer of tax (at the investor level).

Why REITs pay big dividends

Because they must distribute 90%+ of taxable income, REITs have high dividend yields by design. Typical equity REIT yields run 3–6%. This is the main reason income-oriented investors hold REITs.

The major REIT sectors

  • Residential: apartment buildings, single-family rentals. Examples: AvalonBay, Equity Residential, Invitation Homes.
  • Industrial: warehouses and logistics. Massive secular tailwind from e-commerce. Examples: Prologis, Duke Realty.
  • Retail: shopping malls, strip centers. Mixed prospects from e-commerce headwinds; some niches (open-air centers, grocery-anchored) hold up well. Examples: Simon Property Group, Realty Income.
  • Office: traditional office buildings. Under structural pressure from work-from-home. Examples: Boston Properties, SL Green.
  • Healthcare: hospitals, medical office buildings, senior housing, life sciences labs. Examples: Welltower, Ventas, Alexandria Real Estate (lab space).
  • Data centers: server farms. Massive AI-driven demand. Examples: Equinix, Digital Realty.
  • Cell towers: communications infrastructure. Examples: American Tower, Crown Castle.
  • Self-storage: storage facilities. Surprisingly attractive economics. Examples: Public Storage, Extra Space.
  • Specialty: timber, prisons, casinos, billboards, farmland.
  • Mortgage REITs (mREITs): invest in mortgages and mortgage-backed securities rather than physical real estate. Higher yields but more rate-sensitive. Examples: Annaly Capital, AGNC.

How to value REITs

Standard equity multiples don’t work for REITs because GAAP depreciation overstates the “decline” in real estate values. Properties typically appreciate, not depreciate.

Instead, use:

  • FFO (Funds From Operations): net income + depreciation + amortization − gains on property sales. The REIT industry’s preferred earnings measure.
  • AFFO (Adjusted FFO): FFO minus recurring capital expenditures. A closer proxy for distributable cash flow.
  • P/FFO multiple: high-quality REITs trade 15–25x, lower-quality 8–14x.
  • NAV (Net Asset Value): an estimate of the underlying real-estate value per share. REITs sometimes trade at a discount or premium to NAV.

REITs and interest rates

REITs are sensitive to interest rates for two reasons. First, their dividend yields compete with bond yields — rising rates make REITs relatively less attractive. Second, real-estate valuations themselves are sensitive to cap rates (basically a real-estate yield), which move with broader interest rates.

So in rising-rate environments, REITs tend to underperform. In falling-rate environments, they tend to outperform. The 2022 rate-hike cycle was painful for most REITs.

REIT taxation for individual investors

REIT dividends are mostly taxed as ordinary income, not at the lower qualified dividend rate. So REITs are often best held in tax-deferred accounts (IRA, 401(k)) rather than taxable accounts.

The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act created a 20% deduction (Section 199A) on REIT dividends, which mitigated this somewhat. Still, most advisors place REIT exposure in tax-advantaged accounts when possible.

Municipal bonds — the WM client favorite

For high-bracket clients in high-tax states, muni bonds can offer attractive after-tax yields. Let’s really build this out.

What munis are

Bonds issued by state and local governments and their agencies to fund public projects (roads, schools, hospitals, infrastructure).

The tax exemption

Interest on most munis is exempt from federal income tax. If the muni was issued by your state (or municipality), interest is often also exempt from state income tax. This double-exemption makes in-state munis especially attractive to high earners in high-tax states (California, New York, New Jersey, Hawaii).

Tax-equivalent yield — the math

To compare a muni yield to a Treasury yield, convert it to tax-equivalent terms:

Tax-equivalent yield = Muni yield / (1 − marginal tax rate)

Worked example

A 10-year California muni yields 3.2%. The investor is a California resident in the 37% federal bracket plus 13.3% state bracket, with the 3.8% NIIT applicable. Effective marginal tax rate on Treasury interest: 37 + 13.3 + 3.8 = 54.1%. (Note: state tax is technically deductible at the federal level, but with SALT cap, much of it isn’t. Assume not deductible for simplicity.)

Tax-equivalent yield of the muni: 3.2% / (1 − 0.541) = 3.2% / 0.459 = 6.97%.

So a 3.2% in-state California muni gives the same after-tax return as a 7% Treasury for this investor. With current 10-year Treasuries at ~4.5%, the muni is much better on an after-tax basis.

Two main flavors of muni

  • General Obligation (GO) bonds: backed by the taxing authority of the issuer. Generally considered safer because the issuer can raise taxes to service the debt.
  • Revenue bonds: backed by specific revenue streams (toll-road tolls, water-system fees, stadium revenues). Riskier than GO if the revenue stream falters.

Credit quality and ratings

Most munis are investment-grade (AAA to BBB−). Some are speculative. Default rates are historically low for investment-grade munis, even in stressed periods. The famous defaults (Detroit 2013, Puerto Rico 2015–) are exceptions and were idiosyncratic.

When advisors recommend munis

  • For taxable accounts of high-bracket investors (above ~32% marginal).
  • In-state munis for residents of high-tax states.
  • For retirees needing tax-efficient income.
  • As a complement to taxable corporate bond exposure to diversify the fixed-income sleeve.

When NOT to use munis

  • Inside IRAs or 401(k)s — you don’t need the tax exemption, so you’re effectively paying for it twice.
  • For investors in low tax brackets — the tax-equivalent yield doesn’t beat Treasuries.
  • If credit research isn’t available — the muni universe is huge (over 50,000 issuers, vastly more issues), and individual credit selection matters.

Volatility and standard deviation — what risk actually means in finance

You’ve seen “standard deviation” come up. Let me make it really concrete.

The basic idea

Standard deviation measures how spread out a set of numbers is around their average. In finance, we use it to measure how much returns fluctuate.

Worked example

A stock had annual returns of: +20%, −10%, +15%, +5%, −5%. Average return: 5%.

Deviations from average: +15, −15, +10, 0, −10. Squared deviations: 225, 225, 100, 0, 100. Sum: 650. Divide by N−1 = 4: 162.5. Standard deviation = √162.5 = 12.7%.

So this stock has “12.7% annual standard deviation.” That’s the typical year-to-year fluctuation around the 5% average.

Why we care

Standard deviation matters for three reasons:

  • It scales with risk in normal-distribution math. About 68% of returns fall within one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two, 99.7% within three (under the assumption of normal distribution, which is approximately true for diversified portfolios).
  • It feeds into Sharpe ratio, the most-used risk-adjusted metric.
  • It feeds into portfolio optimization in Modern Portfolio Theory.

Typical equity volatility

  • S&P 500 long-term: ~15% annual standard deviation.
  • Individual large-cap stocks: 25–40%.
  • Small caps and emerging markets: 25–35%.
  • Volatile growth stocks (Tesla, etc.): 50%+ in some periods.
  • Bitcoin and crypto: 60–100%+ in many periods.
  • Investment-grade bonds: 4–8%.
  • Cash / T-bills: under 1%.

The limits of standard deviation as risk

Standard deviation is the most-used measure of risk but it has well-known limitations:

  • It treats upside volatility the same as downside — but investors don’t.
  • It assumes returns are normally distributed — but financial returns have “fat tails” (extreme events happen more than the normal distribution predicts).
  • It can be gamed by strategies that suppress volatility most of the time at the cost of occasional catastrophic losses (think LTCM, certain hedge funds before blowing up).

That’s why sophisticated investors complement standard deviation with downside-focused metrics (Sortino ratio, max drawdown, value-at-risk) and pay close attention to scenarios the math can’t easily capture.

Real estate as an asset class

Beyond personal property, real estate shows up in client portfolios through:

  • Public REITs: liquid, daily-traded, behave somewhat like equities with a yield tilt and rate sensitivity. Specialty REIT categories: office, industrial, residential, retail, healthcare, hotels, data centers, cell towers, self-storage, timber.
  • Private real estate funds: longer-locked, more direct exposure to physical properties, often categorized as core, core-plus, value-add, or opportunistic by risk profile.
  • Direct property ownership: highest control, lowest liquidity, real management burden.

Real estate’s major drivers: interest rates (the dominant valuation input for income-producing property), rental growth, occupancy, supply-demand in the specific submarket, and capital availability for development.

Emerging markets — the higher-volatility growth engine

Emerging markets (EM) is a broad category — everything from China and India to Brazil, Mexico, South Africa, Indonesia. Why they’re part of a global portfolio:

  • Higher long-term growth potential than developed economies.
  • Lower starting valuations historically.
  • Diversification away from US-centric exposure.

The risks:

  • Currency volatility (often the biggest variable in returns).
  • Political and governance risk.
  • Less mature capital markets.
  • Commodity dependence in many EM economies.

For US-based HNW investors, EM exposure typically runs 5–15% of equity, scaled by risk tolerance. The post-2010 era was tough for EM relative to US large-cap; whether the next decade reverses that has become a major asset-allocation debate.

The major economic indicators advisors actually watch

You won’t track all of these as a freshman. You should recognize them.

  • Non-farm payrolls: monthly US job report. The single most-watched data print of the month.
  • Unemployment rate: released with payrolls.
  • CPI (Consumer Price Index): monthly inflation. Headline and core (excluding food and energy).
  • PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures price index): the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge. Slightly different weighting than CPI.
  • Retail sales: monthly consumer-spending pulse.
  • Industrial production: factory output.
  • ISM Manufacturing PMI: a survey of purchasing managers. Above 50 = expansion; below = contraction.
  • ISM Services PMI: same idea for the services side, which is most of the US economy.
  • Housing starts and existing home sales: pulse on the residential market, very rate-sensitive.
  • Consumer confidence (Conference Board) and Consumer Sentiment (UMich): surveys of how households feel about the economy.
  • Initial jobless claims: weekly. Real-time labor-market signal.
  • FOMC meeting minutes and dot plot: how the Fed thinks about rates.
  • JOLTS: job openings, quits, hires — labor-market texture.
  • Trade balance, current account: international flow data.

Famous market events every aspiring advisor should know

Clients ask about these. So do interviewers. A short tour:

  • 1929 crash and the Great Depression. The reference event for “tail risk.”
  • 1973–74 bear market. Oil shock plus stagflation. The era that broke the post-war “buy and hold” consensus for a while.
  • 1987 crash (Black Monday). S&P down ~22% in a single day. Sparked the introduction of circuit breakers and a lasting fear of program trading.
  • 2000 dot-com bust. The Nasdaq fell 78% from peak to trough over two-plus years. Teaches the danger of valuation extremes and concentration.
  • 2008 Global Financial Crisis. Housing bubble, Lehman bankruptcy, near-collapse of the global banking system, massive bailouts, the zero-rate era. The defining event of modern macro.
  • 2010 Flash Crash. Algorithmic trading drove the S&P down nearly 10% intraday before recovering.
  • 2011 European sovereign debt crisis. Greece, then peripheral Europe; rescued by the ECB’s “whatever it takes” pivot.
  • 2015–16 China shock and EM selloff. The yuan devaluation rippled through global risk assets.
  • 2018 December selloff. The Fed’s 2018 hiking cycle and trade tensions hit equities hard.
  • 2020 COVID crash and recovery. Fastest bear market on record; fastest recovery on record. Massive monetary and fiscal response.
  • 2022 inflation shock and rate-hike cycle. The fastest tightening in decades; bond losses unprecedented in a 60/40 portfolio; the era of free money ended.

Each of these has a tidy lesson and a messier lesson. The tidy lesson is “diversification works.” The messier lesson is “correlations spike to one in panics.” Mature investors hold both lessons at once.

Market cap structure — how the whole pie is divided

Quick orienting numbers (rough, current era):

  • Global equity market cap: roughly $100T.
  • US share of that: roughly 60% — far above the US share of global GDP, reflecting the depth and quality of US capital markets.
  • Global bond market: roughly $130T — bigger than equities.
  • Global private market AUM: roughly $13T in alternatives.
  • Top 10 US companies often account for 30%+ of S&P 500 market cap — an unusual concentration in modern history.

Understanding these proportions calibrates conversation. When an advisor says “we’re overweight US,” you should understand that 60%+ is roughly the index-neutral weight, not a tilt.

The interview question bank for macro & markets

What’s happening in markets right now?
Elite answer

Use the framework above. Pick one specific driver, tie to evidence, walk the implication, name your uncertainty.

What is the Fed doing, and why?
Elite answer

“The Fed’s policy stance reflects how the dual mandate is balancing in current conditions. When inflation is above target and the labor market is tight, the Fed tightens — raising rates and reducing the balance sheet — to slow demand. When inflation is contained and unemployment is rising, the Fed eases. Right now, the dynamic to watch is [whatever the current dynamic is]. What I’d note is that monetary policy works with long and variable lags, often 12 to 18 months, so the effects of recent tightening or easing show up gradually rather than all at once.”

What is the yield curve telling us?
Elite answer

“The shape of the yield curve embeds market expectations about future short rates and risk. An inverted curve — short rates above long rates — says investors expect future rate cuts, usually because of an expected economic slowdown. It’s been a historically reliable recession indicator but with a variable lead time. A steepening curve from inversion is often associated with the early stages of recession or recovery. As an advisor I’d note that the yield curve isn’t infallible — it’s a signal, not a forecast — but it’s one of the more durable macro indicators we have.”

What is inflation, and why does the Fed target 2%?
Elite answer

“Inflation is the rate at which the general level of prices rises. The Fed targets roughly 2% because that level is high enough to give policymakers room to cut rates in a downturn without hitting the zero lower bound, but low enough that price stability and long-term planning are preserved. Above 2%, the Fed will typically tighten until inflation cools; below 2% for long stretches, the Fed will ease.”

If rates rise sharply, what would you expect in client portfolios?
Elite answer

“Three things, broadly. Long-duration bonds would take a price hit, with the magnitude depending on duration. Long-duration equities — high-growth tech, especially — would see multiple compression as their future cash flows get discounted more heavily. Cash and short-duration fixed income would suddenly look more attractive, drawing flows. In a client portfolio I’d expect to see a rotation from growth toward value, and a real reason to revisit fixed-income allocations now that yields are meaningful again.”

What’s the difference between nominal and real returns?
Elite answer

“Nominal returns are what you actually earn in dollar terms. Real returns are inflation-adjusted — what you earn in purchasing power. If you make 7% on an investment in a year where inflation runs 3%, your real return is about 4%. Real returns are what matter for long-term wealth, especially for retirees whose expenses rise with inflation.”

How would you describe risk-on vs. risk-off?
Elite answer

“Risk-on is a market environment where investors are willing to bid up riskier assets — equities, high-yield credit, emerging-market debt — in expectation of higher returns. Risk-off is the opposite: investors flee toward perceived safe havens — Treasuries, dollar, sometimes gold — even at lower yields. The cycle between the two states is partly fundamental and partly psychological.”

Why do investors care about credit spreads?
Elite answer

“Credit spreads are the extra yield corporate bonds offer over Treasuries to compensate for credit risk. They’re one of the cleanest real-time measures of perceived stress in the corporate sector. Widening spreads usually signal rising concern about defaults or slower growth; tightening spreads usually signal optimism. Spreads often move ahead of stock prices in turning points.”

What is quantitative easing?
Elite answer

“Quantitative easing is when a central bank buys large amounts of bonds — typically government bonds and sometimes mortgage-backed securities — using newly created reserves, with the goal of pushing down long-term interest rates and injecting liquidity into the financial system. It’s a tool the Fed has used when conventional rate cuts hit the zero lower bound — notably after 2008 and during COVID. The opposite, quantitative tightening, is when the Fed lets those bonds roll off the balance sheet, draining liquidity.”

Why does the dollar matter for global markets?
Elite answer

“The dollar is the world’s reserve currency — most cross-border trade and a huge share of global debt is denominated in dollars. A stronger dollar makes US exports less competitive, hurts emerging-market borrowers who owe in dollars, and tends to pressure commodities priced in dollars. A weaker dollar does the opposite. For US investors, dollar strength reduces the returns on foreign-currency assets when translated back to dollars.”

What’s the relationship between bond yields and stock valuations?
Elite answer

“Bond yields are essentially the discount rate for risk-free cash flows. As yields rise, the present value of any future cash flow falls. Since stocks are claims on future cash flows, higher yields generally compress equity multiples — especially for high-growth companies whose value is heavily back-loaded. The reverse is true when yields fall. That’s why so much of the equity market obsesses about the 10-year Treasury yield: it’s the gravitational center of valuation.”

What is a soft landing?
Elite answer

“A soft landing is when a central bank successfully cools inflation without tipping the economy into a meaningful recession. Historically rare — most hiking cycles end in some form of slowdown. The 1994–1995 cycle is the classic soft-landing example. Markets debate constantly whether the current cycle is heading for one.”

How would you talk a client out of a panic-driven decision?
Elite answer

Already covered earlier, but worth repeating because it’ll come up. “I listen first. I anchor in the plan. I walk through historical drawdowns and recoveries. I slow the decision down — never buy or sell on the worst day of fear. I’d also remind them that the decisions that look brave in the moment of panic are usually the ones investors regret most.”

When you can explain the yield curve, inflation, and the Fed’s tools in plain English and tie them to portfolio implications, you’re ready.
Chapter 09 — The Question Bank

The complete question bank.

A library of interview questions across every domain that will come up for a freshman targeting wealth management. Each question is grouped by category; expand any one to see beginner, strong, and elite answers, the interviewer’s real intent, and the common traps. The questions in earlier chapters appear here too — the chapters explain, this bank drills.

Fit & motivation

Why are you interviewing with us today?
Elite answer

“Three reasons. One: the team. Everything I’ve read about [advisor / branch] suggests a practice that takes apprenticeship seriously and trains its young people well. Two: the platform. [Specific firm asset]. Three: the trajectory of the business model — I want to learn in a place that’s leaning into integrated planning rather than just being a brokerage. I’d rather be in a place where I have to stretch to keep up than a place I’d coast through.”

What do you know about the firm?
Elite answer

You should be able to talk about: the firm’s overall size and reputation, the wealth-management division specifically, recent leadership comments, the firm’s public investment outlook, a recent news headline. For Morgan Stanley, mention Wealth Management as a strategic pillar post-Smith Barney acquisition and post-E*TRADE. For J.P. Morgan, mention Chase Private Client and the integration with the bank. For Merrill, mention the Bank of America integration. For UBS, mention the global private bank franchise and the Credit Suisse acquisition’s legacy. For RBC, mention the partnership culture. For Raymond James, mention the independent-friendly model. Don’t pretend you know everything — one or two genuine substantive references beat a Wikipedia recitation.

Why now — why a freshman internship?
Elite answer

“Because the earlier I start learning the actual business, the better the version of me will be by junior year. I’m not interested in spending two years building a resume of finance clubs and then arriving at junior recruiting having never sat inside a real practice. I’d rather be useful in a small way, very early, and then compound from there.”

Why finance and not consulting / tech / something else?
Elite answer

“I considered both. What pulls me to finance is the directness of the feedback loop — the work has measurable consequences for real people and real money, often within a defined time frame. Consulting felt more abstract; tech felt great for the product but slower for the kind of judgment I’m drawn to. Finance — especially the parts that involve advising humans on long-term decisions — is where I want to spend the next ten years.”

Why our wealth management arm specifically vs. the rest of the firm?
Elite answer

“Because the apprenticeship matters more to me than the prestige line on the resume. Wealth management gives you immediate proximity to clients, real decisions about real money, and a chance to learn the integration of investments, tax, and planning that I find the most interesting part of finance. The trading floor and the M&A floor are exciting, but I think they’re a better fit as a sophomore or junior, after I’ve already learned what end-clients actually want.”

What separates a good wealth advisor from a great one?
Elite answer

“The technical work is table stakes; everyone’s portfolio is reasonably similar. What separates a great advisor is two things: the discipline to keep clients invested through fear, and the imagination to spot planning opportunities the client didn’t know to ask about. The first protects the downside; the second creates the upside. Both are mostly human skills, not analytical ones.”

How would you describe your work style?
Elite answer

“Methodical and slightly obsessive. I tend to over-prepare for things I’m unsure about and then under-emphasize the prep in conversation so it doesn’t feel rehearsed. I’m comfortable being told I’m wrong — I’d rather be corrected on day one than make the same mistake on day thirty. I work better with clear ownership than with diffuse responsibility, and I default to written follow-ups so people don’t have to remember verbal commitments.”

Are you applying to other firms?
Elite answer

Be honest, gracious, and confident. “Yes — I’m talking to a few other wealth-management teams in the area. I’ve been deliberate about who I’ve approached, and you’re at the top of the list for [specific reason]. I’d rather be straightforward about that than pretend otherwise.” Interviewers know you’re applying elsewhere. Pretending otherwise is the only wrong move.

Behavioral & story-based (additional)

Tell me about a time you had to deliver bad news.
Interviewer’s real intent
